perfil.resumoExecutivo: Roberto Pires é um empresário paulistano de 45 anos, perfil A2, que chegou à consultoria com um objetivo claro e um prazo real: uma viagem para Barcelona e Paris prevista para agosto. Comunicativo e animado (tom observado pelo gestor como 'constante e animado'), Roberto tem a favor uma pronúncia confortável e vocabulário básico funcional — mas trava na hora de montar frases completas, recorrendo ao português como muleta quando a pressão comunicativa aumenta. Seu maior desafio não é o inglês em si: é a distância entre entender e conseguir falar no momento certo. A trilha de 48 aulas foi desenhada sob medida para esse perfil — Travel English aplicado, com ganhos rápidos e tangíveis em cenários previsíveis, devolvendo a Roberto a autonomia que ele precisa para circular pela Europa com confiança.

necessidadesPedagogicas.adaptacaoNivel: A2 (Pré-Intermediário): tradução PT-BR obrigatória e seletiva para suporte cognitivo. Introduzir 5 a 7 palavras novas por aula, com foco em chunks completos (não palavras isoladas). Frases-modelo de 5 a 8 palavras como unidade de ensino. CCQs em inglês simples com apoio visual quando necessário. Exercícios de matching, fill-in-the-blanks e quiz de múltipla escolha na fase de prática. Respostas sempre em frases completas — nunca respostas monossilábicas. Production via role-play sem script por 10 a 15 minutos por aula, em cenários previsíveis de viagem.
necessidadesPedagogicas.adaptacaoIdade: Adulto de 45 anos (faixa 31–50): foco em ROI imediato e concreto — cada aula deve mostrar aplicação prática direta. Temas profissionais e de viagem têm prioridade absoluta. Referências culturais acessíveis e relevantes para a geração. Ritmo respeitoso do tempo adulto: sem infantilização, sem excesso de jogos sem propósito. Validar experiência de vida do aluno como recurso pedagógico — Roberto já viajou, já negociou, já resolveu problemas; o inglês é o veículo, não o conteúdo.
necessidadesPedagogicas.estiloAprendizagem: Cinestésico-comunicativo: priorizar produção oral desde o primeiro minuto. Usar role-plays em pé, simulações com objetos físicos (cardápio impresso, mapa real de Barcelona/Paris, bilhete de metrô), drilling com movimento. Evitar longas explicações gramaticais — introduzir regras via exemplos em contexto, não via metalinguagem. Feedback imediato e reformulação gentil durante a fala, não após.
necessidadesPedagogicas.ritmoIdeal: Dinâmico e variado, com blocos curtos de 12 a 15 minutos alternando atividades: apresentação do chunk → drilling oral → mini role-play → correção guiada → novo chunk. Evitar aulas monotemáticas ou com mais de 15 minutos na mesma atividade. Interrupções estratégicas para correção são bem-vindas — o aluno declarou preferência por estrutura com roteiro, então o professor deve sempre sinalizar a transição entre blocos.
tiposExercicio: Role-play em cenários de viagem sem script (aeroporto, restaurante, hotel, transporte)
Drilling oral de chunks em repetição guiada (repeat after me → adapte → use sozinho)
Fill-in-the-blanks com diálogos reais de viagem
Quiz de múltipla escolha para vocabulário de cardápio e sinalização
Simulação com objetos físicos: cardápio, mapa, bilhete de metrô, nota de euro
True/false baseado em diálogos gravados (ElevenLabs) de situações de viagem
Correção guiada: professor fala uma frase com erro sutil, Roberto identifica e corrige
midiasApropriadas: Rick Steves' Europe (PBS) — documentário de viagem em inglês americano acessível, episódios sobre Barcelona e Paris disponíveis no YouTube

Série 'Emily in Paris' (Netflix) — inglês americano em contexto parisiense, legendas disponíveis em inglês e português
Série 'The Tourist' (Netflix) — inglês acessível em contexto de viajante em situações imprevisíveis, ideal para motivação
Aplicativo Pimsleur English (nível A2) — áudio focado em produção oral sem texto, 30 minutos por sessão
necessidadesPedagogicas.adaptacaoModalidade: Presencial: explorar ao máximo o espaço físico. Usar flashcards físicos com chunks de um lado e situação-gatilho do outro. Imprimir cardápios reais de restaurantes de Barcelona e Paris para drilling de pedidos. Usar mapas físicos das cidades para prática de direções. Simular ambientes: professor assume papel de garçom, recepcionista ou agente de imigração. Quadro branco para construção colaborativa de frases — Roberto escreve, professor ajusta em tempo real. Gravação em áudio do role-play final de cada aula para post-class review (feedback estruturado pelo professor).

indicacaoProfessor.perfilIdeal:
PERFIL ACADÊMICO: Professor com experiência mínima de 3 anos no ensino de inglês para adultos (31–50 anos) em nível A2 com foco comunicativo. Certificação CELTA ou TESOL preferível — garante domínio de técnicas de form-focused instruction e task-based learning, ambas indicadas no diagnóstico. Especialidade em Travel English e/ou Business English aplicado. Fluência em inglês americano (padrão Alumni) com pronúncia clara e velocidade controlável. Experiência com role-play estruturado e drilling oral. Familiaridade com cultura espanhola e francesa é diferencial significativo dado o destino da viagem.

PERFIL DE PERSONALIDADE: Energia alta e dinâmica — Roberto sustentou energia constante durante a consultoria e declarou preferência por aulas 'dinâmicas e variadas'. Professor apático ou de ritmo lento vai desengajar este aluno rapidamente. Feedback direto mas encorajador — tolerância ao erro variável exige professor que corrija com leveza e sem constrangimento, especialmente nos dias de menor confiança. Capacidade de ler o humor do aluno e ajustar o tom da aula (mais estruturado em dias de ansiedade, mais fluido em dias de confiança). Senso de humor presente mas sem infantilizar — Roberto é um empresário adulto que quer ser tratado como inteligente. Organizado e pontual — Roberto declarou preferência por 'aulas bem estruturadas com roteiro', então o professor deve sempre chegar com plano claro e comunicá-lo no início de cada aula.
justificativa: Roberto tem ansiedade comunicativa moderada e tolerância ao erro variável — professor dinâmico e encorajador reduz o bloqueio e cria ambiente seguro para a produção espontânea
Estilo de aprendizagem cinestésico-comunicativo exige professor que priorize produção oral e role-play desde o primeiro minuto, com habilidade de conduzir drilling sem torná-lo mecânico

A viagem em agosto cria urgência de resultado — professor com experiência em Travel English e conhecimento das culturas espanhola e francesa vai personalizar os cenários com autenticidade e antecipar situações reais que Roberto vai enfrentar

perfil.justificativaPrograma: Roberto Pires chega ao programa com uma combinação rara de urgência real, motivação alta e perfil pedagógico bem definido. A viagem para Barcelona e Paris em agosto não é um sonho distante — é um prazo concreto que transforma cada uma das 48 aulas em preparação aplicada. O nível A2, combinado com a preferência por aprender praticando e a energia constante observada na consultoria, indica que Roberto vai responder bem a uma trilha densa e progressiva de Travel English, desde que o ganho seja tangível a cada aula. A frequência de 3 vezes por semana garante ritmo de imersão sem sobrecarga, e o formato presencial de 60 minutos permite o tipo de interação física (role-plays, objetos, simulações) que o perfil cinestésico demanda. A estrutura de 13 fases — do Survival Kit às simulações integradas finais — foi desenhada para entregar vitórias concretas a cada ciclo, mantendo Roberto engajado pelo ROI imediato: cada fase concluída é um cenário de viagem que ele domina. O programa também respeita a realidade cognitiva do A2: começar pelos chunks de sobrevivência antes de expandir para conversação livre garante que Roberto chegue em agosto com respostas automáticas nos momentos de maior pressão.
perguntasValidacao: Roberto, você já viajou para algum país de língua inglesa antes? Como foi a experiência de se comunicar?
Quando você pensa na viagem para Barcelona e Paris, qual é a situação específica que mais te preocupa em termos de comunicação em inglês?
Você já estudou inglês formalmente antes — escola, curso, aplicativo? Por quanto tempo e até qual ponto chegou?
No seu trabalho como empresário, você tem algum contato com inglês hoje — e-mails, reuniões com fornecedores, documentos? Ou o inglês é exclusivamente para uso pessoal e viagens?
Quais são seus hobbies e interesses fora do trabalho? Séries, esportes, gastronomia? Esses temas podem enriquecer os materiais das aulas.
conflitosIdentificados: Duração de aula declarada é 60 minutos, mas a Regra 138 define 90 minutos como padrão universal para adultos. Gestor deve confirmar se 60 minutos é a duração definitiva ou se há possibilidade de ajuste para 90 minutos, especialmente considerando a densidade da trilha de 48 aulas.
Horários disponíveis não foram informados (Bloco 2) — sem esse dado, a alocação de professor e sala presencial não pode ser concluída. Coleta urgente necessária antes da primeira aula.
WhatsApp não informado (Bloco 1) — canal de comunicação para envio de material pré-aula e feedback pós-aula fica comprometido. Confirmar canal preferido do aluno.

Stake e vitória não foram declarados formalmente (Bloco 4) — os valores presentes em 'eventoAlvo' são inferências baseadas no diagnóstico pedagógico, não declarações do aluno. Validar com Roberto antes ou na aula 1 para fortalecer o compromisso emocional com o programa.

Profissão declarada como 'Empresário' sem detalhes de setor, empresa ou uso do inglês no trabalho — se Roberto usa inglês profissionalmente (e-mails, reuniões, contratos), há oportunidade de integrar Business English à trilha, mas isso requer confirmação explícita para não desviar do foco Travel English acordado.

perfil.transcricao:
OBJETIVO GERAL
Desenvolver fluidez na comunicação em inglês, reduzindo a dependência do português e aprimorando a construção de frases com maior precisão gramatical, com aplicação direta em Travel English (Barcelona e Paris).
Documento confidencial —uso pedagógico interno
Diagnóstico Pedagógico
Roberto Pires —A2
PERFIL GERAL
Aluno em estágio A2. Apresenta vocabulário funcional básico e pronúncia confortável, mas demonstra dificuldade recorrente na construção de frases completas durante a fala espontânea —a compreensão evolui mais rápido do que a produção livre. Como há viagem prevista para agosto, o trabalho tem objetivo aplicado e prazo definido, o que aumenta a relevância pedagógica de cada aula e justifica uma trilha 100% direcionada a Travel English.
PONTOS DE FORÇA
PONTOS DE ATENÇÃO
Vocabulário funcional básico
Boa retenção de palavras isoladas, ainda com pouca variedade de chunks (frases prontas) e collocations —base lexical para apoiar a expansão da construção de frases.
Pronúncia clara e inteligível
Boa percepção sonora e ausência de bloqueios articulatórios.
Construção de frases (sintaxe)
Principal lacuna. Frases incompletas e estruturas truncadas —fragilidade em estruturas-base (SVO completo, do/does, marcação de tempo, conectivos básicos).
Recurso ao português (code-switching)
Frequente, como compensação para lacunas estruturais ou lexicais. Indica carga cognitiva elevada e ansiedade comunicativa moderada em contextos de pressão real.
IMPLICAÇÃO PEDAGÓGICA
Recomenda-se combinar duas frentes: corrigir e construir frases (form-focused instruction) com prática em situações reais de viagem (task-based learning). A ideia é expor o aluno a muitas frases prontas (chunks) para reduzir a tradução mental e gerar fala fluente nas situações previsíveis. Considerando a viagem em agosto (Barcelona e Paris), faz sentido priorizar ganho rápido emcenários previsíveis —aeroporto, hotel, restaurante, transporte —antes de expandir para conversação livre, garantindo retorno tangível em curto prazo.
FORMATO DE ESTUDO
PRE-CLASS
Estudo guiado com material direcionado pelo professor: introdução ao tema da aula e ativação prévia (priming) do vocabulário específico, garantindo repertório mínimo para participação ativa.
IN-CLASS
Aula majoritariamente comunicativa com conversação guiada em situações reais. Interrupções estratégicas para correção, reformulação e drilling. Alternância entre fluência (fala livre) e precisão (correção e construção).
POST-CLASS
Feedback estruturado pelo professor: pontos gramaticais observados, correções recorrentes, sugestão de reforço com micro-prática direcionada e consolidação dos erros mais frequentes.

temas.tema: Diagnostic + First Words: Who Is Roberto Pires? — Self-Introduction and Travel Goals
temas.focoLinguistico: Diagnostic baseline: present simple (I am, I work, I live, I have); basic personal information chunks; travel vocabulary activation check (airport, hotel, restaurant, trip); expressions: 'My name is…', 'I'm from…', 'I work in…', 'I want to…', 'This August, I'm going to…'; vocabulary circulating: name, city, work, trip, travel, hotel, restaurant, airport, Spain, France (10 words — all diagnostic, not new content)
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Ice-breaker diagnostic: Teacher asks Roberto 10 casual questions in English (name, city, job, family, hobbies, travel plans) and maps spontaneous production: sentence length, code-switching moments, pronunciation comfort zones, and vocabulary gaps. No correction — pure observation. Phase 2 (20 min) — Mind map on whiteboard: Roberto places English words he already knows around the theme 'My August Trip' — airport, Barcelona, Paris, food, hotel. Teacher adds 2-3 words per cluster he missed, expanding the map organically. Phase 3 (25 min) — Structured baseline speaking: Roberto delivers a 90-second spoken introduction ('Hi, I'm Roberto. I'm from São Paulo. I'm a businessman. In August, I'm going to Barcelona and Paris.') — teacher models first, then Roberto mirrors, then improvises. Teacher records key gaps. Phase 4 (10 min) — Debrief in English + Portuguese: teacher presents the 48-lesson journey visually (phases 1–13 on paper), explains what Roberto will be able to do by August, and pins the first 3 'wins' on a physical progress board. Product: baseline audio recorded + mind map kept as living document.
temas.homework: Record a 60-second audio on your phone: introduce yourself in English as if meeting someone on the plane to Barcelona. Use: name, city, job, destination, and one thing you want to do there. No script — just speak naturally. Send the audio to the teacher. Estimated time: 15–20 minutes. Also: write 5 English words you already know about airports and 5 about restaurants on a sticky note and bring to class 2.

temas.tema: Survival Kit Part 1 — The Magic Phrases That Open Every Door in Europe
temas.focoLinguistico: Core travel chunks (Presentation focus): 'Excuse me…', 'Can you help me?', 'Where is…?', 'How much is this?', 'I don't understand.', 'Can you repeat that, please?', 'Do you speak English?'; grammar: yes/no questions with 'Can' and 'Do' (A2 Foundation); vocabulary NEW (6): excuse, repeat, slowly, understand, help, speak; vocabulary REVIEW (4): airport, hotel, restaurant, trip; expression: 'I'm sorry, I don't understand' as a complete survival phrase; idiomatic: 'Bear with me' (transparent for A2)
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Presentation via real-life scenario: Teacher narrates: 'Roberto, you just landed in Barcelona. You're tired. You need the exit. A woman walks by. What do you say?' Teacher demonstrates the chunk 'Excuse me, can you help me?' using physical movement — standing up, making eye contact, using hand gesture. Roberto repeats with gesture. Phase 2 (20 min) — Flashcard drilling with physical cards: 8 printed situation cards (lost in airport / can't find hotel / don't understand the menu / need to repeat) — Roberto picks a card, teacher plays the European local, Roberto uses the correct survival chunk. Drill for speed and naturalness, not perfection. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Phrase Tennis' game: Teacher throws a situation verbally ('You need to ask the price of something'), Roberto returns the correct chunk within 5 seconds. Incorrect or slow = teacher models, Roberto repeats 3x. Phase 4 (20 min) — Mini role-play: Teacher is a Spanish hotel receptionist who speaks limited English. Roberto arrives with a problem (lost luggage). He uses only the 8 chunks learned. Debrief: which chunks felt natural? Which needed more drilling? Product: Roberto can produce all 8 chunks on demand without hesitation.
temas.homework: Practice the 8 survival chunks out loud, 3 times each, in front of a mirror. Then record a 45-second audio using at least 5 of the chunks in a 'imaginary conversation' (you play both sides). Send audio to teacher. Estimated time: 20 minutes. Also: find one image of Barcelona online and write 3 sentences in English about what you see, using words from class.

temas.tema: Survival Kit Part 2 — Numbers, Prices, and Saying No Politely at European Markets
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Foundation): present simple for transactions ('It costs…', 'I want…', 'I need…', 'I have…'); numbers 1–1,000 in context (prices in euros); polite refusals: 'No, thank you.', 'That's too expensive.', 'I'll think about it.'; vocabulary NEW (7): price, euro, receipt, cash, card, expensive, cheap; vocabulary REVIEW (5): help, understand, excuse, hotel, restaurant; expression new: 'I'll take it!' / 'Just looking, thanks.'; pronunciation focus: -teen vs. -ty (thirteen vs. thirty — common error for Brazilians)
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Number warm-up with physical flashcards: Teacher holds price tags (€12, €35, €150, €290, €1,000) — Roberto reads aloud. Teacher focuses on the -teen/-ty distinction with immediate gentle correction and repetition. Roberto writes prices dictated by teacher (5 rounds). Phase 2 (20 min) — 'La Boqueria Market' simulation: Teacher sets up 3 'stalls' on the table using printed images of Barcelona's La Boqueria market — one with scarves, one with food, one with souvenirs. Roberto 'shops': picks up item, asks price, decides to buy or politely refuse. Teacher plays vendor with varied accents (slow and clear). Phase 3 (20 min) — Price negotiation role-play: Teacher plays a persistent market seller. Roberto practices saying 'That's too expensive,' 'I'll think about it,' and 'Just looking, thanks' with natural tone and body language. Three rounds with increasing vendor persistence. Phase 4 (15 min) — Listening task: Teacher reads 5 short vendor-customer dialogues aloud at natural speed. Roberto identifies the price and whether the customer bought or refused. Debrief: what was hard to catch? Product: Roberto completes a mock market transaction from greeting to goodbye without switching to Portuguese.
temas.homework: Watch a 3-minute YouTube video about Barcelona's La Boqueria market (teacher sends link). Write down 5 English words you hear or see. Then practice reading prices aloud: write 10 random euro amounts and read them out loud, recording yourself. Send the audio. Estimated time: 25 minutes.

temas.tema: At the Airport — Check-In, Security, and Finding Your Gate Without Panic
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Foundation): past simple — regular verbs (checked, asked, waited, showed, walked); time expressions: yesterday, last time, before (for telling stories at airport); airport-specific vocabulary NEW (8): boarding pass, gate, check-in, security, departure, arrival, carry-on, passport; vocabulary REVIEW (4): help, repeat, excuse, price; expression: 'I used to fly a lot…' (A2 expression list) for warm-up context; chunks: 'Window or aisle?', 'Place your bags on the belt.', 'Do you have anything to declare?', 'My flight is at…'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Vocabulary presentation via airport map: Teacher has a printed GRU (Guarulhos) airport layout and walks Roberto through it in English: 'This is the check-in counter. This is security. This is gate B7.' Roberto labels each area on a blank copy. Physical interaction with the map makes vocabulary sticky. Phase 2 (20 min) — Listening drill: Teacher reads 4 realistic airport announcements aloud (gate change, boarding call, security alert, delayed flight). Roberto identifies key information: flight number, gate, action required. Teacher reads twice — first for gist, second for detail. Phase 3 (20 min) — Role-play sequence (full airport journey): Roberto is at GRU checking in for his Barcelona flight. Teacher plays (a) check-in agent, (b) security officer, (c) gate agent. Roberto uses target chunks at each stop. Teacher introduces one complication per round (overweight bag / forgot to remove laptop / wrong gate). Phase 4 (15 min) — Past simple debrief storytelling: After the role-play, Roberto narrates what happened: 'I checked in. I showed my passport. I waited at gate B7.' Teacher corrects verb forms in context, not in isolation. Product: Roberto narrates a 5-sentence airport story in past simple.
temas.homework: Write a paragraph (5–7 sentences) describing your last flight experience in English, using past simple. Use at least 4 airport words from class. Also: listen to a real boarding announcement on YouTube (search 'airport boarding announcement English') and write down 3 things you understood. Estimated time: 30 minutes.

temas.tema: At the Airport — Lost Baggage, Problems, and Staying Calm Under Pressure
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Foundation): past simple — irregular verbs (went, took, left, came, said, brought, lost, found); problem-reporting chunks: 'My bag didn't arrive.', 'I left it on the plane.', 'Someone took my bag by mistake.', 'I need to report a lost bag.'; vocabulary NEW (6): luggage, claim, carousel, missing, report, delayed; vocabulary REVIEW (6): boarding pass, passport, gate, check-in, arrive, departure; expression: 'It depends on…' (A2 list) — 'It depends on the airline.'; idiomatic: 'Don't panic' used as a teachable chunk in context
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Irregular verb activation game: Teacher lays 10 verb cards face-down (go/went, take/took, leave/left, come/came, say/said, bring/brought, lose/lost, find/found, have/had, see/saw). Roberto flips a card, teacher gives a sentence stem ('Yesterday, I _____ my bag'), Roberto completes. Fast-paced, low-stakes, high energy. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Lost Luggage Crisis' scenario: Roberto lands in Barcelona. His bag isn't on carousel 4. Teacher plays an airline agent at the lost baggage desk (initially speaking fast, then slowing down). Roberto must: identify himself, describe the bag, report the problem, ask what happens next. Three rounds with increasing complexity. Phase 3 (20 min) — Problem-solving dialogue construction: Together, teacher and Roberto build a model 'lost luggage' dialogue on the whiteboard using irregular past verbs. Roberto copies it, then closes the notebook and reconstructs from memory with teacher prompts only. Phase 4 (20 min) — Stress-inoculation drill: Teacher plays an impatient, fast-speaking airline agent. Roberto's job: stay calm, use the chunks, ask for repetition when needed ('Can you repeat that, please?'). Debrief: what worked? What caused a freeze? Product: Roberto can handle a lost luggage scenario from report to resolution using past simple accurately.
temas.homework: Write a short story (6–8 sentences) in past simple: 'Roberto's Worst Day at the Airport.' Make it dramatic — lost bag, wrong gate, missed announcement. Use at least 6 irregular verbs from class. Estimated time: 25 minutes. Optional fun: search 'funny lost luggage stories' on YouTube and note 2 new English words.

temas.tema: Arriving in Barcelona — Hotel Check-In, Room Requests, and First Night Confidence
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Building): present continuous for current situations ('I'm looking for…', 'I'm staying at…', 'I'm waiting for…'); hotel vocabulary NEW (8): reservation, lobby, room service, double room, single room, floor, elevator, Wi-Fi; vocabulary REVIEW (4): check-in, passport, arrival, help; chunks: 'I have a reservation under the name Pires.', 'Can I have a room on a higher floor?', 'What time is breakfast?', 'The air conditioning isn't working.'; expression: 'I'm looking forward to…' (A2 list)
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Presentation via hotel brochure: Teacher brings (or prints) a real Barcelona hotel webpage (e.g., Hotel Arts Barcelona). Roberto and teacher read key sections together — room types, amenities, breakfast info. Teacher pre-teaches 8 vocabulary items using images on the brochure. Roberto labels a room layout diagram. Phase 2 (20 min) — Check-in role-play sequence: Teacher is the front desk agent at the hotel. Roberto arrives: confirms reservation under 'Pires,' asks for a high floor, requests Wi-Fi password, mentions one room problem (air conditioning not working). Three rounds: first scripted (teacher provides model), second semi-scripted (key words only), third fully spontaneous. Phase 3 (20 min) — Present continuous drilling in context: Teacher narrates Roberto's evening: 'You're unpacking. You're looking for a restaurant. You're calling room service.' Roberto transforms each into first person ('I'm unpacking. I'm looking for a restaurant…'). Then role-play: Roberto calls front desk with 3 requests using present continuous. Phase 4 (15 min) — 'Problem at the hotel' simulation: Teacher introduces a realistic complication (wrong room type, noisy neighbors, missing towels). Roberto resolves each using target language. Debrief: which phrases felt automatic? Product: Roberto completes a hotel check-in scenario from arrival to room, using present continuous naturally.
temas.homework: Write a WhatsApp-style message in English to a friend: 'I just arrived at my hotel in Barcelona. I'm staying at [hotel name]. My room is on the [floor] floor. I'm looking for a good restaurant nearby.' Use present continuous at least 3 times. Estimated time: 15 minutes. Also: look up the real Hotel Arts Barcelona in English and write 3 sentences about what you see.

temas.tema: Barcelona Restaurants — Reading a Menu, Ordering Food, and Sounding Like a Local
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Building): present simple vs. present continuous ('I usually eat fish' vs. 'Tonight, I'm eating paella'); ordering expressions: 'I'd like…', 'Can I have…?', 'What do you recommend?', 'Is this spicy?', 'The bill, please.'; restaurant vocabulary NEW (8): menu, starter, main course, dessert, waiter, bill, tip, reservation; vocabulary REVIEW (4): recommend, please, understand, price; chunks: 'For me, I'd like the…', 'Can we have the check, please?', 'What's the soup of the day?'; idiomatic: 'It's a piece of cake' (used post-meal when something was easy — taught in context)
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Real menu analysis: Teacher brings a printed menu from a real Barcelona restaurant (e.g., Bar Mut or El Nacional). Roberto reads section by section — teacher pre-teaches 8 items using images. Roberto circles 3 things he'd order and 3 he'd avoid. Discussion: 'Why? I usually eat… / I don't like…' — present simple in natural use. Phase 2 (20 min) — Ordering sequence role-play: Teacher plays a Barcelona waiter (polite, fast, slight accent). Roberto orders a full meal: greets waiter, asks for recommendation, orders starter + main + drink, asks one question about the menu, requests the bill. Three full rounds — each time teacher adds one complication (dish is sold out / Roberto misunderstands the price). Phase 3 (15 min) — Contrast drilling (present simple vs. continuous): Teacher gives Roberto sentence pairs to sort — 'I eat meat every day' vs. 'Tonight, I'm eating vegetarian.' Roberto creates 5 original sentences about his eating habits vs. tonight's plan. Phase 4 (20 min) — Full dinner simulation: Teacher sets up a 'dinner scene' (printed tablecloth image, menu on table). Roberto plays the full experience from greeting to paying, incorporating all language from the lesson. Debrief using 'It's a piece of cake!' Product: Roberto orders a complete meal in English without pointing at the menu.
temas.homework: Find a real Barcelona restaurant menu online (in English) and write your ideal dinner order in 5 sentences: starter, main, drink, dessert, and one question you would ask the waiter. Then record yourself ordering the meal out loud as if the waiter is in front of you. Send the audio. Estimated time: 20–25 minutes.

temas.tema: Tapas Culture — Ordering Tapas, Sharing Food, and Small Talk with Spanish Locals
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Building): going to (future plans) — 'We're going to share the patatas bravas.', 'I'm going to try the jamón.'; tapas vocabulary NEW (8): tapas, patatas bravas, jamón, chorizo, croquetas, pulpo, pintxos, raciones; vocabulary REVIEW (4): menu, waiter, recommend, bill; sharing/social chunks: 'Let's share this.', 'Have you tried…?', 'This is amazing!', 'What's in this?'; cultural note: tipping culture in Spain (not expected but appreciated — 5–10%); expression: 'Once in a while, I eat very well — like tonight!'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Cultural immersion warm-up: Teacher shows a 2-minute video clip of a Barcelona tapas bar (no comprehension task — just atmosphere). Discussion: 'What do you see? What do you want to try?' Roberto responds in English. Teacher introduces the 8 tapas vocabulary items using printed images. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Tapas Bar' simulation: Teacher sets up 6 printed tapas images on the table like dishes. Roberto and teacher 'share' them — Roberto uses 'Let's share this,' 'Have you tried the croquetas?', 'What's in this?' Teacher responds naturally, introducing cultural info in English (e.g., 'Patatas bravas have a spicy tomato sauce'). Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Going to' production task: Roberto plans his tapas evening using 'going to': 'I'm going to order jamón first. Then we're going to share the pulpo.' Teacher drills the structure through question-answer: 'What are you going to try first?' Roberto answers spontaneously. Phase 4 (15 min) — Small talk with a local: Teacher plays a friendly Spanish local at the tapas bar. Roberto maintains a short conversation (2–3 minutes) using all target language. Goal: sound warm, engaged, and curious — not just transactional. Product: Roberto sustains a 2-minute tapas-bar small talk exchange using 'going to' naturally.
temas.homework: Write a 'tapas plan' for your first night in Barcelona: use 'going to' at least 5 times. Example: 'I'm going to go to a tapas bar near the hotel. I'm going to try patatas bravas and jamón.' Also: look up 3 tapas you've never heard of and write one sentence about each in English. Estimated time: 20 minutes.

temas.tema: Getting Around Barcelona — Metro, Taxi, and Asking for Directions Like a Pro
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Building): going to + will contrast — 'I'm going to take the metro' (plan) vs. 'I'll take a taxi — it's easier' (instant decision); directions vocabulary NEW (8): straight ahead, turn left, turn right, block, corner, near, far, cross; vocabulary REVIEW (4): metro, exit, help, understand; direction chunks: 'How do I get to…?', 'Is it far from here?', 'Take the L5 line.', 'Get off at…'; taxi chunks: 'Can you take me to…?', 'How long will it take?', 'Keep the change.'; idiomatic: 'You can't miss it.'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Barcelona metro map activity: Teacher provides a printed Barcelona metro map. Roberto traces 3 routes from his hotel (e.g., Hotel Arts) to La Sagrada Família, La Barceloneta beach, and Camp Nou — in English, describing the route: 'Take the L4. Get off at Verdaguer. Walk straight ahead.' Teacher corrects and models natural phrasing. Phase 2 (20 min) — Directions role-play on a street grid: Teacher draws a simple Barcelona neighborhood grid on the whiteboard. Roberto is at one point, teacher is a local on the street. Three rounds: Roberto asks for directions to 3 different landmarks. Teacher gives directions (increasing complexity). Roberto must physically trace the route on the grid as he listens. Phase 3 (15 min) — Going to vs. will decision game: Teacher presents 5 transport dilemmas ('It's raining. The metro is closed. You're late.'). Roberto decides instantly: 'I'll take a taxi!' or 'I'm going to walk — it's only 2 blocks.' Teacher drills the contrast through rapid scenarios. Phase 4 (20 min) — Taxi simulation: Teacher plays a Barcelona taxi driver (chatty, fast-talking). Roberto gives destination, asks how long, asks about traffic, and pays at the end. Two rounds: one calm, one with a complication (driver takes wrong route). Product: Roberto gives and follows a 4-step set of directions using both 'going to' and 'will' correctly.
temas.homework: Using Google Maps in English, plan a route from your Barcelona hotel to La Sagrada Família. Write the directions in English (5–6 steps): 'Turn left on…, Walk straight ahead…, Take the metro at…' Estimated time: 20 minutes. Also: record yourself giving those directions out loud as if explaining to a friend. Send the audio.

temas.tema: Barcelona Culture & Sightseeing — Talking About What You See, Feel, and Want to Do
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Building): comparatives and superlatives — 'La Sagrada Família is more impressive than I expected.', 'This is the most beautiful building I've ever seen.'; cultural vocabulary NEW (7): cathedral, architect, neighborhood, exhibition, guided tour, view, architecture; vocabulary REVIEW (5): recommend, amazing, beautiful, visit, go; opinion expressions: 'In my opinion…', 'I think…', 'I agree / I disagree' (A2 list); chunks: 'What's this building called?', 'When was it built?', 'Is there a guided tour in English?'; superlative: 'the most visited', 'the most famous'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Photo comparison warm-up: Teacher brings 6 printed images of Barcelona landmarks (Sagrada Família, Park Güell, Gothic Quarter, Barceloneta, Palau de la Música, Camp Nou). Roberto ranks them from 'most to least interesting' and explains using comparatives: 'Park Güell is more colorful than the Gothic Quarter.' Teacher feeds superlatives naturally: 'Which is the most famous?' Phase 2 (20 min) — Role-play: Tourist and tour guide: Teacher plays a local Barcelona guide. Roberto plays himself as a tourist. At each landmark (shown via image), Roberto asks one question ('What's this called?', 'When was it built?', 'Is there a tour in English?'). Teacher answers at natural pace, Roberto confirms understanding. Three landmarks covered. Phase 3 (20 min) — Opinion debate: Teacher presents two options (e.g., 'Gothic Quarter vs. Barceloneta — which is better for an evening?'). Roberto defends his choice using 'In my opinion…', comparatives, and reasons. Teacher plays devil's advocate to push production. Phase 4 (15 min) — Postcard writing (spoken version): Roberto 'dictates' a postcard from Barcelona to a friend: 'Dear [friend], I'm in Barcelona. This is the most beautiful city I've ever visited. Yesterday, I went to…' Teacher notes grammar and vocabulary accuracy. Product: Roberto produces 5 comparative/superlative sentences about Barcelona from memory.
temas.homework: Write a 'postcard' paragraph (6–8 sentences) from Barcelona to a Brazilian friend. Use: 3 comparatives, 1 superlative, and 2 opinion expressions from class. Also: search for 'Barcelona top sights' in English and add 2 new words you found to your vocabulary notebook. Estimated time: 25 minutes.

temas.tema: Shopping in Barcelona — Clothing, Souvenirs, and Handling Payment Problems
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Building): comparatives review + will for instant decisions ('This one is bigger, but I'll take the smaller one.'); shopping vocabulary NEW (8): size, fitting room, receipt, exchange, refund, discount, souvenir, sale; vocabulary REVIEW (4): price, expensive, cheap, cash; shopping chunks: 'Do you have this in a larger size?', 'Can I try this on?', 'This doesn't fit.', 'Can I get a refund?', 'Do you accept credit cards?'; expression: 'I would rather…' — 'I would rather pay by card.'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Vocabulary introduction via shopping scenario: Teacher lays out 8 printed clothing/souvenir images on the table with price tags in euros. Roberto identifies items, guesses prices, and teacher introduces 8 new vocabulary items through the items themselves. Roberto uses new words immediately: 'This souvenir is too expensive. Do you have a discount?' Phase 2 (20 min) — Shopping role-play in 3 stages: Stage 1 — Roberto enters a Barcelona clothing store, asks for his size, tries something on (mimed). Stage 2 — Item doesn't fit, Roberto asks for a different size. Stage 3 — Item has a defect, Roberto asks for a refund or exchange. Teacher plays a helpful but policy-strict shop assistant. Phase 3 (15 min) — 'I would rather…' drilling: Teacher gives Roberto 5 payment/shopping dilemmas ('Pay cash or card?', 'Buy now or wait for the sale?'). Roberto responds: 'I would rather pay by card because it's safer.' Drill for natural flow. Phase 4 (20 min) — Complaint handling simulation: Teacher plays a shop assistant who says the item can't be refunded. Roberto must calmly insist, ask for a manager, and resolve the situation using all target language. Debrief: how did it feel to complain in English? Product: Roberto completes a full shopping interaction including a problem resolution.
temas.homework: Write a 'shopping diary' entry (5–7 sentences): describe a shopping experience in Barcelona (real or imaginary). Use: 3 new vocabulary words, 1 'I would rather…' sentence, and 1 comparative. Also: record yourself asking for a refund in English (practice the scenario from class). Send audio. Estimated time: 20 minutes.

temas.tema: Mid-Program Check-In — What Roberto Has Learned and What Comes Next (Aulas 1–11 Review)
temas.focoLinguistico: Full review across all 11 aulas: past simple (regular + irregular), present continuous, going to, will, comparatives/superlatives; vocabulary review clusters: airport (8), hotel (8), restaurant/tapas (12), transport/directions (8), shopping (8); expressions recycled: 'Excuse me / Can you help me / I'd like / I'm looking forward to / I would rather / It depends on'; NEW: 2 recycled idiomatic expressions used in fresh contexts ('It's a piece of cake' and 'Once in a while'); grammar gap-fill: identifying what Roberto still confuses (going to vs. will; comparatives vs. superlatives)
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — 'Barcelona Day' warm-up narrative: Roberto narrates his 'ideal day in Barcelona' using all tenses learned: 'Yesterday, I went to the market. Right now, I'm looking for a restaurant. This evening, I'm going to try tapas.' Teacher notes which structures are now automatic and which still need support. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Vocabulary Olympics': Teacher creates a fast-paced game with 4 rounds — (1) Teacher gives a definition, Roberto says the word; (2) Roberto translates chunks back to English from Portuguese; (3) Role-play prompt cards (Roberto picks and performs in 30 seconds); (4) Superlative challenge (Roberto makes sentences about Barcelona landmarks). Phase 3 (20 min) — Diagnostic role-play marathon: 5 mini role-plays back-to-back (airport check-in / hotel problem / restaurant order / tapas bar / shopping return). Teacher times each and notes fluency vs. accuracy balance. This is Roberto's mid-program speaking assessment. Phase 4 (15 min) — Progress debrief + forward planning: Teacher shows Roberto his progress on the physical board from lesson 1. Celebrates genuine wins (specific moments of fluency). Identifies 2–3 focus areas for aulas 13–24. Roberto updates his own 'confidence meter' (1–10) for each scenario covered. Product: teacher-generated written feedback summary handed to Roberto; confidence meter completed.
temas.homework: Record a 2-minute audio: 'My Barcelona Week' — narrate an imaginary week in Barcelona using all tenses and vocabulary from aulas 1–11. Use at least: 3 past simple sentences, 2 present continuous, 2 going to, 1 comparative, and 5 travel vocabulary words. Send audio before next class. Estimated time: 30 minutes.

temas.tema: Barcelona to Paris — The Journey: Train, Transfers, and Staying Comfortable on the Move
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion — intro): modal verbs 'should' and 'could' — 'You should book the AVE in advance.', 'We could take the overnight train.'; transport vocabulary NEW (8): high-speed train, platform, ticket office, connection, departure board, first class, luggage rack, conductor; vocabulary REVIEW (5): boarding pass, departure, arrival, gate, delay; travel advice chunks: 'You should arrive early.', 'Could you show me my seat?', 'I think I missed my connection.'; expression: 'I used to travel a lot, but now…' (A2 list — recycled in new context)
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Route planning with real map: Teacher shows Roberto a printed Barcelona–Paris train route (AVE to Lyon + TGV to Paris Gare de Lyon). Together they read the itinerary in English: departure times, platform numbers, connection in Lyon. Roberto reads aloud, teacher corrects pronunciation of French station names in an English context. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Should/Could' presentation and drilling: Teacher presents should (advice/recommendation) vs. could (possibility/suggestion) using 5 travel scenarios. Roberto drills: 'Should I book in advance?' → 'Yes, you should — the AVE sells out fast.' Five rapid-fire question-answer pairs. Phase 3 (20 min) — Train journey role-play: Teacher plays a train conductor + a fellow passenger. Roberto: (a) shows ticket, (b) asks where to put luggage, (c) asks if the train stops in Lyon, (d) reports that someone is in his seat. Two rounds — first rehearsed, second spontaneous with one surprise complication. Phase 4 (15 min) — Listening task: Teacher reads a realistic train announcement (platform change + delay) at natural speed. Roberto identifies: new platform, reason for delay, new departure time. Three repetitions: gist → detail → verification. Product: Roberto handles 4 train travel scenarios using 'should' and 'could' correctly.
temas.homework: Write 5 travel tips for a friend going from Barcelona to Paris by train. Use 'should' or 'could' in every sentence. Example: 'You should book your ticket at least 3 days before.' Estimated time: 15 minutes. Also: search 'AVE Barcelona Paris' in English and write 2 facts you learned in English. Estimated time: 10 minutes.

temas.tema: Arriving in Paris — First Impressions, RER Airport Train, and Getting to the Hotel
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion): modal verbs 'should/could' + 'might' — 'The RER might be faster.', 'We should validate the ticket.'; Paris arrival vocabulary NEW (8): RER, validate, turnstile, zone, Métro, arrondissement, taxi stand, Uber; vocabulary REVIEW (5): platform, connection, luggage, help, reservation; Paris-specific chunks: 'Which line goes to central Paris?', 'Do I need to validate my ticket?', 'Is this the right platform for the city center?', 'Can you take me to the 8th arrondissement?'; cultural note: Paris taxi etiquette vs. Barcelona (no front seat, fixed airport rates); idiomatic: 'Better safe than sorry' — 'Validate the ticket — better safe than sorry.'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Paris CDG arrival scenario warm-up: Teacher shows a printed Paris CDG airport map. Roberto traces the route from arrivals to the RER B in English. Teacher introduces 8 new vocabulary items through the map itself — zone, validate, turnstile, platform. Roberto labels the map. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Might/Should' transport decision game: Teacher presents 5 Paris transport dilemmas (RER vs. taxi / Métro vs. walking / Uber vs. bus). Roberto evaluates: 'The RER might be cheaper. We should take it.' Teacher drills the three modals (should/could/might) through rapid-fire transport scenarios. Phase 3 (20 min) — Airport-to-hotel full scenario: Teacher plays (a) RER ticket agent, (b) Métro fellow passenger, (c) Parisian taxi driver. Roberto navigates the full journey: buys RER ticket, validates it, asks which platform, takes taxi for last leg, gives hotel address. One complication per leg (ticket machine broken / wrong line / taxi driver doesn't know the hotel). Phase 4 (15 min) — Cultural comparison debrief: Teacher and Roberto compare Barcelona vs. Paris transport systems in English using comparatives and modals: 'The Barcelona metro is simpler. Paris might be more complicated, but the RER is faster.' Product: Roberto navigates the CDG-to-hotel journey using 'should,' 'could,' and 'might' appropriately.
temas.homework: Plan your route from CDG airport to your Paris hotel (use Google Maps in English). Write 6 steps using modals: 'I should take the RER B. It might take 35 minutes. I could also take a taxi, but it would be more expensive.' Estimated time: 20 minutes. Send the written plan as a voice note reading it aloud.

temas.tema: Parisian Cafés — Ordering Coffee, Breakfast, and Navigating French-English Culture
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion): quantifiers — 'a little,' 'a few,' 'a lot of,' 'much,' 'many' (countable vs. uncountable in food context); café vocabulary NEW (8): croissant, espresso, café au lait, baguette, terrace, waiter (garçon — cultural note only), check, jam; vocabulary REVIEW (5): menu, bill, recommend, order, please; Paris café chunks: 'A coffee, please — just a little milk.', 'Do you have a few minutes?', 'Is there much sugar in this?', 'Can I have the check, please?'; cultural etiquette: don't ask for 'café au lait' after noon — cultural survival note; expression: 'I'm looking forward to sitting at a Parisian terrace!'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Café culture presentation: Teacher brings a printed image of a Parisian café terrace with a real menu (Café de Flore or Les Deux Magots). Roberto reads the breakfast section in English. Teacher introduces 8 vocabulary items through the menu. Cultural notes embedded naturally: 'In Paris, you sit, you don't rush. The waiter won't rush you.' Phase 2 (20 min) — Quantifiers in food context: Teacher presents countable vs. uncountable through café items — 'How much coffee? / How many croissants?' Roberto drills using real menu items: 'A little sugar, please.' / 'A few croissants for the table.' / 'Not too much milk.' Five contexts drilled through question-answer with teacher playing waiter. Phase 3 (20 min) — Full café morning role-play: Roberto arrives at a Paris café terrace. Teacher plays a typically reserved Parisian waiter (not unfriendly — just formal). Roberto: greets (with cultural awareness — 'Bonjour' first!), sits, orders breakfast using quantifiers, asks one cultural question, requests the bill. Three rounds with increasing spontaneity. Phase 4 (15 min) — 'Culture clash' debrief discussion: Teacher and Roberto compare Brazilian café culture vs. Parisian in English. Roberto uses 'In my opinion,' comparatives, and new vocabulary. Product: Roberto orders a full café breakfast using quantifiers accurately and demonstrates 2 cultural etiquette points.
temas.homework: Write a 'morning in Paris' paragraph (6–8 sentences): describe ordering breakfast at a café. Use: 'a little,' 'a few,' 'a lot of,' 'much,' and 'many' — at least one each. Include 1 cultural observation. Estimated time: 20 minutes. Also: find a real Café de Flore menu online and identify 3 items you would order. Write one sentence about each.

temas.tema: Paris Restaurants — Fine Dining Language, Wine, and Handling a Formal Dinner
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion): verb + -ing vs. verb + to (introduction) — 'I enjoy eating French food.', 'I want to try the duck.', 'I avoid ordering things I can't pronounce.'; fine dining vocabulary NEW (8): sommelier, appetizer, entrée, duck confit, foie gras, tasting menu, wine pairing, reservation; vocabulary REVIEW (5): recommend, starter, bill, waiter, menu; formal dining chunks: 'We have a reservation for two under Pires.', 'What do you recommend for the main course?', 'Could you suggest a wine to pair with this?', 'It was absolutely delicious.'; expression: 'I would rather have…' in formal dining context
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Fine dining menu reading: Teacher brings a printed upscale Paris restaurant menu (e.g., a brasserie or bistro style). Roberto reads aloud — teacher helps with pronunciation of French-origin words used in English (entrée, foie gras, sommelier). Roberto identifies what he would order and why, using full sentences. Phase 2 (20 min) — Verb + -ing vs. verb + to presentation: Teacher presents 8 common verb patterns in restaurant context: enjoy/love/avoid + -ing (enjoy eating, love trying, avoid ordering) vs. want/need/decide/plan + to (want to try, need to ask, plan to have). Roberto sorts 10 sentences into two columns, then creates 5 original sentences about his food preferences. Phase 3 (20 min) — Formal dinner simulation: Teacher plays a French sommelier + waiter (formal, professional, slightly intimidating). Roberto manages the full dinner: confirms reservation, orders 3 courses, interacts with sommelier about wine, handles a small problem (wrong order), compliments the food at the end. Focus on formal register vs. casual register from earlier lessons. Phase 4 (15 min) — Register comparison drill: Teacher gives Roberto 5 phrases from casual (tapas bar) vs. formal (Paris restaurant) contexts — Roberto identifies which register and adjusts. 'Hey, what's good here?' → 'What do you recommend this evening?' Product: Roberto completes a formal Paris dinner simulation using verb + -ing/-to correctly and formal register appropriately.
temas.homework: Write a 'restaurant review' in English (6–8 sentences) of your ideal Paris dinner. Use: 3 verb + -ing forms, 2 verb + to forms, and 2 fine dining vocabulary words. Example: 'I enjoyed trying the duck confit. I plan to return.' Estimated time: 20 minutes. Send as a voice note reading your review naturally.

temas.tema: Paris Sightseeing — The Eiffel Tower, The Louvre, and Talking About Culture and Art
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion): past continuous (introduction) — 'I was walking along the Seine when I saw the Eiffel Tower.', 'People were taking photos everywhere.'; sightseeing vocabulary NEW (8): gallery, masterpiece, queue, admission, guided tour, sculpture, Seine, landmark; vocabulary REVIEW (5): exhibition, beautiful, impressive, recommend, view; storytelling chunks: 'The best part was…', 'I couldn't believe how…', 'You have to see…', 'It's more impressive in person.'; comparatives recycled: 'The Louvre is much bigger than I expected.'; idiomatic: 'Once in a while, you see something that takes your breath away.'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Past continuous presentation via photo story: Teacher shows 4 printed photos of Paris landmarks — Eiffel Tower, Louvre, Notre-Dame, Seine riverbank. Teacher narrates in past continuous: 'In this photo, people were queuing for the Louvre. A couple was walking by the Seine.' Roberto identifies the ongoing actions. Then Roberto describes each photo using past continuous. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Past continuous + when' storytelling: Teacher gives Roberto 5 story prompt cards (each with a Paris landmark + a surprise event). Roberto creates mini-stories: 'I was looking at the Mona Lisa when the lights went out.' Teacher focuses on the 'was/were + -ing + when + past simple' pattern. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Paris Day Out' narration role-play: Teacher plays Roberto's friend on a video call asking about his Paris day. Roberto narrates his sightseeing using past continuous + past simple: 'I was walking near the Eiffel Tower when I decided to go up. The view was the most beautiful thing I've ever seen.' Minimum 8 sentences. Phase 4 (15 min) — Cultural comparison conversation: 'Is the Louvre worth the queue?' Teacher and Roberto debate using comparatives, superlatives, and opinion expressions. Production should feel natural and opinionated. Product: Roberto tells a 2-minute Paris sightseeing story using past continuous accurately in at least 4 sentences.
temas.homework: Write a story (8–10 sentences) about an imaginary day in Paris. Use past continuous at least 4 times. Include: 2 Paris landmarks, 1 'I couldn't believe how…' sentence, and 1 superlative. Example: 'I was standing in front of the Eiffel Tower when it started to rain. It was the most magical moment of my trip.' Estimated time: 25 minutes.

temas.tema: Paris Safety, Etiquette, and Emergencies — Staying Smart and Calm in the City
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion): modal verbs full set — should (advice), could (possibility), might (uncertainty), must (obligation), mustn't (prohibition) — emergency and safety context; emergency vocabulary NEW (8): police station, embassy, pickpocket, emergency, ambulance, lost, stolen, complaint; vocabulary REVIEW (5): help, report, passport, phone, problem; emergency chunks: 'My wallet was stolen.', 'I need to find the nearest police station.', 'Can you call an ambulance?', 'I lost my passport.', 'I need to contact the Brazilian embassy.'; safety tips in English: mustn't carry all cards together / should keep passport copy; expression: 'It depends on the situation — but you should always stay calm.'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Safety tip brainstorm: Teacher asks Roberto: 'What are 5 things you should do to stay safe in Paris?' Roberto brainstorms in English with support. Teacher introduces must/mustn't through safety rules: 'You must keep your passport safe.' / 'You mustn't carry all your cash in one place.' Roberto creates his own Paris Safety Card (written on an index card to keep). Phase 2 (20 min) — Emergency role-plays (3 rapid scenarios): Scenario 1 — Roberto's phone was pickpocketed on the Métro. Teacher plays a police officer. Roberto reports the incident using past simple + modals. Scenario 2 — Roberto feels unwell at a café. Teacher plays a concerned waiter. Roberto asks for help and describes symptoms. Scenario 3 — Roberto lost his passport. Teacher plays Brazilian consulate officer. Roberto explains calmly using all target modals and past tenses. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Stress inoculation' emergency call: Teacher plays a fast-talking French emergency operator (111 equivalent). Roberto must stay calm, identify himself, give his location, and describe the problem. Teacher increases speed and confusion each round. Debrief: what froze Roberto? What was automatic? Phase 4 (15 min) — 'Must/Mustn't' card game: Teacher presents 10 situation cards. Roberto must state a rule using must or mustn't within 5 seconds. Fast, fun, and builds automaticity. Product: Roberto reports an emergency scenario in English calmly and accurately using the full range of modals.
temas.homework: Write your personal 'Paris Safety Plan' in English: 10 sentences using should, must, mustn't, could, and might. Include real plans (e.g., 'I should keep a photo of my passport on my phone. I mustn't carry all my cash in my wallet.'). Estimated time: 20 minutes. This document will be brought to class 19 for role-play use.

temas.tema: Integration Role Play — Roberto's Full European Day: Airport to Dinner in Paris
temas.focoLinguistico: Full integration of aulas 1–18: all grammar structures in natural connected speech (past simple, past continuous, present continuous, going to, will, should/could/might, comparatives, quantifiers, verb + -ing/to); vocabulary: 40+ words now in active use — rotating across all domains; focus today: connected speech, sentence linking with 'and,' 'but,' 'so,' 'because,' 'when'; new expression (final block intro): 'All in all…' for summarizing; idiomatic recycled: 'It's a piece of cake now' — Roberto evaluates his own progress; pronunciation: linking words in natural speech ('I went_to' / 'a lot_of' / 'should_I')
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Warm-up: Roberto draws his 'European Trip Timeline' on the whiteboard — one image per phase (GRU → BCN airport → hotel → tapas → train → Paris → café → Louvre → dinner). Teacher prompts Roberto to narrate the timeline in 60 seconds using connected tenses. Phase 2 (25 min) — 'The Grand Role Play' (Part 1 — Barcelona): Teacher plays 5 different characters in sequence (check-in agent → hotel receptionist → tapas bar waiter → transport official → shop assistant with problem). Roberto navigates each using target language from aulas 2–11. Teacher introduces one unexpected complication per scene. No pausing for preparation — Roberto responds in real time. Phase 3 (25 min) — 'The Grand Role Play' (Part 2 — Paris): Teacher plays 5 Paris characters (RER ticket agent → café waiter → Louvre guide → Paris restaurant sommelier → police officer for lost wallet). Same rules — real-time, no preparation, one complication each. Phase 4 (20 min) — Fluency debrief + self-assessment: Roberto watches a brief recording (teacher narrates what happened for key moments). Together they identify: (1) moments of real fluency, (2) grammar still causing hesitation, (3) vocabulary gaps that appeared in real time. Roberto updates his confidence meter. Product: teacher-generated 'fluency snapshot' document given to Roberto, showing progress from aula 1 baseline to aula 19.
temas.homework: Record a 3-minute audio called 'My European Adventure.' Tell the full story of your imaginary trip — from GRU to Paris — using all tenses and vocabulary from the course. Make it personal, make it vivid. Use at least: 5 past simple sentences, 3 past continuous, 3 going to, 2 should/might, and 10 travel vocabulary words. This is your most important homework of the block. Send before aula 20. Estimated time: 45 minutes.

temas.tema: Block 1 Closing — Roberto's Progress Showcase, Gap Analysis, and Preview of Block 2
temas.focoLinguistico: Review and consolidation of all Block 1 content: grammar inventory (past simple regular + irregular / present continuous / going to / will / comparatives / superlatives / should/could/might / quantifiers / past continuous / verb + -ing vs. to); active vocabulary count: ~80+ words across all domains; expressions consolidated: 'Excuse me / I'd like / I'm looking forward to / I would rather / I used to / It depends on / In my opinion / Once in a while / It's a piece of cake / All in all'; pronunciation targets revisited: -teen/-ty distinction, linking in connected speech, sentence stress in questions; NEW preview: introduction of 2 B1-adjacent chunks for Block 2 motivation: 'I've been to…' (preview only — not taught) and 'I was wondering if…' (softer request form for Paris formal contexts)
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Homework audio playback: Teacher plays Roberto's 3-minute 'European Adventure' audio from homework. Together they listen and mark: (a) green = fluent and accurate, (b) yellow = understood but grammar uncertain, (c) red = blocked or avoided. This is Roberto's most tangible evidence of growth. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Best of Block 1' celebration game: Teacher creates a rapid-fire game with Roberto's STRONGEST moments from the 20 aulas — sentences he produced, chunks he nailed, breakthroughs in role-play. Roberto must reproduce each one from a prompt. Fun, fast, confidence-building. Genuine celebration of real progress. Phase 3 (20 min) — Gap map for Block 2: Teacher presents a clear, honest, specific gap analysis: 'These 3 structures still need work in Block 2: (1) past continuous in spontaneous speech, (2) quantifier selection under pressure, (3) verb + -ing vs. to in new contexts.' Roberto and teacher agree on 3 personal focus goals for aulas 21–40. Written on his progress board. Phase 4 (15 min) — Block 2 preview and motivation: Teacher previews what's coming (aulas 21–40: deeper Paris immersion, cultural nuance, expanded modals, B1 bridge content). Teacher plays a 30-second authentic audio clip (Paris bistro ambient + English interaction) — Roberto identifies 5 words he recognizes. Closes with Roberto saying one thing in English about what he's looking forward to in Block 2. Product: completed Block 1 progress report (teacher-written, handed to Roberto), confidence meter updated, personal goals for Block 2 signed by Roberto.
temas.homework: Before Block 2 begins: re-listen to your very first audio (aula 1 homework — the 60-second self-introduction from the plane). Write 5 differences you notice between that audio and your aula 19 audio. Write them in English. Then write 3 goals for Block 2 in English: 'In Block 2, I want to…' Estimated time: 30 minutes. Bring both documents to the first class of Block 2.

temas.tema: Welcome to Amsterdam — Arriving at Schiphol, Reading Signs, and Getting to the City Center
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion review): modal verbs 'should/could/might' applied to new destination; NEW vocabulary: arrivals hall, luggage carousel, currency exchange, tram, single ticket, platform, connection, transit, stamp (passport), customs; Chunks: 'Which platform does the train leave from?', 'Should I take the tram or the metro?', 'This might take a while.'; Expression: 'Better safe than sorry.'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher displays a photo of Schiphol Airport and asks Roberto to narrate what he sees using vocab from past aulas — building retrieval and connecting to new destination. Presentation (15 min): Teacher introduces Amsterdam as the new city in Block 2, presents 8 new vocabulary items with visual flashcards of real Schiphol signage (arrivals, customs, tram symbols). Pronunciation drilling with minimal pairs (tram/train, platform/form). Practice (25 min): Gap-fill dialogue at immigration + customs roleplay using modal verbs — teacher plays border officer, Roberto plays passenger; then Roberto plays a confused tourist asking a local which tram to take to the city center, using 'should/could/might' naturally. Production (20 min): Roberto narrates aloud a 'mini story': 'I just landed at Schiphol. I should... I might... I could...' — teacher prompts with visual cues. Debrief (10 min): vocabulary card sort — Roberto categorizes words by airport zone (arrivals, customs, transit, exit).
temas.homework: Record a 90-second audio narrating Roberto's imaginary arrival at Schiphol: what he sees, what he does, which transport he chooses and why — use at least 3 modal verbs and 5 new words from class. (Est. 20 min). Also: look up the real Schiphol airport map online and write 5 sentences describing where things are using 'There is / There are' + location vocabulary. (Est. 15 min).

temas.tema: Dutch Hospitality — Checking Into an Amsterdam Canal Hotel and Requesting Room Upgrades
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: verb + -ing vs. verb + to (Expansion consolidation) in hotel request context — 'I'd like to request a room with a canal view.', 'I enjoy staying in boutique hotels.', 'I need to check out by 11.'; NEW vocabulary: canal view, boutique hotel, adjoining room, amenities, complimentary breakfast, front desk, housekeeping, late checkout, minibar, room service; Chunks: 'Is breakfast included?', 'I was hoping for a quieter room.', 'Would it be possible to…?'; Expression: 'It never hurts to ask.'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Roberto recalls hotel vocabulary from Aula 6 (Barcelona check-in) — teacher elicits phrases, writes on board, and bridges to new 'upgrade' language. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents the 'polite request upgrade' script using verb + -ing / verb + to contrast with color-coded examples on cards. Roberto identifies which form follows which verb using 3 quick sorting tasks. Practice (25 min): Controlled role-play — teacher is the front desk agent at a boutique Amsterdam canal hotel; Roberto uses the new chunks to request a room upgrade, ask about amenities, and negotiate late checkout. First round: scripted; second round: semi-free with only keyword prompts on a card. Production (20 min): Roberto designs his 'perfect Amsterdam hotel stay' — describes it aloud in 8-10 sentences using new vocabulary and verb patterns, while teacher notes errors for reformulation. Debrief (10 min): Teacher returns 3 reformulated sentences; Roberto repeats them 3 times each for pronunciation and internalization.
temas.homework: Write a simulated hotel email (8-10 sentences) from 'Roberto Pires' to a canal hotel in Amsterdam requesting a specific room type, mentioning arrival date (use American date format), asking about amenities, and requesting late checkout. Use at least 4 new vocabulary items and 2 verb + -ing / verb + to structures. (Est. 25 min). Listen to a short YouTube hotel check-in dialogue (teacher provides link) and note 3 phrases Roberto hasn't learned yet. (Est. 15 min).

temas.tema: Amsterdam Street Food and Brown Cafés — Ordering Dutch Snacks and Making Small Talk with Locals
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: quantifiers review (much/many/a lot of/a little/a few) + countable vs. uncountable in food context; NEW vocabulary: stroopwafel, herring, Dutch courage, brown café (bruine kroeg), portion, topping, draft beer, bitterballen, cheese market, open-faced sandwich; Chunks: 'I'll have a small portion of…', 'How many are in one order?', 'Is there much spice in this?'; Expression: 'Once in a while' (review A2 expression — used in new context: 'I eat street food once in a while.').; Collocation: 'grab a bite', 'wash it down with'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places real (or printed) photos of Dutch street food on the table — Roberto guesses names, rates what he'd try from 1-5, producing spontaneous reactions using 'I think', 'It looks like', 'I'd rather have…'. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new food/café words with visual flashcards; drills quantifier contrast using real food items — 'How much herring? How many bitterballen?' with Roberto responding physically (hold up fingers, point to image). Practice (25 min): Menu simulation — teacher provides a printed mock Dutch café menu in English; Roberto orders a full meal for himself and a friend using quantifiers and new vocabulary; teacher plays the waiter and creates one 'problem' (item unavailable) requiring Roberto to adapt. Production (20 min): Roberto plays the role of a Brazilian entrepreneur recommending Amsterdam street food to a colleague — describes 3 items using 'a lot of flavor', 'a few good options', 'not much spice'. Debrief (10 min): Vocabulary callback game — teacher says Dutch food, Roberto gives the English sentence using one quantifier.
temas.homework: Record a 2-minute audio as if recommending Amsterdam food to a Brazilian friend who is visiting next month — describe at least 3 foods, use quantifiers naturally, and include 'once in a while' and 'grab a bite'. (Est. 20 min). Write a short paragraph (5-7 sentences) comparing Amsterdam food to Barcelona tapas using comparatives (review from Aulas 10-11): 'Bitterballen are smaller than tapas, but…' (Est. 15 min).

temas.tema: Mid-Program Milestone — Roberto's Progress Review, Confidence Assessment, and Preview of the Final Stretch (Aulas 1–23)
temas.focoLinguistico: Full grammar inventory review: past simple (regular + irregular), present continuous, going to, will, comparatives/superlatives, modal verbs (should/could/might), quantifiers, verb + -ing vs. verb + to, past continuous; Vocabulary review across all themes: airport, hotel, restaurant, transport, directions, sightseeing, shopping, cultural etiquette; Chunks and expressions recycled: 'Excuse me, can you help me?', 'I'm looking forward to…', 'I would rather…', 'It depends on…', 'Better safe than sorry', 'Once in a while'; Self-assessment language: 'I feel more confident about…', 'I still struggle with…', 'I've improved in…'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up — Confidence Map (15 min): Roberto rates himself 1-5 on a printed grid of all major topics covered (airport, hotel, food, transport, sightseeing, emergencies) — teacher discusses patterns and validates progress. Progress Test — Communicative (30 min): Structured role-play simulating a compressed 'European day': Roberto checks into a hotel (Amsterdam), orders food at a café, asks for directions to a museum, handles a minor problem (wrong room), and tells the story of his trip so far to a new acquaintance — teacher evaluates using a simple rubric: fluency, vocabulary range, grammar accuracy, communication success. Gap Analysis (15 min): Teacher presents 3 key areas where Roberto excelled and 3 areas to develop in Block 2 aulas 25-40 — framed positively as 'next level unlocks'. Preview + Motivation Boost (20 min): Teacher unveils the Block 2 map — London, Rome, practical business English situations and high-stakes travel moments; Roberto shares which situations excite him most. Debrief (10 min): Roberto writes 3 personal goals for the second half of the program on a card to keep.
temas.homework: Write a 10-sentence 'progress journal entry' as Roberto Pires: what you could NOT do in Aula 1 that you CAN do now, what surprised you about learning English, and one goal for the next 10 aulas. (Est. 20 min). Record a 2-minute audio simulating meeting someone new and telling them about your trip — use at least 5 different grammar structures from the program. Send recording. (Est. 20 min).

temas.tema: Flying Into London — Heathrow Arrivals, Oyster Cards, and Roberto's First Tube Ride
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: past continuous + past simple (narrative combination — Expansion consolidation): 'I was waiting at the gate when they announced the delay.', 'While I was riding the Tube, I noticed the signs.'; NEW vocabulary: Oyster card, top up, Underground/Tube, zone, exit barrier, peak hours, delay announcement, terminal, connecting flight, baggage reclaim; Chunks: 'I need to top up my card.', 'Which zone is this station in?', 'The train was delayed by 20 minutes.'; Expression: 'In the nick of time.' [Note: British English context flagged for cultural awareness only — American English remains default for Roberto's production]
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher shows a short video clip (30-60 sec, no subtitles) of a Heathrow arrival scene — Roberto listens and notes 3 words he recognizes and 3 he doesn't; class discusses. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new vocabulary items using London Underground map as visual anchor — Roberto traces a route from Heathrow to central London while learning zone and station vocabulary. Grammar focus: past continuous + past simple with a timeline diagram on the board. Practice (25 min): Listening exercise — teacher reads a short narrative about a traveler's Tube journey with 3 problems; Roberto fills in a timeline placing past simple and past continuous actions. Then: dialogue practice — Roberto plays a traveler who was waiting at the wrong platform and explains what happened to an information desk worker (teacher). Production (20 min): Roberto narrates his imaginary Heathrow arrival story from start to hotel, using past continuous + past simple at least 4 times. Teacher records key errors for reformulation. Debrief (10 min): Vocabulary card game — Roberto matches Tube vocabulary to definitions without looking at notes.
temas.homework: Write 8 sentences narrating Roberto's first Tube journey — alternate between past continuous and past simple in at least 4 of them. (Est. 20 min). Find and listen to one real London Underground announcement on YouTube (teacher provides keywords to search); write down 3 words you understood and 2 you didn't. (Est. 10 min).

temas.tema: London Hotels and Business Districts — Staying Near The City, Networking Language, and Professional Small Talk
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: verb + -ing vs. verb + to (Expansion — consolidation in professional context) + present simple for professional identity; NEW vocabulary (professional + travel blend): business district, networking event, headquarters, agenda, colleague, conference center, rooftop bar, city view, boutique vs. chain hotel, concierge; Chunks: 'I'm staying near the financial district.', 'I work with clients across Europe.', 'I enjoy meeting people from different industries.'; Expression: 'It depends on…' (review — new professional context); Collocation: 'close a deal', 'run a meeting', 'keep in touch'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher asks Roberto: 'Imagine you're in London for a business dinner after a day of tourism — how do you introduce yourself in English?' Roberto attempts cold; teacher notes what flows and what breaks down. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents the 'professional small talk' script — how to introduce yourself as a Brazilian entrepreneur in London without sounding robotic; focus on verb + -ing / verb + to in professional collocations ('I enjoy working with international partners', 'I'd like to expand to Europe'). 8 new vocabulary items via visual flashcards of London business districts. Practice (25 min): Structured networking role-play — teacher plays a British entrepreneur at a hotel rooftop bar; Roberto approaches, introduces himself, asks questions, and sustains a 3-minute conversation using new vocabulary and chunks. Three rounds with different scenario cards (first meeting, reconnecting, asking for advice). Production (20 min): Roberto records a 90-second 'elevator pitch' about himself as if meeting a European partner — teacher gives feedback on structure, vocabulary use, and naturalness. Debrief (10 min): Teacher reformulates 3 of Roberto's sentences into more natural English; Roberto repeats and explains the difference.
temas.homework: Record a 2-minute audio as Roberto Pires introducing yourself to a London business contact at a hotel bar — include: name, city, what you do, what you enjoy about traveling for business, and one question for the other person. Use 3 new collocations. (Est. 20 min). Write a LinkedIn-style bio (5-7 sentences) in English for Roberto Pires. (Est. 20 min).

temas.tema: London Restaurants and Pub Culture — Ordering a Full English, Navigating Pub Menus, and British Food Etiquette
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: going to + will contrast (Building review in new cultural context) + verb + -ing for preferences; NEW vocabulary: pub, pint, full English breakfast, Sunday roast, Yorkshire pudding, side dish, set menu, fixed price, bill/check, tip culture; Chunks: 'I'll go for the…', 'What do you recommend?', 'I'm going to try something traditional tonight.', 'Can we get the check, please?'; Expression: 'I would rather…' (review — applied to food preferences: 'I would rather try the local dish than order pasta.'); Collocation: 'go for a pint', 'split the bill', 'leave a tip'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places a printed mock British pub menu on the table — Roberto reads for 2 minutes and marks what he'd order; teacher elicits reactions using 'I would rather / I'm going to / I'll'. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new vocabulary items with images of real pub dishes; contrasts 'bill' (British) vs. 'check' (American) culturally without changing Roberto's production default. Chunk drilling: 3 rounds of 'ordering ping-pong' — teacher says context, Roberto produces the chunk. Practice (25 min): Full pub dinner simulation — Roberto and teacher perform a complete pub visit from entering to paying; teacher introduces one complication (Roberto's order is unavailable); Roberto handles it using 'I'll go for…', 'In that case, I'd rather…'. Second scenario: Roberto is with a British colleague — makes recommendations and splits the bill. Production (20 min): Roberto tells the story of a 'perfect London pub night' — past tense narration with at least one past continuous + past simple combination and 5 new vocabulary items. Debrief (10 min): Compare British vs. Spanish vs. French food culture — Roberto uses comparatives to reflect on all 4 cities visited so far.
temas.homework: Write a 10-sentence diary entry as Roberto Pires after his first pub dinner in London — include what he ordered, what went wrong, how he handled it, and what he thought of the experience. Use past simple + past continuous at least twice. (Est. 20 min). Record a 60-second audio recommending a London pub dish to a friend. (Est. 10 min).

temas.tema: Sightseeing London — Buckingham Palace, The Thames, and Describing What You See Like a Confident Traveler
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: present continuous for live description ('I'm standing in front of Buckingham Palace…') + comparatives/superlatives review; NEW vocabulary: palace, guard, ceremony, exhibit, Thames River, double-decker, sightseeing tour, landmark, queue, audio guide; Chunks: 'It's bigger than I expected.', 'This is probably the most famous…', 'I'm standing right in front of…'; Expression: 'I'm looking forward to…' (review — future plans for the day's visits); Collocation: 'take a tour', 'join the queue', 'catch the ceremony'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher shows 5 photos of London landmarks without labels — Roberto identifies what he knows and describes each photo in 1-2 sentences using 'It looks like', 'I think this is', 'It's probably'. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new sightseeing vocabulary items via landmark photos; drills present continuous for live description — teacher models a 'live commentary' style ('Right now, I'm looking at the Thames. The river is flowing…') and Roberto repeats with his own observations about the photos. Practice (25 min): 'London Vlog Simulation' — Roberto narrates a walking tour of 3 London landmarks (photos on flashcards) as if recording a video for a Brazilian friend; teacher plays the friend asking questions ('Is it crowded?', 'How long did you wait?'). Roberto switches between present continuous (live description) and past simple (what happened earlier). Production (20 min): Full 3-minute London tour narration combining all 3 landmarks, comparatives, and at least 5 new words. Recorded for self-assessment. Debrief (10 min): Roberto compares London sightseeing to Barcelona and Paris — teacher elicits superlatives ('The most impressive', 'The busiest', 'The most crowded').
temas.homework: Record a 2-minute 'London vlog' audio as Roberto Pires describing one London landmark — use present continuous for live description, at least 2 comparatives, and 5 new vocabulary items. (Est. 20 min). Write 5 comparison sentences between London and Barcelona or Paris using superlatives. (Est. 15 min).

temas.tema: Getting Around London — Black Cabs, Buses, and Asking for Help When the GPS Fails
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: past continuous + past simple (narrative — consolidation) + will for instant decisions in transport; NEW vocabulary: black cab, double-decker bus, bus stop, route number, fare, congestion, pedestrian crossing, cab rank, night bus, transfer; Chunks: 'Could you take me to…?', 'How long will it take?', 'I'll just take a cab — it's easier.', 'The bus was full, so I decided to walk.'; Expression: 'Better safe than sorry.' (review — applied to transport choices); Collocation: 'hail a cab', 'check the route', 'miss the bus'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher presents a 'London transport emergency' scenario card: 'Your phone died. You don't know the bus route. It's raining.' Roberto brainstorms aloud what he would do — eliciting modal verbs, will for decisions, and known chunks. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new transport vocabulary items with a London bus map as visual; models the past continuous + past simple transport narrative ('I was waiting at the bus stop when a black cab appeared — I'll take it!'). Practice (25 min): Role-play carousel — 3 mini-scenarios: (1) Roberto hails a cab and negotiates the route; (2) Roberto asks a stranger which bus goes to the hotel; (3) Roberto explains to the hotel concierge why he was late (past continuous narrative). Teacher plays all 3 counterparts.
