Production (20 min): Roberto narrates the story of 'the day everything went wrong with transport in London' — a 2-minute monologue using past simple + past continuous + will for decisions, plus at least 5 new vocabulary items. Debrief (10 min): Quick vocabulary retrieval — teacher mimes transport actions; Roberto produces the word + a sentence.
temas.homework: Write an 8-10 sentence narrative about a fictional transport problem in London — include past simple, past continuous, and will for instant decisions. (Est. 20 min). Listen to a 2-minute podcast excerpt about London transport (teacher provides link) and note 3 new words. (Est. 15 min).

temas.tema: London Shopping and Souvenirs — Oxford Street, Haggling Etiquette, and Handling Currency Confusion
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: will for instant decisions + comparatives (Building consolidation) in shopping context; NEW vocabulary: department store, receipt, exchange rate, price tag, fitting room, discount, refund policy, souvenir shop, cash only, contactless payment; Chunks: 'Do you have this in a different size?', 'Can I get a refund if it doesn't fit?', 'I'll take the blue one — it's more practical.'; Expression: 'It's a piece of cake.' (review — used when a shopping transaction goes smoothly: 'Paying with my card was a piece of cake.'); Collocation: 'try on', 'pay by card', 'ask for a receipt'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places 5 printed price tags with British pounds on the table — Roberto converts to Brazilian reais mentally and reacts in English ('That's expensive!', 'That's a good deal.', 'I'll think about it.'). Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new shopping vocabulary items with images of Oxford Street stores; drills will for instant decisions in a rapid-fire game ('The blue bag costs £20 and the red one costs £15 — which do you buy?' — Roberto responds instantly with 'I'll take…'). Practice (25 min): Oxford Street shopping simulation — Roberto shops in 3 different stores (teacher plays 3 different shop assistants): (1) clothing store where an item doesn't fit; (2) souvenir shop where he buys gifts for family; (3) electronics store where he checks return policy. Each interaction requires at least 2 new vocabulary items and one 'instant will decision'. Production (20 min): Roberto gives a 2-minute spoken 'shopping report' — narrates what he bought, why, how much he spent, and what he's going to do with each item. Uses past simple for completed actions and 'I'm going to give this to…' for plans. Debrief (10 min): Teacher reformulates 3 error-laden sentences from production phase; Roberto identifies which grammar rule applies.
temas.homework: Record a 90-second audio as Roberto Pires describing 3 souvenirs you bought in London — what they are, who they're for, and how much they cost. Use 'will' for at least one decision and comparatives for at least one comparison. (Est. 15 min). Write a 6-sentence simulated conversation between Roberto and a shop assistant handling a return or exchange. (Est. 20 min).

temas.tema: London Cultural Etiquette — Queueing, Polite Disagreement, and What NOT to Say to a British Person
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: modal verbs full set (should/could/might) for advice and cultural guidance + 'I agree / I disagree' expressions; NEW vocabulary: queue, etiquette, polite disagreement, understatement, sarcasm (awareness), apologize, reserved, formal, informal register, body language; Chunks: 'You should probably queue here.', 'I might be wrong, but…', 'I'm not sure I agree — could it be that…?'; Expression: 'In my opinion…' (review — applied to cultural observations); 'I agree / I disagree' (A2 expression — first formal use in program); Collocation: 'jump the queue', 'keep a straight face', 'mind your manners'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher presents 5 cultural 'would you do this in England?' scenarios (cutting the queue, speaking loudly on the Tube, asking a stranger's salary) — Roberto reacts using 'should/shouldn't', building instinct for British etiquette in English. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new cultural etiquette vocabulary items; introduces the concept of understatement in British English ('not bad' = excellent) as cultural awareness; drills polite disagreement chunks using a back-and-forth 'agree/disagree' card game. Practice (25 min): Structured debate simulation — teacher makes 5 'controversial' statements about travel etiquette (e.g., 'Tourists should always tip in London.'); Roberto practices agreeing/disagreeing politely using the target chunks; focus on tone and body language as much as language. Second activity: Roberto gives cultural advice to an imaginary Brazilian friend visiting London — uses 'you should', 'you might want to', 'it's better to'. Production (20 min): Roberto prepares and delivers a 2-minute 'cultural briefing' for a Brazilian colleague about London etiquette — structured as 3 tips with reasons. Teacher evaluates: register, modal use, chunk integration. Debrief (10 min): Reflective discussion — how does British etiquette compare to Spanish and French? Roberto produces comparison sentences.
temas.homework: Write a 'London Etiquette Guide for Brazilians' — 5 tips in English using modal verbs (should/could/might) for each tip. (Est. 20 min). Record a 60-second audio giving your most important cultural tip for London — speak naturally without reading from notes. (Est. 10 min).

temas.tema: Handling Problems in London — Lost Phone, Medical Appointment, and Staying Calm Under Real Pressure
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: modal verbs (should/could/might) + past simple for problem narration; NEW vocabulary: pharmacy, prescription, urgent care, lost and found, report (verb), insurance, symptom, reception desk, replacement, compensation; Chunks: 'I think I might have left it on the Tube.', 'Could you tell me where the nearest pharmacy is?', 'I should have been more careful.'; Expression: 'Better safe than sorry.' (review — now in health/safety context); Collocation: 'report a loss', 'fill out a form', 'seek medical attention'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher presents 3 'London emergency' scenario cards — Roberto votes on which he finds most stressful; class discusses what he would do first in each, activating known language around problems and emergencies (from Aula 18). Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new emergency/problem vocabulary items via scenario images; models 'I should have…' as a new production chunk for regret/reflection; drills the difference between 'I lost my phone' (past simple) and 'I was looking for my phone when I realized…' (past continuous + past simple). Practice (25 min): Emergency role-play sequence — 3 stations: (1) Roberto reports a lost phone at a Transport for London office (teacher = clerk); (2) Roberto asks a pharmacist for help with a headache and needs to explain symptoms; (3) Roberto calls his hotel from a borrowed phone to cancel dinner plans, explaining the situation. Each scenario requires at least 3 new vocabulary items. Production (20 min): Roberto narrates 'The Worst Morning in London' — a 2-3 minute monologue combining past simple, past continuous, modal verbs, and problem vocabulary. Teacher evaluates communication success over grammatical perfection. Debrief (10 min): Roberto identifies which scenario felt hardest and why — teacher provides targeted reformulation and confidence-building feedback.
temas.homework: Write a 10-sentence insurance claim narrative as Roberto Pires reporting a lost phone in London — include: when it happened, what you were doing, what you did next, and what you should have done differently. (Est. 20 min). Record a 90-second audio calling a hotel to report a problem — speak naturally, don't read a script. (Est. 15 min).

temas.tema: From London to Rome — International Connections, Flight Delays, and Roberto's Italian Arrival Story
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: past continuous + past simple (Expansion — full narrative integration) + quantifiers review in travel context; NEW vocabulary: connecting flight, transit lounge, gate change, boarding pass, runway, turbulence, customs declaration, passport control, time zone, jet lag; Chunks: 'My flight was delayed by two hours.', 'While I was waiting, they changed the gate.', 'There weren't many passengers at that hour.'; Expression: 'In the nick of time.' (review — used in flight narrative: 'I made it to the gate in the nick of time.'); Collocation: 'make a connection', 'clear customs', 'claim your baggage'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher presents a chaotic airport departure board with multiple delays and gate changes — Roberto narrates what is happening using present continuous and what happened using past simple, connecting both naturally. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new flight/transit vocabulary items via a realistic boarding pass and departure board visual; builds a timeline on the board showing a delayed London-Rome flight narrative; models how past continuous and past simple create dramatic storytelling in travel contexts. Practice (25 min): Narrative building exercise — teacher provides 6 illustrated timeline cards of a chaotic travel day; Roberto arranges them and narrates the full story using target grammar; then Roberto and teacher perform a dialogue where Roberto explains to the Rome hotel why he arrived late (using past continuous + past simple naturally). Production (20 min): Roberto performs a 'travel confession' monologue — tells the story of the most chaotic travel leg of the trip so far, incorporating at least 6 new vocabulary items and 4 instances of the grammar target. Debrief (10 min): Roberto listens back to his production (teacher's notes) and self-corrects 2 errors with teacher guidance.
temas.homework: Write a 10-sentence narrative about a delayed flight experience (real or imagined) as Roberto Pires — use past continuous + past simple for at least 4 sentences. (Est. 20 min). Listen to an airport announcement simulation on YouTube (teacher provides keywords); write down the gate number, destination, and reason for delay that you hear. (Est. 10 min).

temas.tema: Rome Arrivals — Fiumicino Airport, Leonardo Express, and Roberto's First Roman Hotel
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: going to + will contrast (Building review — new Roman context) + present continuous for live updates; NEW vocabulary: express train, terminal, shuttle bus, central station, tourist information, key card, elevator, luggage storage, concierge, city map; Chunks: 'I'm going to take the express train — it's faster.', 'I'll ask at the information desk.', 'I'm staying at a hotel near the Colosseum.'; Expression: 'I'm looking forward to…' (review — Roberto's excitement for Rome: 'I'm really looking forward to seeing the Colosseum.'); Collocation: 'store your luggage', 'pick up a map', 'ask for directions'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher shows a photo of Rome's Fiumicino Airport and asks: 'Roberto, you just landed. It's 8pm. Your hotel is near the Colosseum. What are you going to do?' — Roberto plans his arrival using going to + will contrast spontaneously. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new vocabulary items using a real map of central Rome and the Fiumicino Airport layout; models going to vs. will with specific Roman scenarios ('I'm going to book the express train in advance' vs. 'The shuttle is full — I'll find a taxi'). Practice (25 min): Rome arrival simulation — Roberto navigates from landing to hotel in 3 sequential role-plays: (1) at the information desk asking about transport options; (2) at the train ticket machine (teacher plays an Italian stranger who speaks limited English); (3) hotel check-in — Roberto mentions plans for the next morning using going to. Production (20 min): Roberto narrates his 'Roman arrival plan' — what he's going to do tonight, tomorrow morning, and tomorrow afternoon, using going to for plans and will for spontaneous decisions. At least 6 new vocabulary items included. Debrief (10 min): Quick map challenge — teacher points to places on a Rome map; Roberto says how he'd get there from the hotel.
temas.homework: Write Roberto Pires's Rome itinerary for Day 1 — 8 sentences using 'going to' for planned activities and 'will' for at least 2 spontaneous decisions. (Est. 20 min). Record a 90-second audio telling a friend what you're looking forward to in Rome — use 'I'm looking forward to', going to, and 3 new vocabulary items. (Est. 15 min).

temas.tema: Roman Restaurants and Trattorie — Ordering Italian Food in English, Wine Culture, and La Dolce Vita
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: verb + -ing vs. verb + to (Expansion consolidation) + going to for meal plans; NEW vocabulary: trattoria, antipasto, primo, secondo, dolce, house wine, sparkling water, cover charge, prix fixe, sommelier; Chunks: 'I'd like to start with the antipasto.', 'I enjoy trying local wine.', 'We're going to share the dessert.', 'What do you recommend for the main course?'; Expression: 'I would rather…' (review — food preference: 'I would rather have the fish than the pasta.'); Collocation: 'pair with wine', 'save room for dessert', 'ask for the wine list'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places a printed mock Italian trattoria menu (in English) on the table — Roberto reads and marks what he'd order for a full 4-course Italian meal; teacher elicits reactions, comparing Italian and French menu structure (review from Aula 16). Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new restaurant vocabulary items with images of iconic Roman dishes; drills verb + -ing / verb + to in food preference context — 'I enjoy eating pasta', 'I want to try the tiramisu', 'I need to avoid spicy food'. Students categorize verbs by which pattern they follow using a card sort. Practice (25 min): Full Roman trattoria simulation — Roberto and teacher perform a 3-course dinner: Roberto orders, asks for recommendations, handles a substitution request, and asks about the wine list; teacher introduces a complication (the sommelier recommends an expensive wine — Roberto politely declines and chooses differently). Second round: Roberto is hosting a fictional Italian business contact — he orders for both and makes recommendations. Production (20 min): Roberto narrates 'My perfect Roman dinner' — 2-minute spoken description of the ideal meal, using going to, verb + -ing / verb + to, and 6 new vocabulary items. Debrief (10 min): Roberto compares Rome, Paris, Barcelona, and London dining using superlatives and personal preferences.
temas.homework: Write a 10-sentence restaurant review of a fictional Roman trattoria as Roberto Pires — include what you ordered, what you enjoyed, what you would recommend, and what you would rather avoid. Use verb + -ing / verb + to at least 3 times. (Est. 20 min). Record a 60-second audio ordering a full Italian dinner — speak naturally without reading. (Est. 10 min).

temas.tema: Rome Sightseeing — The Colosseum, Vatican, and Telling Stories About Historic Places
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: past continuous + past simple (Expansion — storytelling peak) + superlatives for emotional impact; NEW vocabulary: ancient, ruins, monument, guided tour, audio guide, chapel, fresco, sculpture, crowd, restoration; Chunks: 'I was walking through the ruins when I realized how old this place is.', 'It's the most breathtaking thing I've ever seen.', 'There were thousands of people, but it was still impressive.'; Expression: 'Once in a while, you see something that changes the way you think.' (extended use of 'once in a while'); Collocation: 'take in the view', 'stand in awe', 'capture the moment'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher shows 5 photos of Rome's major sites without names — Roberto identifies what he knows, describes each using present continuous ('I can see...', 'There are...'), and guesses historical significance. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new sightseeing/history vocabulary items using Colosseum and Vatican images; models emotional storytelling with past continuous + past simple: 'I was climbing the steps when I first saw the Colosseum — it was the most impressive moment of the trip.' Roberto identifies the grammar structure and emotional effect. Practice (25 min): 'Roman storytelling' activity — teacher gives Roberto 4 illustrated scene cards from a Colosseum visit; Roberto arranges them and narrates a full story, naturally alternating past simple and past continuous; teacher interrupts with 'tourist questions' that Roberto must answer in real time. Second activity: Roberto describes a Vatican chapel fresco (photo) using superlatives and emotional vocabulary. Production (20 min): Roberto delivers a 3-minute spoken travel narrative about his 'most memorable moment' in Rome — must include: past continuous + past simple at least 4 times, 2 superlatives, and 5 new vocabulary items. Recorded for portfolio. Debrief (10 min): Teacher compares Roberto's current storytelling with his Aula 1 baseline — names 3 specific improvements. Roberto identifies 1 area he wants to strengthen.
temas.homework: Write a 12-sentence travel story about visiting the Colosseum as Roberto Pires — use past continuous + past simple for the narrative structure, at least 2 superlatives, and 6 new vocabulary items. (Est. 25 min). Record a 2-minute audio narrating your most impressive moment in Rome — don't read from notes. (Est. 15 min).

temas.tema: Solving Real Problems in Rome — Overbooked Tours, Missed Connections, and Keeping Your Cool in Italian
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: modal verbs full set (should/could/might) + past simple for problem resolution narration; NEW vocabulary: overbooked, alternative, refund, complaint, supervisor, reschedule, priority, voucher, inconvenience, resolve; Chunks: 'I'd like to speak to the manager, please.', 'Could you offer an alternative?', 'I should have booked in advance, but…', 'This might take some time to resolve.'; Expression: 'It depends on…' (review — applied to problem-solving: 'It depends on whether they have availability tomorrow.'); Collocation: 'file a complaint', 'escalate the issue', 'offer a solution'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher presents 3 'Rome problems' scenario cards (overbooked Vatican tour, taxi overcharges, hotel double-books a room) — Roberto votes on most stressful, class discusses which language tools from the program would help most in each situation. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new problem/complaint vocabulary items via scenario visuals; models the 'polite complaint' language arc: acknowledge → explain → request → negotiate → resolve; drills 'should have + past participle' as a new production pattern for regret: 'I should have booked earlier.' Practice (25 min): Problem-solving role-play sequence — 3 stations: (1) Roberto arrives at a Vatican tour — he's not on the list; (2) Roberto disputes an overcharge with a taxi driver; (3) Roberto speaks to a hotel supervisor about a room double-booking. Each scenario has a 'resolution card' — Roberto must reach it using polite, clear English within 3 minutes. Production (20 min): Roberto narrates 'The Day Everything Went Wrong in Rome' — a 3-minute spoken story using past simple (what happened), past continuous (what was happening around him), and modal verbs (what he should/could/might have done). Debrief (10 min): Class reviews: which problem was hardest to handle linguistically? Teacher provides 3 reformulated sentences for internalization.
temas.homework: Write a formal complaint email (10-12 sentences) from Roberto Pires to a Rome tour company — describe the problem, explain the impact, request a solution. Use modal verbs, past simple, and 4 new vocabulary items. (Est. 25 min). Record a 90-second audio practicing the complaint conversation as if calling the tour company directly. (Est. 15 min).

temas.tema: Roberto's Grand Tour Wrap-Up — Telling the Full Story of 5 Cities in 5 Minutes
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: integration of ALL structures (past simple, past continuous, going to, will, modals, comparatives, superlatives, quantifiers, verb patterns); Discourse markers: 'first', 'then', 'after that', 'while', 'finally', 'by the time'; NEW vocabulary: highlight, unforgettable, challenging, rewarding, itinerary, cultural immersion, confidence, milestone, perspective, recommendation; Chunks: 'The highlight of the trip was…', 'Looking back, I should have…', 'I'm planning to go back to…'; Expression: 'I used to be afraid of speaking English — but not anymore.' (A2 expression 'I used to' — first meaningful use in program, emotionally loaded)
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher draws a blank map of Europe on the board — Roberto labels Barcelona, Paris, Amsterdam, London, and Rome from memory and says one word that describes each city; builds anticipation for the full narrative task. Presentation (15 min): Teacher introduces discourse markers for storytelling ('first', 'while', 'after that', 'by the time', 'finally') using a printed guide; models how to stitch 5 city experiences into one coherent narrative — Roberto identifies markers in the model text and categorizes them. Practice (25 min): Structured narrative building — Roberto receives 10 'event cards' (one per major city experience); arranges them on the table, then narrates the full story using discourse markers; teacher interrupts with follow-up questions to practice spontaneous response. Second task: Roberto identifies his 3 best and 3 hardest moments of the 'trip' and explains why using full sentences. Production (20 min): Roberto delivers an uninterrupted 5-minute spoken narrative of his full European Grand Tour — from Barcelona to Rome; teacher notes fluency, grammar range, vocabulary density, and natural discourse flow. This is Roberto's most ambitious speaking task to date. Recorded for portfolio. Debrief (10 min): Teacher celebrates specific growth moments with evidence from the recording; identifies 2 stretch goals for the final 2 aulas.
temas.homework: Write a 15-sentence 'Grand Tour Summary' as Roberto Pires — cover all 5 cities, use at least 6 different grammar structures from the program, 3 discourse markers, and the expression 'I used to'. (Est. 30 min). Record a 3-minute 'travel podcast episode' audio telling a Brazilian audience about your favorite European city and why. Don't read — tell the story naturally. (Est. 20 min).

temas.tema: Fluency Under Pressure — Simulated Real Situations Roberto Will Face in August (Full Role-Play Simulation)
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar: ALL structures in unpredictable combination; Focus: fluency, recovery strategies, and communication success over perfection; NEW vocabulary (meta-communication): 'Could you repeat that?', 'I didn't catch that.', 'Sorry, could you speak more slowly?', 'What I meant to say was…', 'Let me think for a second.'; Chunks (fluency fillers): 'Actually…', 'You know what I mean?', 'The thing is…', 'Something like that.'; Expression: 'It's a piece of cake.' (review — Roberto uses it to describe situations that used to be hard: 'Ordering food in English? It's a piece of cake now.').
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher introduces the 'communication toolbox' — 5 recovery strategies for when Roberto gets stuck in real English situations; drills each one 3 times with exaggerated scenarios until they feel automatic. Presentation (10 min): Teacher explains the format of the 'August Simulation' — 6 rapid-fire real scenarios Roberto will actually face during his trip; Roberto receives no script, only the situation; evaluation is on communication success, not grammar. Simulation (40 min): 6 back-to-back role-play scenarios — teacher plays different characters: (1) Barcelona check-in agent who doesn't understand Roberto's accent; (2) Parisian waiter who speaks limited English; (3) Amsterdam tram passenger giving confusing directions; (4) London pharmacist asking about Roberto's symptoms; (5) Rome tour guide who announces a last-minute change; (6) Airport staff at an overbooked gate. Roberto handles each with 3-4 minute interaction time, using all tools from the program. Production/Debrief (30 min): Teacher plays back 3 moments from the simulation (audio notes) — Roberto self-evaluates using 'What went well', 'What I'd do differently', 'What I need to practice before August'. Teacher provides specific praise + 2 targeted improvement micro-tasks. Closing: Roberto rates his readiness for the real trip: 1-10 with justification.
temas.homework: Record a 3-minute audio simulating one of the 6 August scenarios from class — choose the one that felt hardest; perform it again with improvements. (Est. 20 min). Write 10 sentences: 5 things Roberto can now do in English that he couldn't before, and 5 situations he still wants to feel more confident in before the trip. (Est. 20 min).

temas.tema: Block 2 Closing — Roberto's Language Portfolio, August Readiness Score, and Preview of the Final Sprint (Aulas 41–48)
temas.focoLinguistico: Full grammar and vocabulary integration review across all Block 2 content: modal verbs, past continuous, verb patterns, discourse markers, quantifiers; Self-assessment language: 'I've become more confident in…', 'I can now handle…', 'I still need to work on…', 'Before this program, I used to…'; Reflection chunks: 'The most useful thing I learned was…', 'I'm ready to…', 'I'm looking forward to August because…'; Final expression activation: 'I used to be afraid — but now I know I can handle it.'
temas.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up — Language Inventory Game (15 min): Teacher calls out a travel situation ('You're at a Paris restaurant and the waiter only speaks French') — Roberto responds with 3 English sentences using the best tools he has; repeat for 5 situations spanning all Block 2 cities. This activates the full vocabulary and grammar range in a low-pressure format. Portfolio Review (20 min): Teacher presents Roberto with a printed 'language portfolio' — a visual map of all grammar structures, vocabulary clusters, expressions, and chunks covered in Aulas 21-40; Roberto marks items as 'solid', 'needs practice', or 'still uncertain' using colored stickers. Gap Discussion (15 min): Teacher and Roberto identify 3-4 areas to prioritize in the final sprint (Aulas 41-48) — framed as final preparation for August, not as deficiencies. Preview of Final Sprint (15 min): Teacher unveils Aulas 41-48 themes — practical business conversation, full trip simulation, emotional language, and the culminating role-play; Roberto expresses what he most wants to practice. Confidence Ceremony (15 min): Roberto delivers a 2-minute unscripted 'I am ready for August' speech — no prompts, no script, no teacher interruption. This is a milestone moment. Teacher affirms 5 specific improvements with evidence. Roberto signs his 'August Readiness Commitment Card' — a personal pledge to use English from Day 1 of the trip.
temas.homework: Write Roberto Pires's 'English Passport' — a one-page document (in English) summarizing: who you are, where you're going, what you can now do in English, and what you're most excited about for August. Use at least 8 grammar structures from the program. (Est. 30 min). Record a final 3-minute 'Block 2 Closing Statement' audio — tell your future self in August what you learned and why you're ready. Keep this recording to listen to during the trip. (Est. 20 min).

temas.tema: Roberto's European Diary — Telling the Full Story with Confidence and Flow
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion — full integration): past simple + past continuous in extended narrative ('I was walking through the Boqueria when I saw…', 'We arrived late, but the hotel was still open.'); connectives for storytelling: first, then, after that, suddenly, in the end; Review vocabulary: all 5 cities (Barcelona, Paris, Amsterdam, London, Rome) key words reactivated as production tools — NOT new content; Expressions: 'It was absolutely worth it.', 'I couldn't believe my eyes.', 'The best part was…'; Chunk: 'Looking back, I think…' for reflection; Idiomatic: 'once in a while' (A2 expression — review + production); Target: Roberto produces a 10-sentence connected narrative about one European moment using past simple + past continuous + at least 3 connectives without switching to Portuguese.
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — Warm-up (10 min): Teacher spreads 10 city photo cards (Sagrada Família, Eiffel Tower, Colosseum, etc.) on the table face down. Roberto picks one randomly and must say 2 sentences about it using past tense — no preparation. This activates retrieval and reveals gaps. Phase 2 — Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents a model 'European diary entry' on a printed card — 8 sentences combining past simple and past continuous with connectives highlighted in color. Roberto reads aloud, teacher marks natural stress and pauses. Phase 3 — Guided Practice (20 min): Roberto uses a structured 'story skeleton' card (First… / I was… when… / Then… / After that… / In the end…) to reconstruct one of his favorite moments from the simulated Grand Tour (Aula 38 reference). Teacher gives corrective feedback on verb forms only — not pronunciation. Phase 4 — Production (25 min): Roberto tells his full European story to the teacher as if speaking to a Brazilian friend who has never traveled to Europe. Teacher plays the curious friend, asking natural follow-up questions ('Wait — what happened next?', 'How did you feel?'). Roberto must sustain the narrative for at least 3 minutes. Final product: a 3-minute spoken story recorded on the teacher's phone for Roberto's progress portfolio. Phase 5 — Debrief (10 min): Teacher replays 30 seconds of the recording. Roberto identifies: one thing he did well, one verb form to fix. Teacher highlights the most fluent moment as positive reinforcement.
temas.homework: Task 1 (20 min): Write your European diary — a 10-sentence paragraph about one real or imagined moment from the trip (airport, restaurant, museum, hotel). Use: past simple, past continuous, and at least 4 connectives (first, then, after that, suddenly, in the end). Handwrite it in your notebook. Task 2 (10 min): Record a 2-minute audio of the same story — speak naturally, as if telling a friend. Do NOT read your written version. Send the audio to the teacher. Task 3 (5 min): Look up 2 photos of the place you wrote about and bring them to Aula 42 to use as a speaking prompt.

temas.tema: Roberto Talks Business — Networking in English at European Conferences and Airport Lounges
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion consolidation): present simple for professional identity ('I run a company in São Paulo.', 'I work in the business sector.', 'I travel to Europe for…') + going to + will for professional plans ('I'm going to meet a client in London next year.', 'I'll send you my contact.'); NEW vocabulary (professional travel context, 40% foco): lounge, networking, contact, deal, schedule, partner, headquarters, market, opportunity, conference; NEW expressions: 'Nice to meet you — what brings you here?', 'Here's my card.', 'We should stay in touch.'; Idiomatic: 'It's a small world.' (transparent A2); Chunk: 'I'm in the business of…'; Target: Roberto introduces himself professionally in English, maintains a 2-minute small-talk exchange, and closes with a natural farewell — all without Portuguese.
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — Warm-up (10 min): Teacher sets the scene: 'Imagine you're in the business lounge at Heathrow, waiting for your flight back to São Paulo. A British businessman sits next to you.' Teacher plays the businessman and opens with: 'Long day?' Roberto must respond naturally. No preparation — this is a diagnostic for business register. Phase 2 — Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents a 'Business Small Talk Map' on a printed card — 4 conversation moves: (1) Opening (comment on situation), (2) Self-introduction (name + company + city), (3) Common ground (travel, destination, industry), (4) Closing (exchange contacts, farewell). Each move has 2 model sentences. Roberto reads and repeats with natural intonation. Phase 3 — Vocabulary Drilling (10 min): Flashcard game — teacher holds up business vocabulary flashcards (lounge, schedule, headquarters, partner, opportunity). Roberto says the word, uses it in a sentence, and rates his confidence (1–3). Cards rated 1 go back in the pile for re-drilling. Phase 4 — Guided Role-Play (20 min): Roberto and teacher do a structured airport lounge role-play. Teacher is an American tech entrepreneur; Roberto is himself. Script skeleton provided: Roberto must hit all 4 conversation moves. Teacher interrupts with realistic follow-up questions ('Oh, São Paulo — do you travel to the US often?'). Two rounds: first with skeleton card, second without. Phase 5 — Production (15 min): Roberto does a final 'cold' role-play — teacher is now a French hotel manager met at a Paris networking dinner. Roberto must adapt the same 4 moves to a new context. Final product: Roberto completes the exchange and writes the 'businessman's' email address and name as a closing action (physical notecard). Phase 6 — Debrief (10 min): Teacher highlights the most natural moment. Roberto writes 3 sentences he wants to remember in his notebook.
temas.homework: Task 1 (15 min): Write a 6-sentence LinkedIn-style introduction as if Roberto Pires is presenting himself to an international audience. Use: present simple, going to, and at least 2 new vocabulary words (lounge, market, opportunity, partner, etc.). Task 2 (10 min): Record a 90-second audio — 'You are at an airport lounge in Rome. Introduce yourself to the person next to you.' Speak naturally. No script. Send to teacher. Task 3 (5 min): Find and screenshot one real LinkedIn post or professional introduction in English and bring it to Aula 43.

temas.tema: Smooth Operator — Handling Every Unexpected Problem in Europe Without Freezing
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion — consolidation): modal verbs full set in problem-solving context — should (advice), could (option), might (uncertainty), must (necessity): 'You should call the airline.', 'We could take a different route.', 'The train might be delayed.', 'I must find my passport.'; past simple for problem narration: 'I lost my bag at the Gare du Nord.', 'The hotel gave me the wrong room.'; NEW vocabulary (problem + solution, 40% foco): delay, cancel, replace, complaint, refund, insurance, emergency, receipt, report, alternative; Expressions (review + production): 'I need help, please.', 'Can you explain that again?', 'What are my options?'; NEW expression: 'Is there anything you can do?'; Idiomatic: 'stay calm' used as a fixed chunk; Target: Roberto narrates a problem, uses modal verbs to explore solutions, and closes with a resolution — all in connected English under simulated pressure.
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — Warm-up: 'Problem Roulette' (10 min): Teacher has a deck of 8 'problem cards' (flight cancelled, hotel overbooked, wallet stolen, lost metro card, wrong food order, sick on travel day, language misunderstanding, overcharged at restaurant). Roberto picks a card and has 30 seconds to say what he would do — in English only. No judgment — teacher tracks which problems trigger code-switching. Phase 2 — Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents a 'Problem → Option → Solution' model on a printed card using one scenario (lost baggage at CDG airport). Three sentences per stage, each using a modal verb highlighted in bold. Roberto reads, identifies the modal, and explains why it was used (advice / option / uncertainty). Phase 3 — Controlled Practice (15 min): Gap-fill dialogue — teacher gives Roberto a printed conversation between a traveler and an airline desk agent. 8 blanks where modal verbs are missing. Roberto fills in alone, then reads the full dialogue aloud. Teacher corrects only modal verb errors. Phase 4 — Guided Role-Play (20 min): Two scenarios back to back. Scenario A: Roberto's hotel in Amsterdam gave away his room — he must negotiate with the receptionist (teacher) using modal verbs and problem vocabulary. Scenario B: Roberto was overcharged at a London restaurant — he must address the situation politely with the waiter (teacher). Roberto uses 'Is there anything you can do?' in each scenario. Phase 5 — Free Production (10 min): Roberto narrates — without prompts — the 'worst travel problem he can imagine' as a hypothetical past story. Teacher listens and counts: (1) modal verbs used, (2) past simple verbs used, (3) times he switched to Portuguese. Target: zero code-switches. Phase 6 — Debrief (10 min): Teacher gives Roberto a 'performance card' with 3 green (strength) and 1 red (focus) item. Roberto writes the red item in his notebook as his personal target for Aula 44.
temas.homework: Task 1 (15 min): Write a problem story — 8 sentences minimum. Choose any travel problem from the lesson. Use: past simple to describe what happened + modal verbs to describe what you could/should/might do. Task 2 (10 min): Record a 90-second audio — 'You are at a hotel reception in Paris. Your room is not ready and it is 11 PM. Explain the problem and ask for options.' Speak without notes. Send to teacher. Task 3 (5 min): Write 3 sentences using 'might' + 'should' + 'could' about something that could go wrong on your August trip — and what you would do.

temas.tema: Roberto's Language Toolkit — Fluency Strategies When You Don't Know the Word
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion — consolidation across all structures): NO new grammar introduced — this is a fluency and communication strategies lesson; Focus: communication strategies for A2 speakers — paraphrasing ('It's a kind of…', 'It's like a…', 'The thing you use to…'), asking for clarification ('Sorry, could you say that again?', 'What do you mean by…?', 'How do you spell that?'), buying time ('Let me think…', 'How do I say this…', 'Give me a second.'), recovery from a freeze ('Actually, what I mean is…', 'Sorry — let me start again.'); NEW vocabulary (meta-communication, 60% geral): paraphrase, clarify, repeat, explain, describe, mean, understand, similar, kind of, basically; Expressions (NEW): 'Bear with me.', 'Does that make sense?'; Idiomatic (review): 'It depends on…' used in strategy selection; Target: Roberto completes a 5-minute conversation without freezing — using at least 3 communication strategies when blocked, instead of switching to Portuguese.
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — Warm-up: 'The Forbidden Word' (10 min): Teacher says a word in English (e.g., 'passport'). Roberto must explain it WITHOUT using the word, in English. 5 rounds. Vocabulary: items from the full program (metro, receipt, lounge, reservation, customs). This activates paraphrasing instinctively before it's taught formally. Phase 2 — Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents the 'Fluency Toolkit' — a laminated A5 card with 4 strategy categories: (1) Paraphrase, (2) Ask for clarification, (3) Buy time, (4) Recover from a freeze. Each has 2 model phrases. Roberto reads each phrase aloud with natural rhythm. Teacher models exaggerated intonation for 'Bear with me' and 'Let me think' — showing these are NORMAL, not embarrassing. Phase 3 — Drilling (10 min): Teacher deliberately says something too fast or complex. Roberto must use a clarification strategy — no guessing, no nodding and pretending. 6 rounds with different speeds and accents (teacher simulates mild British, American, non-native English). Phase 4 — Paraphrase Challenge (15 min): Teacher shows Roberto 8 flashcards with words he does NOT know (deliberate advanced vocabulary). Roberto must describe each one in simple English using 'It's a kind of…' or 'It's like a… but…'. Teacher counts successful paraphrases. Target: 6/8. Phase 5 — Free Conversation with Strategy Tracking (20 min): Teacher and Roberto have a free 5-minute conversation about Roberto's August trip plans — teacher deliberately uses unfamiliar words and speaks at near-natural pace. Roberto uses the Toolkit card. After the conversation, teacher replays the moments where Roberto froze and they identify together: did he use a strategy? Which one would have worked? Phase 6 — Debrief (10 min): Roberto writes in his notebook: 'My 3 favorite strategies from today.' He also selects his 'emergency phrase' — the one sentence he will use first if he freezes in Europe.
temas.homework: Task 1 (15 min): Watch a 3-minute English video (YouTube — travel vlog recommended: Mark Wiens or Lost LeBlancs). When you don't understand a word, pause and write your best GUESS of what it means based on context. List 5 words + your guesses. Task 2 (10 min): Record a 2-minute audio — describe your apartment or office in São Paulo WITHOUT using any Portuguese words. If you don't know a word, use a strategy from the Toolkit. Send to teacher. Task 3 (5 min): Practice saying these 3 phrases aloud 5 times each until they feel automatic: 'Bear with me.', 'What do you mean by that?', 'It's kind of like a…'

temas.tema: Grand Finale Preparation Part 1 — Roberto Practices Every City, Every Situation
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Full integration — all A2 structures): past simple + past continuous (narrative), present continuous (live description), going to + will (plans + instant decisions), modal verbs (should/could/might), comparatives + superlatives, quantifiers, verb + -ing vs verb + to — ALL in natural connected speech across travel scenarios; Vocabulary review (active retrieval — NOT new): airport (check-in, customs, gate, baggage claim), hotel (check-in, upgrade, complaint), restaurant (order, recommend, bill, tip), transport (metro, taxi, directions), shopping (price, size, refund), emergencies (delay, cancel, lost, insurance); Expressions (review + production): 'I'm looking forward to…', 'I would rather…', 'I used to…', 'In my opinion…', 'It depends on…'; Target: Roberto moves fluidly between 5 city contexts in a 20-minute simulation, using appropriate vocabulary and grammar for each WITHOUT teacher prompting grammar structures.
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — Warm-up: 'City Roulette' (10 min): 5 city name cards face down on the table. Roberto picks one, teacher says a situation (e.g., 'You're ordering dinner.') and Roberto has 30 seconds to produce 3 sentences. 5 rounds — one per city. This is diagnostic AND motivational — Roberto sees how much he remembers. Phase 2 — The 5-City Carousel (40 min): Five structured role-play stations, 7 minutes each. Station 1 — Barcelona: Roberto orders tapas and asks for the bill in a busy restaurant. Station 2 — Paris: Roberto asks for directions to the Louvre from a hotel concierge and describes what he sees when he arrives. Station 3 — Amsterdam: Roberto checks in to a canal hotel and requests a room change because of noise. Station 4 — London: Roberto is in a pub, makes small talk with a British local, and navigates the menu. Station 5 — Rome: Roberto's tour of the Colosseum was cancelled — he negotiates a refund and books an alternative. Teacher plays all characters. After each station, 30-second micro-debrief: one strength, one fix. Phase 3 — Cross-City Narrative (20 min): Roberto tells the complete story of all 5 cities in sequence — as if recounting his trip to a Brazilian business partner who asked 'How was Europe?' Teacher does NOT interrupt — only listens and notes. Target: 5 minutes of connected speech. Phase 4 — Debrief + Preview (10 min): Teacher gives Roberto a 'Readiness Map' — a visual showing all 5 cities with a confidence score (1–3) for each, based on today's performance. Roberto identifies his weakest city and weakest situation for targeted practice in Aula 46.
temas.homework: Task 1 (20 min): Record a 4-minute audio — 'Tell the story of your European trip from Barcelona to Rome.' Use past simple, past continuous, and connectives. Speak without notes. This will be your BEFORE recording for the final celebration in Aula 48. Send to teacher. Task 2 (10 min): Review your notebook from Aulas 41–44. Write the 10 words and 3 expressions you feel LEAST confident about. Bring this list to Aula 46. Task 3 (5 min): Say your 'emergency phrase' from Aula 44 out loud before sleeping tonight and before Aula 46.

temas.tema: Grand Finale Preparation Part 2 — Closing the Gaps and Building Roberto's Confidence Peak
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Targeted review — student-driven): gaps identified in Aula 45 Readiness Map + Roberto's self-identified list from homework; likely focus areas: past continuous precision ('I was waiting' vs. 'I waited'), modal verb selection under pressure, comparatives in spontaneous speech; Vocabulary (targeted retrieval — student's weak list from homework): personalized per Roberto's self-assessment; Expressions (consolidation): all 8 A2 expressions activated in natural speech — 'It's a piece of cake.', 'Once in a while.', 'I'm looking forward to…', 'I would rather…', 'I used to…', 'It depends on…', 'In my opinion…', 'I agree / I disagree'; NEW idiomatic (A2 transparent): 'The more, the merrier.' — introduced as a bonus; Target: Roberto uses ALL 8 A2 expressions naturally in a 15-minute conversation without being cued — and demonstrates measurable improvement in his 2 weakest areas from Aula 45.
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — Personal Gap Clinic (20 min): Teacher reviews Roberto's homework recording from Aula 45 and his self-identified weak list. Together they identify the 3 most important language items to consolidate before Aula 47. Teacher designs a 10-minute micro-drill specifically for those items — e.g., if past continuous is weak, 5 rapid-fire sentence prompts; if modal verbs are shaky, a quick 'advice game' where Roberto advises a fictional traveler. This session is 100% responsive to Roberto's actual performance data. Phase 2 — Expression Marathon (20 min): Teacher places 8 expression cards face down. Roberto picks one, and teacher immediately creates a travel scenario where that expression fits naturally. Roberto must use the expression in context within 10 seconds. E.g., card: 'I would rather…' — teacher: 'The waiter offers you the window table or the bar seat. Which do you prefer?' Roberto: 'I would rather sit by the window, please.' All 8 expressions covered. Roberto rates each: green (automatic), yellow (needs practice), red (still insecure). Phase 3 — Pressure Simulation (20 min): Teacher runs two 10-minute unscripted role-plays in Roberto's identified weak cities/situations from Aula 45. Teacher plays difficult characters: one who speaks fast, one who is impatient. Roberto uses communication strategies from Aula 44 when blocked. After each, Roberto self-assesses: 'Did I freeze? Did I use a strategy? Did I switch to Portuguese?' Phase 4 — Confidence Builder (15 min): Teacher replays a 60-second clip of Roberto's most fluent moment from today's session. Roberto listens and identifies: vocabulary used, grammar structure, fluency level. Teacher says: 'This is what you sound like when you're in flow. Remember this feeling for August.' Roberto writes one sentence in his notebook: 'I am ready for Europe because…' Phase 5 — Preview of Aula 47–48 (5 min): Teacher explains the format of the final two sessions — full unscripted simulation (Aula 47) and celebration + portfolio review (Aula 48). Roberto knows what to expect.
temas.homework: Task 1 (15 min): Practice all 8 A2 expressions — write one original sentence for each using your real August travel plans as context. Example: 'I'm looking forward to eating paella in Barcelona.' Handwrite in notebook. Task 2 (10 min): Record a 90-second audio using your 3 personally weakest language items from today's gap clinic — deliberately put them in context. Send to teacher. Task 3 (10 min): Read your Aula 45 homework recording transcript (if teacher sends it) OR listen again and count: How many times did you use a connective? How many modal verbs? Write the numbers in your notebook. Target for Aula 47: beat those numbers.

temas.tema: The Full European Simulation — Roberto Lives His August Trip in 90 Minutes
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (ALL A2 structures — unscripted integration): past simple + past continuous, present continuous, going to + will, modals (should/could/might), comparatives + superlatives, quantifiers, verb + -ing vs verb + to — all deployed naturally in unpredictable, sequential real-world scenarios without grammar prompting; Vocabulary (full program — ALL active): airport, hotel, restaurant, transport, shopping, emergencies, business networking, city sightseeing — Roberto retrieves from memory, no word banks provided; Expressions (all 8 A2 + communication strategies from Aula 44): fully autonomous deployment; Target: Roberto completes a 60-minute uninterrupted simulated travel day — check-in to Rome dinner — in English only, with zero teacher grammar prompting, using at least 3 communication strategies when blocked, and producing at least 10 different grammar structures across the simulation.
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — Briefing (5 min): Teacher sets the scene on a printed 'Mission Card': 'Today, you are Roberto Pires. It is August 15th. You are flying from São Paulo to Barcelona. From there, you will travel to Paris, London, and Rome. I will play everyone you meet. You must handle everything in English. There is no Portuguese today.' Roberto receives NO vocabulary cards, NO grammar cards — only his Aula 44 Fluency Toolkit card as emergency resource. Phase 2 — The Full Simulation (60 min): 8 sequential scenarios, approximately 7 minutes each. Scenario 1 — GRU Airport: Check-in desk. Bag is 3kg overweight. Roberto negotiates with agent. Scenario 2 — Barcelona Hotel: Check-in. Room not ready. Roberto requests an upgrade and explains he has a dinner reservation in 2 hours. Scenario 3 — Barcelona Restaurant: Orders tapas for himself and a business partner. One dish is wrong — he addresses it politely. Scenario 4 — Paris: Roberto asks a local (teacher, non-native English accent) for directions to the Eiffel Tower. Gets confused by the answer. Uses clarification strategy. Scenario 5 — Paris Fine Dining: Formal dinner. Waiter recommends dishes. Roberto asks about ingredients, makes a choice, and handles the bill with a discrepancy. Scenario 6 — London Pub: Roberto meets a British entrepreneur. Small talk, professional introduction, exchange contacts. Scenario 7 — London Emergency: Roberto's phone is stolen on the Tube. He goes to the police station and files a report. Scenario 8 — Rome Airport: Flight home is delayed 4 hours. Roberto speaks with the airline desk, finds out options, decides what to do, and calls his assistant (teacher) to update plans. Phase 3 — Debrief and Performance Review (20 min): Teacher gives Roberto a printed 'Performance Summary Card' with 5 categories: Fluency, Grammar Range, Vocabulary, Communication Strategies, Confidence. Each rated 1–5. Teacher reviews: top 3 most impressive moments (with exact quotes), 2 areas for final polish. Roberto writes in his notebook: 'I am ready for Europe. Here is the proof:' followed by 3 specific things he did well today. Phase 4 — Emotional Anchoring (5 min): Teacher says: 'Roberto, on August 15th, this is what you will do. You've already done it today.' Roberto identifies his single strongest moment from the simulation.
temas.homework: Task 1 (15 min): Write a full 'August Travel Diary' — 10 sentences minimum telling the story of your simulated travel day from today's class. Use ALL grammar structures you can. This will be read aloud at Aula 48. Task 2 (10 min): Record your FINAL audio — 4 minutes, telling your European story from start to finish. This is your AFTER recording, to compare with your Aula 45 homework. Send to teacher before Aula 48. Task 3 (Reflection — 5 min): Answer in writing: 'What was the moment in today's class when I felt most confident? Why?' Bring this to Aula 48.

temas.tema: Roberto's Grand Finale — 48 Aulas, 5 Cities, and a Man Ready for Europe: Celebration, Portfolio Review, and Farewell
temas.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Full A2 inventory — celebratory review): ALL structures present as living proof of progress — Roberto uses them in authentic reflection and narrative, not as exercises; Vocabulary (complete program portfolio): active retrieval of the most meaningful words from the full 48-aula journey — no new content; Expressions (all 8 A2 — farewell production): 'I'm looking forward to…' (August trip), 'I used to…' (before the program), 'In my opinion…' (reflection on the experience), 'It was a piece of cake… actually, no, but now it is.'; NEW final expression (gift): 'I've come a long way.' — presented as a meaningful farewell chunk; Idiomatic farewell: 'Go for it, Roberto.' — teacher's closing gift phrase; Target: Roberto speaks for 5 uninterrupted minutes about his learning journey, uses the expression 'I've come a long way,' and leaves Aula 48 with a documented language portfolio and a clear confidence statement for August.
temas.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — The Before and After (15 min): Teacher plays Roberto's very first Aula 1 diagnostic recording (baseline) and then his Aula 47 homework recording (after). Both play out loud. Roberto listens to BOTH without interruption. Then teacher asks: 'What do you notice?' Roberto describes the difference in his own words — in English. This is the emotional anchor of the entire session — Roberto hears his own transformation. Phase 2 — The Language Portfolio Walk (20 min): Teacher presents Roberto's printed 'Language Portfolio' — a 2-page A4 document summarizing: all grammar structures mastered (with example sentences from his own recordings), vocabulary count across the program (approximate), expressions learned and used, communication strategies acquired, top 5 breakthrough moments identified by teacher from the full program. Roberto reads each section aloud. Teacher adds personal commentary for each. This is Roberto's trophy. Phase 3 — The Final Story (15 min): Roberto reads his 'August Travel Diary' written for homework — aloud, with expression, as if telling his family after returning from Europe. Teacher listens without correcting. At the end, teacher asks 3 authentic questions: 'Which city are you most excited about?', 'What will you do if something goes wrong?', 'What's one phrase you will definitely use in Barcelona?' Roberto answers naturally. Phase 4 — The Confidence Statement (10 min): Roberto writes — by hand — in the front page of his notebook: 'My name is Roberto Pires. I speak English. I am ready for Europe.' Then he reads it aloud to the teacher. Teacher witnesses it. They shake hands. Phase 5 — Celebration and Practical Farewell (15 min): Teacher gives Roberto a 'Europe Ready Kit' — a printed pocket card with: 5 emergency expressions (laminated), 8 A2 expressions, 3 communication strategies, and Roberto's personal 'emergency phrase' from Aula 44. Teacher reviews: 'Keep this in your bag. Pull it out if you freeze. But you won't need it.' Final 5 minutes: open conversation — Roberto talks about August, his family, the trip, whatever he wants — in English, freely, joyfully. Teacher's final words: 'Go for it, Roberto. You've come a long way.'
temas.homework: No formal homework — Roberto has graduated. Optional personal challenge: Before your August trip, record one final 2-minute audio: 'My name is Roberto Pires. I'm going to Europe. Here's what I'm going to do.' Keep it. Listen to it on the flight. And when you land in Barcelona — say 'Excuse me, can you help me?' to the first person you need. You already know how.

perfil._geracaoFoco: Travel English 100% aplicado — trilha de 48 aulas dividida em 13 fases cobrindo: (1) Survival Kit geral de viagem; (2) Aeroporto internacional; (3-7) Barcelona — hotel, restaurantes, tapas, cultura, transporte, compras; (8) Transição Barcelona→Paris; (9-12) Paris — cafés, restaurantes, cultura, transporte, etiqueta e segurança; (13) Integração final com role plays simulados. Proporção de habilidades: fala (speaking) e escuta (listening) prioritárias (~70%), leitura funcional de cardápios/mapas/placas (~20%), escrita mínima (~10%). Skills prioritários: chunks de sobrevivência, pedidos em restaurante, orientação espacial, interação com atendentes, etiqueta cultural (espanhola e francesa), gestão de imprevistos.

curriculo.tema: Diagnostic + First Words: Who Is Roberto Pires? — Self-Introduction and Travel Goals
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Diagnostic baseline: present simple (I am, I work, I live, I have); basic personal information chunks; travel vocabulary activation check (airport, hotel, restaurant, trip); expressions: 'My name is…', 'I'm from…', 'I work in…', 'I want to…', 'This August, I'm going to…'; vocabulary circulating: name, city, work, trip, travel, hotel, restaurant, airport, Spain, France (10 words — all diagnostic, not new content)
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Ice-breaker diagnostic: Teacher asks Roberto 10 casual questions in English (name, city, job, family, hobbies, travel plans) and maps spontaneous production: sentence length, code-switching moments, pronunciation comfort zones, and vocabulary gaps. No correction — pure observation. Phase 2 (20 min) — Mind map on whiteboard: Roberto places English words he already knows around the theme 'My August Trip' — airport, Barcelona, Paris, food, hotel. Teacher adds 2-3 words per cluster he missed, expanding the map organically. Phase 3 (25 min) — Structured baseline speaking: Roberto delivers a 90-second spoken introduction ('Hi, I'm Roberto. I'm from São Paulo. I'm a businessman. In August, I'm going to Barcelona and Paris.') — teacher models first, then Roberto mirrors, then improvises. Teacher records key gaps. Phase 4 (10 min) — Debrief in English + Portuguese: teacher presents the 48-lesson journey visually (phases 1–13 on paper), explains what Roberto will be able to do by August, and pins the first 3 'wins' on a physical progress board. Product: baseline audio recorded + mind map kept as living document.
curriculo.homework: Record a 60-second audio on your phone: introduce yourself in English as if meeting someone on the plane to Barcelona. Use: name, city, job, destination, and one thing you want to do there. No script — just speak naturally. Send the audio to the teacher. Estimated time: 15–20 minutes. Also: write 5 English words you already know about airports and 5 about restaurants on a sticky note and bring to class 2.

curriculo.tema: Survival Kit Part 1 — The Magic Phrases That Open Every Door in Europe
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Core travel chunks (Presentation focus): 'Excuse me…', 'Can you help me?', 'Where is…?', 'How much is this?', 'I don't understand.', 'Can you repeat that, please?', 'Do you speak English?'; grammar: yes/no questions with 'Can' and 'Do' (A2 Foundation); vocabulary NEW (6): excuse, repeat, slowly, understand, help, speak; vocabulary REVIEW (4): airport, hotel, restaurant, trip; expression: 'I'm sorry, I don't understand' as a complete survival phrase; idiomatic: 'Bear with me' (transparent for A2)
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Presentation via real-life scenario: Teacher narrates: 'Roberto, you just landed in Barcelona. You're tired. You need the exit. A woman walks by. What do you say?' Teacher demonstrates the chunk 'Excuse me, can you help me?' using physical movement — standing up, making eye contact, using hand gesture. Roberto repeats with gesture. Phase 2 (20 min) — Flashcard drilling with physical cards: 8 printed situation cards (lost in airport / can't find hotel / don't understand the menu / need to repeat) — Roberto picks a card, teacher plays the European local, Roberto uses the correct survival chunk. Drill for speed and naturalness, not perfection. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Phrase Tennis' game: Teacher throws a situation verbally ('You need to ask the price of something'), Roberto returns the correct chunk within 5 seconds. Incorrect or slow = teacher models, Roberto repeats 3x. Phase 4 (20 min) — Mini role-play: Teacher is a Spanish hotel receptionist who speaks limited English. Roberto arrives with a problem (lost luggage). He uses only the 8 chunks learned. Debrief: which chunks felt natural? Which needed more drilling? Product: Roberto can produce all 8 chunks on demand without hesitation.
curriculo.homework: Practice the 8 survival chunks out loud, 3 times each, in front of a mirror. Then record a 45-second audio using at least 5 of the chunks in a 'imaginary conversation' (you play both sides). Send audio to teacher. Estimated time: 20 minutes. Also: find one image of Barcelona online and write 3 sentences in English about what you see, using words from class.

curriculo.tema: Survival Kit Part 2 — Numbers, Prices, and Saying No Politely at European Markets
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Foundation): present simple for transactions ('It costs…', 'I want…', 'I need…', 'I have…'); numbers 1–1,000 in context (prices in euros); polite refusals: 'No, thank you.', 'That's too expensive.', 'I'll think about it.'; vocabulary NEW (7): price, euro, receipt, cash, card, expensive, cheap; vocabulary REVIEW (5): help, understand, excuse, hotel, restaurant; expression new: 'I'll take it!' / 'Just looking, thanks.'; pronunciation focus: -teen vs. -ty (thirteen vs. thirty — common error for Brazilians)
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Number warm-up with physical flashcards: Teacher holds price tags (€12, €35, €150, €290, €1,000) — Roberto reads aloud. Teacher focuses on the -teen/-ty distinction with immediate gentle correction and repetition. Roberto writes prices dictated by teacher (5 rounds). Phase 2 (20 min) — 'La Boqueria Market' simulation: Teacher sets up 3 'stalls' on the table using printed images of Barcelona's La Boqueria market — one with scarves, one with food, one with souvenirs. Roberto 'shops': picks up item, asks price, decides to buy or politely refuse. Teacher plays vendor with varied accents (slow and clear). Phase 3 (20 min) — Price negotiation role-play: Teacher plays a persistent market seller. Roberto practices saying 'That's too expensive,' 'I'll think about it,' and 'Just looking, thanks' with natural tone and body language. Three rounds with increasing vendor persistence. Phase 4 (15 min) — Listening task: Teacher reads 5 short vendor-customer dialogues aloud at natural speed. Roberto identifies the price and whether the customer bought or refused. Debrief: what was hard to catch? Product: Roberto completes a mock market transaction from greeting to goodbye without switching to Portuguese.
curriculo.homework: Watch a 3-minute YouTube video about Barcelona's La Boqueria market (teacher sends link). Write down 5 English words you hear or see. Then practice reading prices aloud: write 10 random euro amounts and read them out loud, recording yourself. Send the audio. Estimated time: 25 minutes.

curriculo.tema: At the Airport — Check-In, Security, and Finding Your Gate Without Panic
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Foundation): past simple — regular verbs (checked, asked, waited, showed, walked); time expressions: yesterday, last time, before (for telling stories at airport); airport-specific vocabulary NEW (8): boarding pass, gate, check-in, security, departure, arrival, carry-on, passport; vocabulary REVIEW (4): help, repeat, excuse, price; expression: 'I used to fly a lot…' (A2 expression list) for warm-up context; chunks: 'Window or aisle?', 'Place your bags on the belt.', 'Do you have anything to declare?', 'My flight is at…'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Vocabulary presentation via airport map: Teacher has a printed GRU (Guarulhos) airport layout and walks Roberto through it in English: 'This is the check-in counter. This is security. This is gate B7.' Roberto labels each area on a blank copy. Physical interaction with the map makes vocabulary sticky. Phase 2 (20 min) — Listening drill: Teacher reads 4 realistic airport announcements aloud (gate change, boarding call, security alert, delayed flight). Roberto identifies key information: flight number, gate, action required. Teacher reads twice — first for gist, second for detail. Phase 3 (20 min) — Role-play sequence (full airport journey): Roberto is at GRU checking in for his Barcelona flight. Teacher plays (a) check-in agent, (b) security officer, (c) gate agent. Roberto uses target chunks at each stop. Teacher introduces one complication per round (overweight bag / forgot to remove laptop / wrong gate). Phase 4 (15 min) — Past simple debrief storytelling: After the role-play, Roberto narrates what happened: 'I checked in. I showed my passport. I waited at gate B7.' Teacher corrects verb forms in context, not in isolation. Product: Roberto narrates a 5-sentence airport story in past simple.
curriculo.homework: Write a paragraph (5–7 sentences) describing your last flight experience in English, using past simple. Use at least 4 airport words from class. Also: listen to a real boarding announcement on YouTube (search 'airport boarding announcement English') and write down 3 things you understood. Estimated time: 30 minutes.

curriculo.tema: At the Airport — Lost Baggage, Problems, and Staying Calm Under Pressure
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Foundation): past simple — irregular verbs (went, took, left, came, said, brought, lost, found); problem-reporting chunks: 'My bag didn't arrive.', 'I left it on the plane.', 'Someone took my bag by mistake.', 'I need to report a lost bag.'; vocabulary NEW (6): luggage, claim, carousel, missing, report, delayed; vocabulary REVIEW (6): boarding pass, passport, gate, check-in, arrive, departure; expression: 'It depends on…' (A2 list) — 'It depends on the airline.'; idiomatic: 'Don't panic' used as a teachable chunk in context
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Irregular verb activation game: Teacher lays 10 verb cards face-down (go/went, take/took, leave/left, come/came, say/said, bring/brought, lose/lost, find/found, have/had, see/saw). Roberto flips a card, teacher gives a sentence stem ('Yesterday, I _____ my bag'), Roberto completes. Fast-paced, low-stakes, high energy. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Lost Luggage Crisis' scenario: Roberto lands in Barcelona. His bag isn't on carousel 4. Teacher plays an airline agent at the lost baggage desk (initially speaking fast, then slowing down). Roberto must: identify himself, describe the bag, report the problem, ask what happens next. Three rounds with increasing complexity. Phase 3 (20 min) — Problem-solving dialogue construction: Together, teacher and Roberto build a model 'lost luggage' dialogue on the whiteboard using irregular past verbs. Roberto copies it, then closes the notebook and reconstructs from memory with teacher prompts only. Phase 4 (20 min) — Stress-inoculation drill: Teacher plays an impatient, fast-speaking airline agent. Roberto's job: stay calm, use the chunks, ask for repetition when needed ('Can you repeat that, please?'). Debrief: what worked? What caused a freeze? Product: Roberto can handle a lost luggage scenario from report to resolution using past simple accurately.
curriculo.homework: Write a short story (6–8 sentences) in past simple: 'Roberto's Worst Day at the Airport.' Make it dramatic — lost bag, wrong gate, missed announcement. Use at least 6 irregular verbs from class. Estimated time: 25 minutes. Optional fun: search 'funny lost luggage stories' on YouTube and note 2 new English words.

curriculo.tema: Arriving in Barcelona — Hotel Check-In, Room Requests, and First Night Confidence
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Building): present continuous for current situations ('I'm looking for…', 'I'm staying at…', 'I'm waiting for…'); hotel vocabulary NEW (8): reservation, lobby, room service, double room, single room, floor, elevator, Wi-Fi; vocabulary REVIEW (4): check-in, passport, arrival, help; chunks: 'I have a reservation under the name Pires.', 'Can I have a room on a higher floor?', 'What time is breakfast?', 'The air conditioning isn't working.'; expression: 'I'm looking forward to…' (A2 list)
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Presentation via hotel brochure: Teacher brings (or prints) a real Barcelona hotel webpage (e.g., Hotel Arts Barcelona). Roberto and teacher read key sections together — room types, amenities, breakfast info. Teacher pre-teaches 8 vocabulary items using images on the brochure. Roberto labels a room layout diagram. Phase 2 (20 min) — Check-in role-play sequence: Teacher is the front desk agent at the hotel. Roberto arrives: confirms reservation under 'Pires,' asks for a high floor, requests Wi-Fi password, mentions one room problem (air conditioning not working). Three rounds: first scripted (teacher provides model), second semi-scripted (key words only), third fully spontaneous. Phase 3 (20 min) — Present continuous drilling in context: Teacher narrates Roberto's evening: 'You're unpacking. You're looking for a restaurant. You're calling room service.' Roberto transforms each into first person ('I'm unpacking. I'm looking for a restaurant…'). Then role-play: Roberto calls front desk with 3 requests using present continuous. Phase 4 (15 min) — 'Problem at the hotel' simulation: Teacher introduces a realistic complication (wrong room type, noisy neighbors, missing towels). Roberto resolves each using target language. Debrief: which phrases felt automatic? Product: Roberto completes a hotel check-in scenario from arrival to room, using present continuous naturally.
curriculo.homework: Write a WhatsApp-style message in English to a friend: 'I just arrived at my hotel in Barcelona. I'm staying at [hotel name]. My room is on the [floor] floor. I'm looking for a good restaurant nearby.' Use present continuous at least 3 times. Estimated time: 15 minutes. Also: look up the real Hotel Arts Barcelona in English and write 3 sentences about what you see.

curriculo.tema: Barcelona Restaurants — Reading a Menu, Ordering Food, and Sounding Like a Local
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Building): present simple vs. present continuous ('I usually eat fish' vs. 'Tonight, I'm eating paella'); ordering expressions: 'I'd like…', 'Can I have…?', 'What do you recommend?', 'Is this spicy?', 'The bill, please.'; restaurant vocabulary NEW (8): menu, starter, main course, dessert, waiter, bill, tip, reservation; vocabulary REVIEW (4): recommend, please, understand, price; chunks: 'For me, I'd like the…', 'Can we have the check, please?', 'What's the soup of the day?'; idiomatic: 'It's a piece of cake' (used post-meal when something was easy — taught in context)
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Real menu analysis: Teacher brings a printed menu from a real Barcelona restaurant (e.g., Bar Mut or El Nacional). Roberto reads section by section — teacher pre-teaches 8 items using images. Roberto circles 3 things he'd order and 3 he'd avoid. Discussion: 'Why? I usually eat… / I don't like…' — present simple in natural use. Phase 2 (20 min) — Ordering sequence role-play: Teacher plays a Barcelona waiter (polite, fast, slight accent). Roberto orders a full meal: greets waiter, asks for recommendation, orders starter + main + drink, asks one question about the menu, requests the bill. Three full rounds — each time teacher adds one complication (dish is sold out / Roberto misunderstands the price). Phase 3 (15 min) — Contrast drilling (present simple vs. continuous): Teacher gives Roberto sentence pairs to sort — 'I eat meat every day' vs. 'Tonight, I'm eating vegetarian.' Roberto creates 5 original sentences about his eating habits vs. tonight's plan. Phase 4 (20 min) — Full dinner simulation: Teacher sets up a 'dinner scene' (printed tablecloth image, menu on table). Roberto plays the full experience from greeting to paying, incorporating all language from the lesson. Debrief using 'It's a piece of cake!' Product: Roberto orders a complete meal in English without pointing at the menu.
curriculo.homework: Find a real Barcelona restaurant menu online (in English) and write your ideal dinner order in 5 sentences: starter, main, drink, dessert, and one question you would ask the waiter. Then record yourself ordering the meal out loud as if the waiter is in front of you. Send the audio. Estimated time: 20–25 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Tapas Culture — Ordering Tapas, Sharing Food, and Small Talk with Spanish Locals
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Building): going to (future plans) — 'We're going to share the patatas bravas.', 'I'm going to try the jamón.'; tapas vocabulary NEW (8): tapas, patatas bravas, jamón, chorizo, croquetas, pulpo, pintxos, raciones; vocabulary REVIEW (4): menu, waiter, recommend, bill; sharing/social chunks: 'Let's share this.', 'Have you tried…?', 'This is amazing!', 'What's in this?'; cultural note: tipping culture in Spain (not expected but appreciated — 5–10%); expression: 'Once in a while, I eat very well — like tonight!'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Cultural immersion warm-up: Teacher shows a 2-minute video clip of a Barcelona tapas bar (no comprehension task — just atmosphere). Discussion: 'What do you see? What do you want to try?' Roberto responds in English. Teacher introduces the 8 tapas vocabulary items using printed images. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Tapas Bar' simulation: Teacher sets up 6 printed tapas images on the table like dishes. Roberto and teacher 'share' them — Roberto uses 'Let's share this,' 'Have you tried the croquetas?', 'What's in this?' Teacher responds naturally, introducing cultural info in English (e.g., 'Patatas bravas have a spicy tomato sauce'). Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Going to' production task: Roberto plans his tapas evening using 'going to': 'I'm going to order jamón first. Then we're going to share the pulpo.' Teacher drills the structure through question-answer: 'What are you going to try first?' Roberto answers spontaneously. Phase 4 (15 min) — Small talk with a local: Teacher plays a friendly Spanish local at the tapas bar. Roberto maintains a short conversation (2–3 minutes) using all target language. Goal: sound warm, engaged, and curious — not just transactional. Product: Roberto sustains a 2-minute tapas-bar small talk exchange using 'going to' naturally.
curriculo.homework: Write a 'tapas plan' for your first night in Barcelona: use 'going to' at least 5 times. Example: 'I'm going to go to a tapas bar near the hotel. I'm going to try patatas bravas and jamón.' Also: look up 3 tapas you've never heard of and write one sentence about each in English. Estimated time: 20 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Getting Around Barcelona — Metro, Taxi, and Asking for Directions Like a Pro
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Building): going to + will contrast — 'I'm going to take the metro' (plan) vs. 'I'll take a taxi — it's easier' (instant decision); directions vocabulary NEW (8): straight ahead, turn left, turn right, block, corner, near, far, cross; vocabulary REVIEW (4): metro, exit, help, understand; direction chunks: 'How do I get to…?', 'Is it far from here?', 'Take the L5 line.', 'Get off at…'; taxi chunks: 'Can you take me to…?', 'How long will it take?', 'Keep the change.'; idiomatic: 'You can't miss it.'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Barcelona metro map activity: Teacher provides a printed Barcelona metro map. Roberto traces 3 routes from his hotel (e.g., Hotel Arts) to La Sagrada Família, La Barceloneta beach, and Camp Nou — in English, describing the route: 'Take the L4. Get off at Verdaguer. Walk straight ahead.' Teacher corrects and models natural phrasing. Phase 2 (20 min) — Directions role-play on a street grid: Teacher draws a simple Barcelona neighborhood grid on the whiteboard. Roberto is at one point, teacher is a local on the street. Three rounds: Roberto asks for directions to 3 different landmarks. Teacher gives directions (increasing complexity). Roberto must physically trace the route on the grid as he listens. Phase 3 (15 min) — Going to vs. will decision game: Teacher presents 5 transport dilemmas ('It's raining. The metro is closed. You're late.'). Roberto decides instantly: 'I'll take a taxi!' or 'I'm going to walk — it's only 2 blocks.' Teacher drills the contrast through rapid scenarios. Phase 4 (20 min) — Taxi simulation: Teacher plays a Barcelona taxi driver (chatty, fast-talking). Roberto gives destination, asks how long, asks about traffic, and pays at the end. Two rounds: one calm, one with a complication (driver takes wrong route). Product: Roberto gives and follows a 4-step set of directions using both 'going to' and 'will' correctly.
curriculo.homework: Using Google Maps in English, plan a route from your Barcelona hotel to La Sagrada Família. Write the directions in English (5–6 steps): 'Turn left on…, Walk straight ahead…, Take the metro at…' Estimated time: 20 minutes. Also: record yourself giving those directions out loud as if explaining to a friend. Send the audio.

curriculo.tema: Barcelona Culture & Sightseeing — Talking About What You See, Feel, and Want to Do
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Building): comparatives and superlatives — 'La Sagrada Família is more impressive than I expected.', 'This is the most beautiful building I've ever seen.'; cultural vocabulary NEW (7): cathedral, architect, neighborhood, exhibition, guided tour, view, architecture; vocabulary REVIEW (5): recommend, amazing, beautiful, visit, go; opinion expressions: 'In my opinion…', 'I think…', 'I agree / I disagree' (A2 list); chunks: 'What's this building called?', 'When was it built?', 'Is there a guided tour in English?'; superlative: 'the most visited', 'the most famous'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Photo comparison warm-up: Teacher brings 6 printed images of Barcelona landmarks (Sagrada Família, Park Güell, Gothic Quarter, Barceloneta, Palau de la Música, Camp Nou). Roberto ranks them from 'most to least interesting' and explains using comparatives: 'Park Güell is more colorful than the Gothic Quarter.' Teacher feeds superlatives naturally: 'Which is the most famous?' Phase 2 (20 min) — Role-play: Tourist and tour guide: Teacher plays a local Barcelona guide. Roberto plays himself as a tourist. At each landmark (shown via image), Roberto asks one question ('What's this called?', 'When was it built?', 'Is there a tour in English?'). Teacher answers at natural pace, Roberto confirms understanding. Three landmarks covered. Phase 3 (20 min) — Opinion debate: Teacher presents two options (e.g., 'Gothic Quarter vs. Barceloneta — which is better for an evening?'). Roberto defends his choice using 'In my opinion…', comparatives, and reasons. Teacher plays devil's advocate to push production. Phase 4 (15 min) — Postcard writing (spoken version): Roberto 'dictates' a postcard from Barcelona to a friend: 'Dear [friend], I'm in Barcelona. This is the most beautiful city I've ever visited. Yesterday, I went to…' Teacher notes grammar and vocabulary accuracy. Product: Roberto produces 5 comparative/superlative sentences about Barcelona from memory.
curriculo.homework: Write a 'postcard' paragraph (6–8 sentences) from Barcelona to a Brazilian friend. Use: 3 comparatives, 1 superlative, and 2 opinion expressions from class. Also: search for 'Barcelona top sights' in English and add 2 new words you found to your vocabulary notebook. Estimated time: 25 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Shopping in Barcelona — Clothing, Souvenirs, and Handling Payment Problems
