curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Building): comparatives review + will for instant decisions ('This one is bigger, but I'll take the smaller one.'); shopping vocabulary NEW (8): size, fitting room, receipt, exchange, refund, discount, souvenir, sale; vocabulary REVIEW (4): price, expensive, cheap, cash; shopping chunks: 'Do you have this in a larger size?', 'Can I try this on?', 'This doesn't fit.', 'Can I get a refund?', 'Do you accept credit cards?'; expression: 'I would rather…' — 'I would rather pay by card.'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Vocabulary introduction via shopping scenario: Teacher lays out 8 printed clothing/souvenir images on the table with price tags in euros. Roberto identifies items, guesses prices, and teacher introduces 8 new vocabulary items through the items themselves. Roberto uses new words immediately: 'This souvenir is too expensive. Do you have a discount?' Phase 2 (20 min) — Shopping role-play in 3 stages: Stage 1 — Roberto enters a Barcelona clothing store, asks for his size, tries something on (mimed). Stage 2 — Item doesn't fit, Roberto asks for a different size. Stage 3 — Item has a defect, Roberto asks for a refund or exchange. Teacher plays a helpful but policy-strict shop assistant. Phase 3 (15 min) — 'I would rather…' drilling: Teacher gives Roberto 5 payment/shopping dilemmas ('Pay cash or card?', 'Buy now or wait for the sale?'). Roberto responds: 'I would rather pay by card because it's safer.' Drill for natural flow. Phase 4 (20 min) — Complaint handling simulation: Teacher plays a shop assistant who says the item can't be refunded. Roberto must calmly insist, ask for a manager, and resolve the situation using all target language. Debrief: how did it feel to complain in English? Product: Roberto completes a full shopping interaction including a problem resolution.
curriculo.homework: Write a 'shopping diary' entry (5–7 sentences): describe a shopping experience in Barcelona (real or imaginary). Use: 3 new vocabulary words, 1 'I would rather…' sentence, and 1 comparative. Also: record yourself asking for a refund in English (practice the scenario from class). Send audio. Estimated time: 20 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Mid-Program Check-In — What Roberto Has Learned and What Comes Next (Aulas 1–11 Review)
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Full review across all 11 aulas: past simple (regular + irregular), present continuous, going to, will, comparatives/superlatives; vocabulary review clusters: airport (8), hotel (8), restaurant/tapas (12), transport/directions (8), shopping (8); expressions recycled: 'Excuse me / Can you help me / I'd like / I'm looking forward to / I would rather / It depends on'; NEW: 2 recycled idiomatic expressions used in fresh contexts ('It's a piece of cake' and 'Once in a while'); grammar gap-fill: identifying what Roberto still confuses (going to vs. will; comparatives vs. superlatives)
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — 'Barcelona Day' warm-up narrative: Roberto narrates his 'ideal day in Barcelona' using all tenses learned: 'Yesterday, I went to the market. Right now, I'm looking for a restaurant. This evening, I'm going to try tapas.' Teacher notes which structures are now automatic and which still need support. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Vocabulary Olympics': Teacher creates a fast-paced game with 4 rounds — (1) Teacher gives a definition, Roberto says the word; (2) Roberto translates chunks back to English from Portuguese; (3) Role-play prompt cards (Roberto picks and performs in 30 seconds); (4) Superlative challenge (Roberto makes sentences about Barcelona landmarks). Phase 3 (20 min) — Diagnostic role-play marathon: 5 mini role-plays back-to-back (airport check-in / hotel problem / restaurant order / tapas bar / shopping return). Teacher times each and notes fluency vs. accuracy balance. This is Roberto's mid-program speaking assessment. Phase 4 (15 min) — Progress debrief + forward planning: Teacher shows Roberto his progress on the physical board from lesson 1. Celebrates genuine wins (specific moments of fluency). Identifies 2–3 focus areas for aulas 13–24. Roberto updates his own 'confidence meter' (1–10) for each scenario covered. Product: teacher-generated written feedback summary handed to Roberto; confidence meter completed.
curriculo.homework: Record a 2-minute audio: 'My Barcelona Week' — narrate an imaginary week in Barcelona using all tenses and vocabulary from aulas 1–11. Use at least: 3 past simple sentences, 2 present continuous, 2 going to, 1 comparative, and 5 travel vocabulary words. Send audio before next class. Estimated time: 30 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Barcelona to Paris — The Journey: Train, Transfers, and Staying Comfortable on the Move
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion — intro): modal verbs 'should' and 'could' — 'You should book the AVE in advance.', 'We could take the overnight train.'; transport vocabulary NEW (8): high-speed train, platform, ticket office, connection, departure board, first class, luggage rack, conductor; vocabulary REVIEW (5): boarding pass, departure, arrival, gate, delay; travel advice chunks: 'You should arrive early.', 'Could you show me my seat?', 'I think I missed my connection.'; expression: 'I used to travel a lot, but now…' (A2 list — recycled in new context)
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Route planning with real map: Teacher shows Roberto a printed Barcelona–Paris train route (AVE to Lyon + TGV to Paris Gare de Lyon). Together they read the itinerary in English: departure times, platform numbers, connection in Lyon. Roberto reads aloud, teacher corrects pronunciation of French station names in an English context. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Should/Could' presentation and drilling: Teacher presents should (advice/recommendation) vs. could (possibility/suggestion) using 5 travel scenarios. Roberto drills: 'Should I book in advance?' → 'Yes, you should — the AVE sells out fast.' Five rapid-fire question-answer pairs. Phase 3 (20 min) — Train journey role-play: Teacher plays a train conductor + a fellow passenger. Roberto: (a) shows ticket, (b) asks where to put luggage, (c) asks if the train stops in Lyon, (d) reports that someone is in his seat. Two rounds — first rehearsed, second spontaneous with one surprise complication. Phase 4 (15 min) — Listening task: Teacher reads a realistic train announcement (platform change + delay) at natural speed. Roberto identifies: new platform, reason for delay, new departure time. Three repetitions: gist → detail → verification. Product: Roberto handles 4 train travel scenarios using 'should' and 'could' correctly.
curriculo.homework: Write 5 travel tips for a friend going from Barcelona to Paris by train. Use 'should' or 'could' in every sentence. Example: 'You should book your ticket at least 3 days before.' Estimated time: 15 minutes. Also: search 'AVE Barcelona Paris' in English and write 2 facts you learned in English. Estimated time: 10 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Arriving in Paris — First Impressions, RER Airport Train, and Getting to the Hotel
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion): modal verbs 'should/could' + 'might' — 'The RER might be faster.', 'We should validate the ticket.'; Paris arrival vocabulary NEW (8): RER, validate, turnstile, zone, Métro, arrondissement, taxi stand, Uber; vocabulary REVIEW (5): platform, connection, luggage, help, reservation; Paris-specific chunks: 'Which line goes to central Paris?', 'Do I need to validate my ticket?', 'Is this the right platform for the city center?', 'Can you take me to the 8th arrondissement?'; cultural note: Paris taxi etiquette vs. Barcelona (no front seat, fixed airport rates); idiomatic: 'Better safe than sorry' — 'Validate the ticket — better safe than sorry.'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Paris CDG arrival scenario warm-up: Teacher shows a printed Paris CDG airport map. Roberto traces the route from arrivals to the RER B in English. Teacher introduces 8 new vocabulary items through the map itself — zone, validate, turnstile, platform. Roberto labels the map. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Might/Should' transport decision game: Teacher presents 5 Paris transport dilemmas (RER vs. taxi / Métro vs. walking / Uber vs. bus). Roberto evaluates: 'The RER might be cheaper. We should take it.' Teacher drills the three modals (should/could/might) through rapid-fire transport scenarios. Phase 3 (20 min) — Airport-to-hotel full scenario: Teacher plays (a) RER ticket agent, (b) Métro fellow passenger, (c) Parisian taxi driver. Roberto navigates the full journey: buys RER ticket, validates it, asks which platform, takes taxi for last leg, gives hotel address. One complication per leg (ticket machine broken / wrong line / taxi driver doesn't know the hotel). Phase 4 (15 min) — Cultural comparison debrief: Teacher and Roberto compare Barcelona vs. Paris transport systems in English using comparatives and modals: 'The Barcelona metro is simpler. Paris might be more complicated, but the RER is faster.' Product: Roberto navigates the CDG-to-hotel journey using 'should,' 'could,' and 'might' appropriately.
curriculo.homework: Plan your route from CDG airport to your Paris hotel (use Google Maps in English). Write 6 steps using modals: 'I should take the RER B. It might take 35 minutes. I could also take a taxi, but it would be more expensive.' Estimated time: 20 minutes. Send the written plan as a voice note reading it aloud.

curriculo.tema: Parisian Cafés — Ordering Coffee, Breakfast, and Navigating French-English Culture
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion): quantifiers — 'a little,' 'a few,' 'a lot of,' 'much,' 'many' (countable vs. uncountable in food context); café vocabulary NEW (8): croissant, espresso, café au lait, baguette, terrace, waiter (garçon — cultural note only), check, jam; vocabulary REVIEW (5): menu, bill, recommend, order, please; Paris café chunks: 'A coffee, please — just a little milk.', 'Do you have a few minutes?', 'Is there much sugar in this?', 'Can I have the check, please?'; cultural etiquette: don't ask for 'café au lait' after noon — cultural survival note; expression: 'I'm looking forward to sitting at a Parisian terrace!'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Café culture presentation: Teacher brings a printed image of a Parisian café terrace with a real menu (Café de Flore or Les Deux Magots). Roberto reads the breakfast section in English. Teacher introduces 8 vocabulary items through the menu. Cultural notes embedded naturally: 'In Paris, you sit, you don't rush. The waiter won't rush you.' Phase 2 (20 min) — Quantifiers in food context: Teacher presents countable vs. uncountable through café items — 'How much coffee? / How many croissants?' Roberto drills using real menu items: 'A little sugar, please.' / 'A few croissants for the table.' / 'Not too much milk.' Five contexts drilled through question-answer with teacher playing waiter. Phase 3 (20 min) — Full café morning role-play: Roberto arrives at a Paris café terrace. Teacher plays a typically reserved Parisian waiter (not unfriendly — just formal). Roberto: greets (with cultural awareness — 'Bonjour' first!), sits, orders breakfast using quantifiers, asks one cultural question, requests the bill. Three rounds with increasing spontaneity. Phase 4 (15 min) — 'Culture clash' debrief discussion: Teacher and Roberto compare Brazilian café culture vs. Parisian in English. Roberto uses 'In my opinion,' comparatives, and new vocabulary. Product: Roberto orders a full café breakfast using quantifiers accurately and demonstrates 2 cultural etiquette points.
curriculo.homework: Write a 'morning in Paris' paragraph (6–8 sentences): describe ordering breakfast at a café. Use: 'a little,' 'a few,' 'a lot of,' 'much,' and 'many' — at least one each. Include 1 cultural observation. Estimated time: 20 minutes. Also: find a real Café de Flore menu online and identify 3 items you would order. Write one sentence about each.

curriculo.tema: Paris Restaurants — Fine Dining Language, Wine, and Handling a Formal Dinner
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion): verb + -ing vs. verb + to (introduction) — 'I enjoy eating French food.', 'I want to try the duck.', 'I avoid ordering things I can't pronounce.'; fine dining vocabulary NEW (8): sommelier, appetizer, entrée, duck confit, foie gras, tasting menu, wine pairing, reservation; vocabulary REVIEW (5): recommend, starter, bill, waiter, menu; formal dining chunks: 'We have a reservation for two under Pires.', 'What do you recommend for the main course?', 'Could you suggest a wine to pair with this?', 'It was absolutely delicious.'; expression: 'I would rather have…' in formal dining context
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Fine dining menu reading: Teacher brings a printed upscale Paris restaurant menu (e.g., a brasserie or bistro style). Roberto reads aloud — teacher helps with pronunciation of French-origin words used in English (entrée, foie gras, sommelier). Roberto identifies what he would order and why, using full sentences. Phase 2 (20 min) — Verb + -ing vs. verb + to presentation: Teacher presents 8 common verb patterns in restaurant context: enjoy/love/avoid + -ing (enjoy eating, love trying, avoid ordering) vs. want/need/decide/plan + to (want to try, need to ask, plan to have). Roberto sorts 10 sentences into two columns, then creates 5 original sentences about his food preferences. Phase 3 (20 min) — Formal dinner simulation: Teacher plays a French sommelier + waiter (formal, professional, slightly intimidating). Roberto manages the full dinner: confirms reservation, orders 3 courses, interacts with sommelier about wine, handles a small problem (wrong order), compliments the food at the end. Focus on formal register vs. casual register from earlier lessons. Phase 4 (15 min) — Register comparison drill: Teacher gives Roberto 5 phrases from casual (tapas bar) vs. formal (Paris restaurant) contexts — Roberto identifies which register and adjusts. 'Hey, what's good here?' → 'What do you recommend this evening?' Product: Roberto completes a formal Paris dinner simulation using verb + -ing/-to correctly and formal register appropriately.
curriculo.homework: Write a 'restaurant review' in English (6–8 sentences) of your ideal Paris dinner. Use: 3 verb + -ing forms, 2 verb + to forms, and 2 fine dining vocabulary words. Example: 'I enjoyed trying the duck confit. I plan to return.' Estimated time: 20 minutes. Send as a voice note reading your review naturally.

curriculo.tema: Paris Sightseeing — The Eiffel Tower, The Louvre, and Talking About Culture and Art
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion): past continuous (introduction) — 'I was walking along the Seine when I saw the Eiffel Tower.', 'People were taking photos everywhere.'; sightseeing vocabulary NEW (8): gallery, masterpiece, queue, admission, guided tour, sculpture, Seine, landmark; vocabulary REVIEW (5): exhibition, beautiful, impressive, recommend, view; storytelling chunks: 'The best part was…', 'I couldn't believe how…', 'You have to see…', 'It's more impressive in person.'; comparatives recycled: 'The Louvre is much bigger than I expected.'; idiomatic: 'Once in a while, you see something that takes your breath away.'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Past continuous presentation via photo story: Teacher shows 4 printed photos of Paris landmarks — Eiffel Tower, Louvre, Notre-Dame, Seine riverbank. Teacher narrates in past continuous: 'In this photo, people were queuing for the Louvre. A couple was walking by the Seine.' Roberto identifies the ongoing actions. Then Roberto describes each photo using past continuous. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Past continuous + when' storytelling: Teacher gives Roberto 5 story prompt cards (each with a Paris landmark + a surprise event). Roberto creates mini-stories: 'I was looking at the Mona Lisa when the lights went out.' Teacher focuses on the 'was/were + -ing + when + past simple' pattern. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Paris Day Out' narration role-play: Teacher plays Roberto's friend on a video call asking about his Paris day. Roberto narrates his sightseeing using past continuous + past simple: 'I was walking near the Eiffel Tower when I decided to go up. The view was the most beautiful thing I've ever seen.' Minimum 8 sentences. Phase 4 (15 min) — Cultural comparison conversation: 'Is the Louvre worth the queue?' Teacher and Roberto debate using comparatives, superlatives, and opinion expressions. Production should feel natural and opinionated. Product: Roberto tells a 2-minute Paris sightseeing story using past continuous accurately in at least 4 sentences.
curriculo.homework: Write a story (8–10 sentences) about an imaginary day in Paris. Use past continuous at least 4 times. Include: 2 Paris landmarks, 1 'I couldn't believe how…' sentence, and 1 superlative. Example: 'I was standing in front of the Eiffel Tower when it started to rain. It was the most magical moment of my trip.' Estimated time: 25 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Paris Safety, Etiquette, and Emergencies — Staying Smart and Calm in the City
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion): modal verbs full set — should (advice), could (possibility), might (uncertainty), must (obligation), mustn't (prohibition) — emergency and safety context; emergency vocabulary NEW (8): police station, embassy, pickpocket, emergency, ambulance, lost, stolen, complaint; vocabulary REVIEW (5): help, report, passport, phone, problem; emergency chunks: 'My wallet was stolen.', 'I need to find the nearest police station.', 'Can you call an ambulance?', 'I lost my passport.', 'I need to contact the Brazilian embassy.'; safety tips in English: mustn't carry all cards together / should keep passport copy; expression: 'It depends on the situation — but you should always stay calm.'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Safety tip brainstorm: Teacher asks Roberto: 'What are 5 things you should do to stay safe in Paris?' Roberto brainstorms in English with support. Teacher introduces must/mustn't through safety rules: 'You must keep your passport safe.' / 'You mustn't carry all your cash in one place.' Roberto creates his own Paris Safety Card (written on an index card to keep). Phase 2 (20 min) — Emergency role-plays (3 rapid scenarios): Scenario 1 — Roberto's phone was pickpocketed on the Métro. Teacher plays a police officer. Roberto reports the incident using past simple + modals. Scenario 2 — Roberto feels unwell at a café. Teacher plays a concerned waiter. Roberto asks for help and describes symptoms. Scenario 3 — Roberto lost his passport. Teacher plays Brazilian consulate officer. Roberto explains calmly using all target modals and past tenses. Phase 3 (20 min) — 'Stress inoculation' emergency call: Teacher plays a fast-talking French emergency operator (111 equivalent). Roberto must stay calm, identify himself, give his location, and describe the problem. Teacher increases speed and confusion each round. Debrief: what froze Roberto? What was automatic? Phase 4 (15 min) — 'Must/Mustn't' card game: Teacher presents 10 situation cards. Roberto must state a rule using must or mustn't within 5 seconds. Fast, fun, and builds automaticity. Product: Roberto reports an emergency scenario in English calmly and accurately using the full range of modals.
curriculo.homework: Write your personal 'Paris Safety Plan' in English: 10 sentences using should, must, mustn't, could, and might. Include real plans (e.g., 'I should keep a photo of my passport on my phone. I mustn't carry all my cash in my wallet.'). Estimated time: 20 minutes. This document will be brought to class 19 for role-play use.

curriculo.tema: Integration Role Play — Roberto's Full European Day: Airport to Dinner in Paris
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Full integration of aulas 1–18: all grammar structures in natural connected speech (past simple, past continuous, present continuous, going to, will, should/could/might, comparatives, quantifiers, verb + -ing/to); vocabulary: 40+ words now in active use — rotating across all domains; focus today: connected speech, sentence linking with 'and,' 'but,' 'so,' 'because,' 'when'; new expression (final block intro): 'All in all…' for summarizing; idiomatic recycled: 'It's a piece of cake now' — Roberto evaluates his own progress; pronunciation: linking words in natural speech ('I went_to' / 'a lot_of' / 'should_I')
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (10 min) — Warm-up: Roberto draws his 'European Trip Timeline' on the whiteboard — one image per phase (GRU → BCN airport → hotel → tapas → train → Paris → café → Louvre → dinner). Teacher prompts Roberto to narrate the timeline in 60 seconds using connected tenses. Phase 2 (25 min) — 'The Grand Role Play' (Part 1 — Barcelona): Teacher plays 5 different characters in sequence (check-in agent → hotel receptionist → tapas bar waiter → transport official → shop assistant with problem). Roberto navigates each using target language from aulas 2–11. Teacher introduces one unexpected complication per scene. No pausing for preparation — Roberto responds in real time. Phase 3 (25 min) — 'The Grand Role Play' (Part 2 — Paris): Teacher plays 5 Paris characters (RER ticket agent → café waiter → Louvre guide → Paris restaurant sommelier → police officer for lost wallet). Same rules — real-time, no preparation, one complication each. Phase 4 (20 min) — Fluency debrief + self-assessment: Roberto watches a brief recording (teacher narrates what happened for key moments). Together they identify: (1) moments of real fluency, (2) grammar still causing hesitation, (3) vocabulary gaps that appeared in real time. Roberto updates his confidence meter. Product: teacher-generated 'fluency snapshot' document given to Roberto, showing progress from aula 1 baseline to aula 19.
curriculo.homework: Record a 3-minute audio called 'My European Adventure.' Tell the full story of your imaginary trip — from GRU to Paris — using all tenses and vocabulary from the course. Make it personal, make it vivid. Use at least: 5 past simple sentences, 3 past continuous, 3 going to, 2 should/might, and 10 travel vocabulary words. This is your most important homework of the block. Send before aula 20. Estimated time: 45 minutes.

curriculo.tema: Block 1 Closing — Roberto's Progress Showcase, Gap Analysis, and Preview of Block 2
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Review and consolidation of all Block 1 content: grammar inventory (past simple regular + irregular / present continuous / going to / will / comparatives / superlatives / should/could/might / quantifiers / past continuous / verb + -ing vs. to); active vocabulary count: ~80+ words across all domains; expressions consolidated: 'Excuse me / I'd like / I'm looking forward to / I would rather / I used to / It depends on / In my opinion / Once in a while / It's a piece of cake / All in all'; pronunciation targets revisited: -teen/-ty distinction, linking in connected speech, sentence stress in questions; NEW preview: introduction of 2 B1-adjacent chunks for Block 2 motivation: 'I've been to…' (preview only — not taught) and 'I was wondering if…' (softer request form for Paris formal contexts)
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 (15 min) — Homework audio playback: Teacher plays Roberto's 3-minute 'European Adventure' audio from homework. Together they listen and mark: (a) green = fluent and accurate, (b) yellow = understood but grammar uncertain, (c) red = blocked or avoided. This is Roberto's most tangible evidence of growth. Phase 2 (20 min) — 'Best of Block 1' celebration game: Teacher creates a rapid-fire game with Roberto's STRONGEST moments from the 20 aulas — sentences he produced, chunks he nailed, breakthroughs in role-play. Roberto must reproduce each one from a prompt. Fun, fast, confidence-building. Genuine celebration of real progress. Phase 3 (20 min) — Gap map for Block 2: Teacher presents a clear, honest, specific gap analysis: 'These 3 structures still need work in Block 2: (1) past continuous in spontaneous speech, (2) quantifier selection under pressure, (3) verb + -ing vs. to in new contexts.' Roberto and teacher agree on 3 personal focus goals for aulas 21–40. Written on his progress board. Phase 4 (15 min) — Block 2 preview and motivation: Teacher previews what's coming (aulas 21–40: deeper Paris immersion, cultural nuance, expanded modals, B1 bridge content). Teacher plays a 30-second authentic audio clip (Paris bistro ambient + English interaction) — Roberto identifies 5 words he recognizes. Closes with Roberto saying one thing in English about what he's looking forward to in Block 2. Product: completed Block 1 progress report (teacher-written, handed to Roberto), confidence meter updated, personal goals for Block 2 signed by Roberto.
curriculo.homework: Before Block 2 begins: re-listen to your very first audio (aula 1 homework — the 60-second self-introduction from the plane). Write 5 differences you notice between that audio and your aula 19 audio. Write them in English. Then write 3 goals for Block 2 in English: 'In Block 2, I want to…' Estimated time: 30 minutes. Bring both documents to the first class of Block 2.

curriculo.tema: Welcome to Amsterdam — Arriving at Schiphol, Reading Signs, and Getting to the City Center
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion review): modal verbs 'should/could/might' applied to new destination; NEW vocabulary: arrivals hall, luggage carousel, currency exchange, tram, single ticket, platform, connection, transit, stamp (passport), customs; Chunks: 'Which platform does the train leave from?', 'Should I take the tram or the metro?', 'This might take a while.'; Expression: 'Better safe than sorry.'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher displays a photo of Schiphol Airport and asks Roberto to narrate what he sees using vocab from past aulas — building retrieval and connecting to new destination. Presentation (15 min): Teacher introduces Amsterdam as the new city in Block 2, presents 8 new vocabulary items with visual flashcards of real Schiphol signage (arrivals, customs, tram symbols). Pronunciation drilling with minimal pairs (tram/train, platform/form). Practice (25 min): Gap-fill dialogue at immigration + customs roleplay using modal verbs — teacher plays border officer, Roberto plays passenger; then Roberto plays a confused tourist asking a local which tram to take to the city center, using 'should/could/might' naturally. Production (20 min): Roberto narrates aloud a 'mini story': 'I just landed at Schiphol. I should... I might... I could...' — teacher prompts with visual cues. Debrief (10 min): vocabulary card sort — Roberto categorizes words by airport zone (arrivals, customs, transit, exit).
curriculo.homework: Record a 90-second audio narrating Roberto's imaginary arrival at Schiphol: what he sees, what he does, which transport he chooses and why — use at least 3 modal verbs and 5 new words from class. (Est. 20 min). Also: look up the real Schiphol airport map online and write 5 sentences describing where things are using 'There is / There are' + location vocabulary. (Est. 15 min).

curriculo.tema: Dutch Hospitality — Checking Into an Amsterdam Canal Hotel and Requesting Room Upgrades
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: verb + -ing vs. verb + to (Expansion consolidation) in hotel request context — 'I'd like to request a room with a canal view.', 'I enjoy staying in boutique hotels.', 'I need to check out by 11.'; NEW vocabulary: canal view, boutique hotel, adjoining room, amenities, complimentary breakfast, front desk, housekeeping, late checkout, minibar, room service; Chunks: 'Is breakfast included?', 'I was hoping for a quieter room.', 'Would it be possible to…?'; Expression: 'It never hurts to ask.'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Roberto recalls hotel vocabulary from Aula 6 (Barcelona check-in) — teacher elicits phrases, writes on board, and bridges to new 'upgrade' language. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents the 'polite request upgrade' script using verb + -ing / verb + to contrast with color-coded examples on cards. Roberto identifies which form follows which verb using 3 quick sorting tasks. Practice (25 min): Controlled role-play — teacher is the front desk agent at a boutique Amsterdam canal hotel; Roberto uses the new chunks to request a room upgrade, ask about amenities, and negotiate late checkout. First round: scripted; second round: semi-free with only keyword prompts on a card. Production (20 min): Roberto designs his 'perfect Amsterdam hotel stay' — describes it aloud in 8-10 sentences using new vocabulary and verb patterns, while teacher notes errors for reformulation. Debrief (10 min): Teacher returns 3 reformulated sentences; Roberto repeats them 3 times each for pronunciation and internalization.
curriculo.homework: Write a simulated hotel email (8-10 sentences) from 'Roberto Pires' to a canal hotel in Amsterdam requesting a specific room type, mentioning arrival date (use American date format), asking about amenities, and requesting late checkout. Use at least 4 new vocabulary items and 2 verb + -ing / verb + to structures. (Est. 25 min). Listen to a short YouTube hotel check-in dialogue (teacher provides link) and note 3 phrases Roberto hasn't learned yet. (Est. 15 min).

curriculo.tema: Amsterdam Street Food and Brown Cafés — Ordering Dutch Snacks and Making Small Talk with Locals
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: quantifiers review (much/many/a lot of/a little/a few) + countable vs. uncountable in food context; NEW vocabulary: stroopwafel, herring, Dutch courage, brown café (bruine kroeg), portion, topping, draft beer, bitterballen, cheese market, open-faced sandwich; Chunks: 'I'll have a small portion of…', 'How many are in one order?', 'Is there much spice in this?'; Expression: 'Once in a while' (review A2 expression — used in new context: 'I eat street food once in a while.').; Collocation: 'grab a bite', 'wash it down with'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places real (or printed) photos of Dutch street food on the table — Roberto guesses names, rates what he'd try from 1-5, producing spontaneous reactions using 'I think', 'It looks like', 'I'd rather have…'. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new food/café words with visual flashcards; drills quantifier contrast using real food items — 'How much herring? How many bitterballen?' with Roberto responding physically (hold up fingers, point to image). Practice (25 min): Menu simulation — teacher provides a printed mock Dutch café menu in English; Roberto orders a full meal for himself and a friend using quantifiers and new vocabulary; teacher plays the waiter and creates one 'problem' (item unavailable) requiring Roberto to adapt. Production (20 min): Roberto plays the role of a Brazilian entrepreneur recommending Amsterdam street food to a colleague — describes 3 items using 'a lot of flavor', 'a few good options', 'not much spice'. Debrief (10 min): Vocabulary callback game — teacher says Dutch food, Roberto gives the English sentence using one quantifier.
curriculo.homework: Record a 2-minute audio as if recommending Amsterdam food to a Brazilian friend who is visiting next month — describe at least 3 foods, use quantifiers naturally, and include 'once in a while' and 'grab a bite'. (Est. 20 min). Write a short paragraph (5-7 sentences) comparing Amsterdam food to Barcelona tapas using comparatives (review from Aulas 10-11): 'Bitterballen are smaller than tapas, but…' (Est. 15 min).

curriculo.tema: Mid-Program Milestone — Roberto's Progress Review, Confidence Assessment, and Preview of the Final Stretch (Aulas 1–23)
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Full grammar inventory review: past simple (regular + irregular), present continuous, going to, will, comparatives/superlatives, modal verbs (should/could/might), quantifiers, verb + -ing vs. verb + to, past continuous; Vocabulary review across all themes: airport, hotel, restaurant, transport, directions, sightseeing, shopping, cultural etiquette; Chunks and expressions recycled: 'Excuse me, can you help me?', 'I'm looking forward to…', 'I would rather…', 'It depends on…', 'Better safe than sorry', 'Once in a while'; Self-assessment language: 'I feel more confident about…', 'I still struggle with…', 'I've improved in…'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up — Confidence Map (15 min): Roberto rates himself 1-5 on a printed grid of all major topics covered (airport, hotel, food, transport, sightseeing, emergencies) — teacher discusses patterns and validates progress. Progress Test — Communicative (30 min): Structured role-play simulating a compressed 'European day': Roberto checks into a hotel (Amsterdam), orders food at a café, asks for directions to a museum, handles a minor problem (wrong room), and tells the story of his trip so far to a new acquaintance — teacher evaluates using a simple rubric: fluency, vocabulary range, grammar accuracy, communication success. Gap Analysis (15 min): Teacher presents 3 key areas where Roberto excelled and 3 areas to develop in Block 2 aulas 25-40 — framed positively as 'next level unlocks'. Preview + Motivation Boost (20 min): Teacher unveils the Block 2 map — London, Rome, practical business English situations and high-stakes travel moments; Roberto shares which situations excite him most. Debrief (10 min): Roberto writes 3 personal goals for the second half of the program on a card to keep.
curriculo.homework: Write a 10-sentence 'progress journal entry' as Roberto Pires: what you could NOT do in Aula 1 that you CAN do now, what surprised you about learning English, and one goal for the next 10 aulas. (Est. 20 min). Record a 2-minute audio simulating meeting someone new and telling them about your trip — use at least 5 different grammar structures from the program. Send recording. (Est. 20 min).

curriculo.tema: Flying Into London — Heathrow Arrivals, Oyster Cards, and Roberto's First Tube Ride
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: past continuous + past simple (narrative combination — Expansion consolidation): 'I was waiting at the gate when they announced the delay.', 'While I was riding the Tube, I noticed the signs.'; NEW vocabulary: Oyster card, top up, Underground/Tube, zone, exit barrier, peak hours, delay announcement, terminal, connecting flight, baggage reclaim; Chunks: 'I need to top up my card.', 'Which zone is this station in?', 'The train was delayed by 20 minutes.'; Expression: 'In the nick of time.' [Note: British English context flagged for cultural awareness only — American English remains default for Roberto's production]
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher shows a short video clip (30-60 sec, no subtitles) of a Heathrow arrival scene — Roberto listens and notes 3 words he recognizes and 3 he doesn't; class discusses. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new vocabulary items using London Underground map as visual anchor — Roberto traces a route from Heathrow to central London while learning zone and station vocabulary. Grammar focus: past continuous + past simple with a timeline diagram on the board. Practice (25 min): Listening exercise — teacher reads a short narrative about a traveler's Tube journey with 3 problems; Roberto fills in a timeline placing past simple and past continuous actions. Then: dialogue practice — Roberto plays a traveler who was waiting at the wrong platform and explains what happened to an information desk worker (teacher). Production (20 min): Roberto narrates his imaginary Heathrow arrival story from start to hotel, using past continuous + past simple at least 4 times. Teacher records key errors for reformulation. Debrief (10 min): Vocabulary card game — Roberto matches Tube vocabulary to definitions without looking at notes.
curriculo.homework: Write 8 sentences narrating Roberto's first Tube journey — alternate between past continuous and past simple in at least 4 of them. (Est. 20 min). Find and listen to one real London Underground announcement on YouTube (teacher provides keywords to search); write down 3 words you understood and 2 you didn't. (Est. 10 min).

curriculo.tema: London Hotels and Business Districts — Staying Near The City, Networking Language, and Professional Small Talk
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: verb + -ing vs. verb + to (Expansion — consolidation in professional context) + present simple for professional identity; NEW vocabulary (professional + travel blend): business district, networking event, headquarters, agenda, colleague, conference center, rooftop bar, city view, boutique vs. chain hotel, concierge; Chunks: 'I'm staying near the financial district.', 'I work with clients across Europe.', 'I enjoy meeting people from different industries.'; Expression: 'It depends on…' (review — new professional context); Collocation: 'close a deal', 'run a meeting', 'keep in touch'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher asks Roberto: 'Imagine you're in London for a business dinner after a day of tourism — how do you introduce yourself in English?' Roberto attempts cold; teacher notes what flows and what breaks down. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents the 'professional small talk' script — how to introduce yourself as a Brazilian entrepreneur in London without sounding robotic; focus on verb + -ing / verb + to in professional collocations ('I enjoy working with international partners', 'I'd like to expand to Europe'). 8 new vocabulary items via visual flashcards of London business districts. Practice (25 min): Structured networking role-play — teacher plays a British entrepreneur at a hotel rooftop bar; Roberto approaches, introduces himself, asks questions, and sustains a 3-minute conversation using new vocabulary and chunks. Three rounds with different scenario cards (first meeting, reconnecting, asking for advice). Production (20 min): Roberto records a 90-second 'elevator pitch' about himself as if meeting a European partner — teacher gives feedback on structure, vocabulary use, and naturalness. Debrief (10 min): Teacher reformulates 3 of Roberto's sentences into more natural English; Roberto repeats and explains the difference.
curriculo.homework: Record a 2-minute audio as Roberto Pires introducing yourself to a London business contact at a hotel bar — include: name, city, what you do, what you enjoy about traveling for business, and one question for the other person. Use 3 new collocations. (Est. 20 min). Write a LinkedIn-style bio (5-7 sentences) in English for Roberto Pires. (Est. 20 min).

curriculo.tema: London Restaurants and Pub Culture — Ordering a Full English, Navigating Pub Menus, and British Food Etiquette
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: going to + will contrast (Building review in new cultural context) + verb + -ing for preferences; NEW vocabulary: pub, pint, full English breakfast, Sunday roast, Yorkshire pudding, side dish, set menu, fixed price, bill/check, tip culture; Chunks: 'I'll go for the…', 'What do you recommend?', 'I'm going to try something traditional tonight.', 'Can we get the check, please?'; Expression: 'I would rather…' (review — applied to food preferences: 'I would rather try the local dish than order pasta.'); Collocation: 'go for a pint', 'split the bill', 'leave a tip'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places a printed mock British pub menu on the table — Roberto reads for 2 minutes and marks what he'd order; teacher elicits reactions using 'I would rather / I'm going to / I'll'. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new vocabulary items with images of real pub dishes; contrasts 'bill' (British) vs. 'check' (American) culturally without changing Roberto's production default. Chunk drilling: 3 rounds of 'ordering ping-pong' — teacher says context, Roberto produces the chunk. Practice (25 min): Full pub dinner simulation — Roberto and teacher perform a complete pub visit from entering to paying; teacher introduces one complication (Roberto's order is unavailable); Roberto handles it using 'I'll go for…', 'In that case, I'd rather…'. Second scenario: Roberto is with a British colleague — makes recommendations and splits the bill. Production (20 min): Roberto tells the story of a 'perfect London pub night' — past tense narration with at least one past continuous + past simple combination and 5 new vocabulary items. Debrief (10 min): Compare British vs. Spanish vs. French food culture — Roberto uses comparatives to reflect on all 4 cities visited so far.
curriculo.homework: Write a 10-sentence diary entry as Roberto Pires after his first pub dinner in London — include what he ordered, what went wrong, how he handled it, and what he thought of the experience. Use past simple + past continuous at least twice. (Est. 20 min). Record a 60-second audio recommending a London pub dish to a friend. (Est. 10 min).

curriculo.tema: Sightseeing London — Buckingham Palace, The Thames, and Describing What You See Like a Confident Traveler
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: present continuous for live description ('I'm standing in front of Buckingham Palace…') + comparatives/superlatives review; NEW vocabulary: palace, guard, ceremony, exhibit, Thames River, double-decker, sightseeing tour, landmark, queue, audio guide; Chunks: 'It's bigger than I expected.', 'This is probably the most famous…', 'I'm standing right in front of…'; Expression: 'I'm looking forward to…' (review — future plans for the day's visits); Collocation: 'take a tour', 'join the queue', 'catch the ceremony'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher shows 5 photos of London landmarks without labels — Roberto identifies what he knows and describes each photo in 1-2 sentences using 'It looks like', 'I think this is', 'It's probably'. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new sightseeing vocabulary items via landmark photos; drills present continuous for live description — teacher models a 'live commentary' style ('Right now, I'm looking at the Thames. The river is flowing…') and Roberto repeats with his own observations about the photos. Practice (25 min): 'London Vlog Simulation' — Roberto narrates a walking tour of 3 London landmarks (photos on flashcards) as if recording a video for a Brazilian friend; teacher plays the friend asking questions ('Is it crowded?', 'How long did you wait?'). Roberto switches between present continuous (live description) and past simple (what happened earlier). Production (20 min): Full 3-minute London tour narration combining all 3 landmarks, comparatives, and at least 5 new words. Recorded for self-assessment. Debrief (10 min): Roberto compares London sightseeing to Barcelona and Paris — teacher elicits superlatives ('The most impressive', 'The busiest', 'The most crowded').
curriculo.homework: Record a 2-minute 'London vlog' audio as Roberto Pires describing one London landmark — use present continuous for live description, at least 2 comparatives, and 5 new vocabulary items. (Est. 20 min). Write 5 comparison sentences between London and Barcelona or Paris using superlatives. (Est. 15 min).

curriculo.tema: Getting Around London — Black Cabs, Buses, and Asking for Help When the GPS Fails
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: past continuous + past simple (narrative — consolidation) + will for instant decisions in transport; NEW vocabulary: black cab, double-decker bus, bus stop, route number, fare, congestion, pedestrian crossing, cab rank, night bus, transfer; Chunks: 'Could you take me to…?', 'How long will it take?', 'I'll just take a cab — it's easier.', 'The bus was full, so I decided to walk.'; Expression: 'Better safe than sorry.' (review — applied to transport choices); Collocation: 'hail a cab', 'check the route', 'miss the bus'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher presents a 'London transport emergency' scenario card: 'Your phone died. You don't know the bus route. It's raining.' Roberto brainstorms aloud what he would do — eliciting modal verbs, will for decisions, and known chunks. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new transport vocabulary items with a London bus map as visual; models the past continuous + past simple transport narrative ('I was waiting at the bus stop when a black cab appeared — I'll take it!'). Practice (25 min): Role-play carousel — 3 mini-scenarios: (1) Roberto hails a cab and negotiates the route; (2) Roberto asks a stranger which bus goes to the hotel; (3) Roberto explains to the hotel concierge why he was late (past continuous narrative). Teacher plays all 3 counterparts. Production (20 min): Roberto narrates the story of 'the day everything went wrong with transport in London' — a 2-minute monologue using past simple + past continuous + will for decisions, plus at least 5 new vocabulary items. Debrief (10 min): Quick vocabulary retrieval — teacher mimes transport actions; Roberto produces the word + a sentence.
curriculo.homework: Write an 8-10 sentence narrative about a fictional transport problem in London — include past simple, past continuous, and will for instant decisions. (Est. 20 min). Listen to a 2-minute podcast excerpt about London transport (teacher provides link) and note 3 new words. (Est. 15 min).

curriculo.tema: London Shopping and Souvenirs — Oxford Street, Haggling Etiquette, and Handling Currency Confusion
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: will for instant decisions + comparatives (Building consolidation) in shopping context; NEW vocabulary: department store, receipt, exchange rate, price tag, fitting room, discount, refund policy, souvenir shop, cash only, contactless payment; Chunks: 'Do you have this in a different size?', 'Can I get a refund if it doesn't fit?', 'I'll take the blue one — it's more practical.'; Expression: 'It's a piece of cake.' (review — used when a shopping transaction goes smoothly: 'Paying with my card was a piece of cake.'); Collocation: 'try on', 'pay by card', 'ask for a receipt'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places 5 printed price tags with British pounds on the table — Roberto converts to Brazilian reais mentally and reacts in English ('That's expensive!', 'That's a good deal.', 'I'll think about it.'). Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new shopping vocabulary items with images of Oxford Street stores; drills will for instant decisions in a rapid-fire game ('The blue bag costs £20 and the red one costs £15 — which do you buy?' — Roberto responds instantly with 'I'll take…'). Practice (25 min): Oxford Street shopping simulation — Roberto shops in 3 different stores (teacher plays 3 different shop assistants): (1) clothing store where an item doesn't fit; (2) souvenir shop where he buys gifts for family; (3) electronics store where he checks return policy. Each interaction requires at least 2 new vocabulary items and one 'instant will decision'. Production (20 min): Roberto gives a 2-minute spoken 'shopping report' — narrates what he bought, why, how much he spent, and what he's going to do with each item. Uses past simple for completed actions and 'I'm going to give this to…' for plans. Debrief (10 min): Teacher reformulates 3 error-laden sentences from production phase; Roberto identifies which grammar rule applies.
curriculo.homework: Record a 90-second audio as Roberto Pires describing 3 souvenirs you bought in London — what they are, who they're for, and how much they cost. Use 'will' for at least one decision and comparatives for at least one comparison. (Est. 15 min). Write a 6-sentence simulated conversation between Roberto and a shop assistant handling a return or exchange. (Est. 20 min).

curriculo.tema: London Cultural Etiquette — Queueing, Polite Disagreement, and What NOT to Say to a British Person
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: modal verbs full set (should/could/might) for advice and cultural guidance + 'I agree / I disagree' expressions; NEW vocabulary: queue, etiquette, polite disagreement, understatement, sarcasm (awareness), apologize, reserved, formal, informal register, body language; Chunks: 'You should probably queue here.', 'I might be wrong, but…', 'I'm not sure I agree — could it be that…?'; Expression: 'In my opinion…' (review — applied to cultural observations); 'I agree / I disagree' (A2 expression — first formal use in program); Collocation: 'jump the queue', 'keep a straight face', 'mind your manners'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher presents 5 cultural 'would you do this in England?' scenarios (cutting the queue, speaking loudly on the Tube, asking a stranger's salary) — Roberto reacts using 'should/shouldn't', building instinct for British etiquette in English. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new cultural etiquette vocabulary items; introduces the concept of understatement in British English ('not bad' = excellent) as cultural awareness; drills polite disagreement chunks using a back-and-forth 'agree/disagree' card game. Practice (25 min): Structured debate simulation — teacher makes 5 'controversial' statements about travel etiquette (e.g., 'Tourists should always tip in London.'); Roberto practices agreeing/disagreeing politely using the target chunks; focus on tone and body language as much as language. Second activity: Roberto gives cultural advice to an imaginary Brazilian friend visiting London — uses 'you should', 'you might want to', 'it's better to'. Production (20 min): Roberto prepares and delivers a 2-minute 'cultural briefing' for a Brazilian colleague about London etiquette — structured as 3 tips with reasons. Teacher evaluates: register, modal use, chunk integration. Debrief (10 min): Reflective discussion — how does British etiquette compare to Spanish and French? Roberto produces comparison sentences.
curriculo.homework: Write a 'London Etiquette Guide for Brazilians' — 5 tips in English using modal verbs (should/could/might) for each tip. (Est. 20 min). Record a 60-second audio giving your most important cultural tip for London — speak naturally without reading from notes. (Est. 10 min).

curriculo.tema: Handling Problems in London — Lost Phone, Medical Appointment, and Staying Calm Under Real Pressure
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: modal verbs (should/could/might) + past simple for problem narration; NEW vocabulary: pharmacy, prescription, urgent care, lost and found, report (verb), insurance, symptom, reception desk, replacement, compensation; Chunks: 'I think I might have left it on the Tube.', 'Could you tell me where the nearest pharmacy is?', 'I should have been more careful.'; Expression: 'Better safe than sorry.' (review — now in health/safety context); Collocation: 'report a loss', 'fill out a form', 'seek medical attention'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher presents 3 'London emergency' scenario cards — Roberto votes on which he finds most stressful; class discusses what he would do first in each, activating known language around problems and emergencies (from Aula 18). Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new emergency/problem vocabulary items via scenario images; models 'I should have…' as a new production chunk for regret/reflection; drills the difference between 'I lost my phone' (past simple) and 'I was looking for my phone when I realized…' (past continuous + past simple). Practice (25 min): Emergency role-play sequence — 3 stations: (1) Roberto reports a lost phone at a Transport for London office (teacher = clerk); (2) Roberto asks a pharmacist for help with a headache and needs to explain symptoms; (3) Roberto calls his hotel from a borrowed phone to cancel dinner plans, explaining the situation. Each scenario requires at least 3 new vocabulary items. Production (20 min): Roberto narrates 'The Worst Morning in London' — a 2-3 minute monologue combining past simple, past continuous, modal verbs, and problem vocabulary. Teacher evaluates communication success over grammatical perfection. Debrief (10 min): Roberto identifies which scenario felt hardest and why — teacher provides targeted reformulation and confidence-building feedback.
curriculo.homework: Write a 10-sentence insurance claim narrative as Roberto Pires reporting a lost phone in London — include: when it happened, what you were doing, what you did next, and what you should have done differently. (Est. 20 min). Record a 90-second audio calling a hotel to report a problem — speak naturally, don't read a script. (Est. 15 min).

curriculo.tema: From London to Rome — International Connections, Flight Delays, and Roberto's Italian Arrival Story
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: past continuous + past simple (Expansion — full narrative integration) + quantifiers review in travel context; NEW vocabulary: connecting flight, transit lounge, gate change, boarding pass, runway, turbulence, customs declaration, passport control, time zone, jet lag; Chunks: 'My flight was delayed by two hours.', 'While I was waiting, they changed the gate.', 'There weren't many passengers at that hour.'; Expression: 'In the nick of time.' (review — used in flight narrative: 'I made it to the gate in the nick of time.'); Collocation: 'make a connection', 'clear customs', 'claim your baggage'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher presents a chaotic airport departure board with multiple delays and gate changes — Roberto narrates what is happening using present continuous and what happened using past simple, connecting both naturally. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new flight/transit vocabulary items via a realistic boarding pass and departure board visual; builds a timeline on the board showing a delayed London-Rome flight narrative; models how past continuous and past simple create dramatic storytelling in travel contexts. Practice (25 min): Narrative building exercise — teacher provides 6 illustrated timeline cards of a chaotic travel day; Roberto arranges them and narrates the full story using target grammar; then Roberto and teacher perform a dialogue where Roberto explains to the Rome hotel why he arrived late (using past continuous + past simple naturally). Production (20 min): Roberto performs a 'travel confession' monologue — tells the story of the most chaotic travel leg of the trip so far, incorporating at least 6 new vocabulary items and 4 instances of the grammar target. Debrief (10 min): Roberto listens back to his production (teacher's notes) and self-corrects 2 errors with teacher guidance.
curriculo.homework: Write a 10-sentence narrative about a delayed flight experience (real or imagined) as Roberto Pires — use past continuous + past simple for at least 4 sentences. (Est. 20 min). Listen to an airport announcement simulation on YouTube (teacher provides keywords); write down the gate number, destination, and reason for delay that you hear. (Est. 10 min).

curriculo.tema: Rome Arrivals — Fiumicino Airport, Leonardo Express, and Roberto's First Roman Hotel
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: going to + will contrast (Building review — new Roman context) + present continuous for live updates; NEW vocabulary: express train, terminal, shuttle bus, central station, tourist information, key card, elevator, luggage storage, concierge, city map; Chunks: 'I'm going to take the express train — it's faster.', 'I'll ask at the information desk.', 'I'm staying at a hotel near the Colosseum.'; Expression: 'I'm looking forward to…' (review — Roberto's excitement for Rome: 'I'm really looking forward to seeing the Colosseum.'); Collocation: 'store your luggage', 'pick up a map', 'ask for directions'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher shows a photo of Rome's Fiumicino Airport and asks: 'Roberto, you just landed. It's 8pm. Your hotel is near the Colosseum. What are you going to do?' — Roberto plans his arrival using going to + will contrast spontaneously. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new vocabulary items using a real map of central Rome and the Fiumicino Airport layout; models going to vs. will with specific Roman scenarios ('I'm going to book the express train in advance' vs. 'The shuttle is full — I'll find a taxi'). Practice (25 min): Rome arrival simulation — Roberto navigates from landing to hotel in 3 sequential role-plays: (1) at the information desk asking about transport options; (2) at the train ticket machine (teacher plays an Italian stranger who speaks limited English); (3) hotel check-in — Roberto mentions plans for the next morning using going to. Production (20 min): Roberto narrates his 'Roman arrival plan' — what he's going to do tonight, tomorrow morning, and tomorrow afternoon, using going to for plans and will for spontaneous decisions. At least 6 new vocabulary items included. Debrief (10 min): Quick map challenge — teacher points to places on a Rome map; Roberto says how he'd get there from the hotel.
curriculo.homework: Write Roberto Pires's Rome itinerary for Day 1 — 8 sentences using 'going to' for planned activities and 'will' for at least 2 spontaneous decisions. (Est. 20 min). Record a 90-second audio telling a friend what you're looking forward to in Rome — use 'I'm looking forward to', going to, and 3 new vocabulary items. (Est. 15 min).

curriculo.tema: Roman Restaurants and Trattorie — Ordering Italian Food in English, Wine Culture, and La Dolce Vita
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: verb + -ing vs. verb + to (Expansion consolidation) + going to for meal plans; NEW vocabulary: trattoria, antipasto, primo, secondo, dolce, house wine, sparkling water, cover charge, prix fixe, sommelier; Chunks: 'I'd like to start with the antipasto.', 'I enjoy trying local wine.', 'We're going to share the dessert.', 'What do you recommend for the main course?'; Expression: 'I would rather…' (review — food preference: 'I would rather have the fish than the pasta.'); Collocation: 'pair with wine', 'save room for dessert', 'ask for the wine list'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher places a printed mock Italian trattoria menu (in English) on the table — Roberto reads and marks what he'd order for a full 4-course Italian meal; teacher elicits reactions, comparing Italian and French menu structure (review from Aula 16). Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new restaurant vocabulary items with images of iconic Roman dishes; drills verb + -ing / verb + to in food preference context — 'I enjoy eating pasta', 'I want to try the tiramisu', 'I need to avoid spicy food'. Students categorize verbs by which pattern they follow using a card sort. Practice (25 min): Full Roman trattoria simulation — Roberto and teacher perform a 3-course dinner: Roberto orders, asks for recommendations, handles a substitution request, and asks about the wine list; teacher introduces a complication (the sommelier recommends an expensive wine — Roberto politely declines and chooses differently). Second round: Roberto is hosting a fictional Italian business contact — he orders for both and makes recommendations. Production (20 min): Roberto narrates 'My perfect Roman dinner' — 2-minute spoken description of the ideal meal, using going to, verb + -ing / verb + to, and 6 new vocabulary items. Debrief (10 min): Roberto compares Rome, Paris, Barcelona, and London dining using superlatives and personal preferences.
curriculo.homework: Write a 10-sentence restaurant review of a fictional Roman trattoria as Roberto Pires — include what you ordered, what you enjoyed, what you would recommend, and what you would rather avoid. Use verb + -ing / verb + to at least 3 times. (Est. 20 min). Record a 60-second audio ordering a full Italian dinner — speak naturally without reading. (Est. 10 min).

curriculo.tema: Rome Sightseeing — The Colosseum, Vatican, and Telling Stories About Historic Places
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: past continuous + past simple (Expansion — storytelling peak) + superlatives for emotional impact; NEW vocabulary: ancient, ruins, monument, guided tour, audio guide, chapel, fresco, sculpture, crowd, restoration; Chunks: 'I was walking through the ruins when I realized how old this place is.', 'It's the most breathtaking thing I've ever seen.', 'There were thousands of people, but it was still impressive.'; Expression: 'Once in a while, you see something that changes the way you think.' (extended use of 'once in a while'); Collocation: 'take in the view', 'stand in awe', 'capture the moment'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher shows 5 photos of Rome's major sites without names — Roberto identifies what he knows, describes each using present continuous ('I can see...', 'There are...'), and guesses historical significance. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new sightseeing/history vocabulary items using Colosseum and Vatican images; models emotional storytelling with past continuous + past simple: 'I was climbing the steps when I first saw the Colosseum — it was the most impressive moment of the trip.' Roberto identifies the grammar structure and emotional effect. Practice (25 min): 'Roman storytelling' activity — teacher gives Roberto 4 illustrated scene cards from a Colosseum visit; Roberto arranges them and narrates a full story, naturally alternating past simple and past continuous; teacher interrupts with 'tourist questions' that Roberto must answer in real time. Second activity: Roberto describes a Vatican chapel fresco (photo) using superlatives and emotional vocabulary. Production (20 min): Roberto delivers a 3-minute spoken travel narrative about his 'most memorable moment' in Rome — must include: past continuous + past simple at least 4 times, 2 superlatives, and 5 new vocabulary items. Recorded for portfolio. Debrief (10 min): Teacher compares Roberto's current storytelling with his Aula 1 baseline — names 3 specific improvements. Roberto identifies 1 area he wants to strengthen.
curriculo.homework: Write a 12-sentence travel story about visiting the Colosseum as Roberto Pires — use past continuous + past simple for the narrative structure, at least 2 superlatives, and 6 new vocabulary items. (Est. 25 min). Record a 2-minute audio narrating your most impressive moment in Rome — don't read from notes. (Est. 15 min).

curriculo.tema: Solving Real Problems in Rome — Overbooked Tours, Missed Connections, and Keeping Your Cool in Italian
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: modal verbs full set (should/could/might) + past simple for problem resolution narration; NEW vocabulary: overbooked, alternative, refund, complaint, supervisor, reschedule, priority, voucher, inconvenience, resolve; Chunks: 'I'd like to speak to the manager, please.', 'Could you offer an alternative?', 'I should have booked in advance, but…', 'This might take some time to resolve.'; Expression: 'It depends on…' (review — applied to problem-solving: 'It depends on whether they have availability tomorrow.'); Collocation: 'file a complaint', 'escalate the issue', 'offer a solution'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher presents 3 'Rome problems' scenario cards (overbooked Vatican tour, taxi overcharges, hotel double-books a room) — Roberto votes on most stressful, class discusses which language tools from the program would help most in each situation. Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents 8 new problem/complaint vocabulary items via scenario visuals; models the 'polite complaint' language arc: acknowledge → explain → request → negotiate → resolve; drills 'should have + past participle' as a new production pattern for regret: 'I should have booked earlier.' Practice (25 min): Problem-solving role-play sequence — 3 stations: (1) Roberto arrives at a Vatican tour — he's not on the list; (2) Roberto disputes an overcharge with a taxi driver; (3) Roberto speaks to a hotel supervisor about a room double-booking. Each scenario has a 'resolution card' — Roberto must reach it using polite, clear English within 3 minutes. Production (20 min): Roberto narrates 'The Day Everything Went Wrong in Rome' — a 3-minute spoken story using past simple (what happened), past continuous (what was happening around him), and modal verbs (what he should/could/might have done). Debrief (10 min): Class reviews: which problem was hardest to handle linguistically? Teacher provides 3 reformulated sentences for internalization.
curriculo.homework: Write a formal complaint email (10-12 sentences) from Roberto Pires to a Rome tour company — describe the problem, explain the impact, request a solution. Use modal verbs, past simple, and 4 new vocabulary items. (Est. 25 min). Record a 90-second audio practicing the complaint conversation as if calling the tour company directly. (Est. 15 min).

curriculo.tema: Roberto's Grand Tour Wrap-Up — Telling the Full Story of 5 Cities in 5 Minutes
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: integration of ALL structures (past simple, past continuous, going to, will, modals, comparatives, superlatives, quantifiers, verb patterns); Discourse markers: 'first', 'then', 'after that', 'while', 'finally', 'by the time'; NEW vocabulary: highlight, unforgettable, challenging, rewarding, itinerary, cultural immersion, confidence, milestone, perspective, recommendation; Chunks: 'The highlight of the trip was…', 'Looking back, I should have…', 'I'm planning to go back to…'; Expression: 'I used to be afraid of speaking English — but not anymore.' (A2 expression 'I used to' — first meaningful use in program, emotionally loaded)
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher draws a blank map of Europe on the board — Roberto labels Barcelona, Paris, Amsterdam, London, and Rome from memory and says one word that describes each city; builds anticipation for the full narrative task. Presentation (15 min): Teacher introduces discourse markers for storytelling ('first', 'while', 'after that', 'by the time', 'finally') using a printed guide; models how to stitch 5 city experiences into one coherent narrative — Roberto identifies markers in the model text and categorizes them. Practice (25 min): Structured narrative building — Roberto receives 10 'event cards' (one per major city experience); arranges them on the table, then narrates the full story using discourse markers; teacher interrupts with follow-up questions to practice spontaneous response. Second task: Roberto identifies his 3 best and 3 hardest moments of the 'trip' and explains why using full sentences. Production (20 min): Roberto delivers an uninterrupted 5-minute spoken narrative of his full European Grand Tour — from Barcelona to Rome; teacher notes fluency, grammar range, vocabulary density, and natural discourse flow. This is Roberto's most ambitious speaking task to date. Recorded for portfolio. Debrief (10 min): Teacher celebrates specific growth moments with evidence from the recording; identifies 2 stretch goals for the final 2 aulas.
curriculo.homework: Write a 15-sentence 'Grand Tour Summary' as Roberto Pires — cover all 5 cities, use at least 6 different grammar structures from the program, 3 discourse markers, and the expression 'I used to'. (Est. 30 min). Record a 3-minute 'travel podcast episode' audio telling a Brazilian audience about your favorite European city and why. Don't read — tell the story naturally. (Est. 20 min).

curriculo.tema: Fluency Under Pressure — Simulated Real Situations Roberto Will Face in August (Full Role-Play Simulation)
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar: ALL structures in unpredictable combination; Focus: fluency, recovery strategies, and communication success over perfection; NEW vocabulary (meta-communication): 'Could you repeat that?', 'I didn't catch that.', 'Sorry, could you speak more slowly?', 'What I meant to say was…', 'Let me think for a second.'; Chunks (fluency fillers): 'Actually…', 'You know what I mean?', 'The thing is…', 'Something like that.'; Expression: 'It's a piece of cake.' (review — Roberto uses it to describe situations that used to be hard: 'Ordering food in English? It's a piece of cake now.').
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up (10 min): Teacher introduces the 'communication toolbox' — 5 recovery strategies for when Roberto gets stuck in real English situations; drills each one 3 times with exaggerated scenarios until they feel automatic. Presentation (10 min): Teacher explains the format of the 'August Simulation' — 6 rapid-fire real scenarios Roberto will actually face during his trip; Roberto receives no script, only the situation; evaluation is on communication success, not grammar. Simulation (40 min): 6 back-to-back role-play scenarios — teacher plays different characters: (1) Barcelona check-in agent who doesn't understand Roberto's accent; (2) Parisian waiter who speaks limited English; (3) Amsterdam tram passenger giving confusing directions; (4) London pharmacist asking about Roberto's symptoms; (5) Rome tour guide who announces a last-minute change; (6) Airport staff at an overbooked gate. Roberto handles each with 3-4 minute interaction time, using all tools from the program. Production/Debrief (30 min): Teacher plays back 3 moments from the simulation (audio notes) — Roberto self-evaluates using 'What went well', 'What I'd do differently', 'What I need to practice before August'. Teacher provides specific praise + 2 targeted improvement micro-tasks. Closing: Roberto rates his readiness for the real trip: 1-10 with justification.
curriculo.homework: Record a 3-minute audio simulating one of the 6 August scenarios from class — choose the one that felt hardest; perform it again with improvements. (Est. 20 min). Write 10 sentences: 5 things Roberto can now do in English that he couldn't before, and 5 situations he still wants to feel more confident in before the trip. (Est. 20 min).

curriculo.tema: Block 2 Closing — Roberto's Language Portfolio, August Readiness Score, and Preview of the Final Sprint (Aulas 41–48)
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Full grammar and vocabulary integration review across all Block 2 content: modal verbs, past continuous, verb patterns, discourse markers, quantifiers; Self-assessment language: 'I've become more confident in…', 'I can now handle…', 'I still need to work on…', 'Before this program, I used to…'; Reflection chunks: 'The most useful thing I learned was…', 'I'm ready to…', 'I'm looking forward to August because…'; Final expression activation: 'I used to be afraid — but now I know I can handle it.'
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Warm-up — Language Inventory Game (15 min): Teacher calls out a travel situation ('You're at a Paris restaurant and the waiter only speaks French') — Roberto responds with 3 English sentences using the best tools he has; repeat for 5 situations spanning all Block 2 cities. This activates the full vocabulary and grammar range in a low-pressure format. Portfolio Review (20 min): Teacher presents Roberto with a printed 'language portfolio' — a visual map of all grammar structures, vocabulary clusters, expressions, and chunks covered in Aulas 21-40; Roberto marks items as 'solid', 'needs practice', or 'still uncertain' using colored stickers. Gap Discussion (15 min): Teacher and Roberto identify 3-4 areas to prioritize in the final sprint (Aulas 41-48) — framed as final preparation for August, not as deficiencies. Preview of Final Sprint (15 min): Teacher unveils Aulas 41-48 themes — practical business conversation, full trip simulation, emotional language, and the culminating role-play; Roberto expresses what he most wants to practice. Confidence Ceremony (15 min): Roberto delivers a 2-minute unscripted 'I am ready for August' speech — no prompts, no script, no teacher interruption. This is a milestone moment. Teacher affirms 5 specific improvements with evidence. Roberto signs his 'August Readiness Commitment Card' — a personal pledge to use English from Day 1 of the trip.
curriculo.homework: Write Roberto Pires's 'English Passport' — a one-page document (in English) summarizing: who you are, where you're going, what you can now do in English, and what you're most excited about for August. Use at least 8 grammar structures from the program. (Est. 30 min). Record a final 3-minute 'Block 2 Closing Statement' audio — tell your future self in August what you learned and why you're ready. Keep this recording to listen to during the trip. (Est. 20 min).

curriculo.tema: Roberto's European Diary — Telling the Full Story with Confidence and Flow
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion — full integration): past simple + past continuous in extended narrative ('I was walking through the Boqueria when I saw…', 'We arrived late, but the hotel was still open.'); connectives for storytelling: first, then, after that, suddenly, in the end; Review vocabulary: all 5 cities (Barcelona, Paris, Amsterdam, London, Rome) key words reactivated as production tools — NOT new content; Expressions: 'It was absolutely worth it.', 'I couldn't believe my eyes.', 'The best part was…'; Chunk: 'Looking back, I think…' for reflection; Idiomatic: 'once in a while' (A2 expression — review + production); Target: Roberto produces a 10-sentence connected narrative about one European moment using past simple + past continuous + at least 3 connectives without switching to Portuguese.
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — Warm-up (10 min): Teacher spreads 10 city photo cards (Sagrada Família, Eiffel Tower, Colosseum, etc.) on the table face down. Roberto picks one randomly and must say 2 sentences about it using past tense — no preparation. This activates retrieval and reveals gaps. Phase 2 — Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents a model 'European diary entry' on a printed card — 8 sentences combining past simple and past continuous with connectives highlighted in color. Roberto reads aloud, teacher marks natural stress and pauses. Phase 3 — Guided Practice (20 min): Roberto uses a structured 'story skeleton' card (First… / I was… when… / Then… / After that… / In the end…) to reconstruct one of his favorite moments from the simulated Grand Tour (Aula 38 reference). Teacher gives corrective feedback on verb forms only — not pronunciation. Phase 4 — Production (25 min): Roberto tells his full European story to the teacher as if speaking to a Brazilian friend who has never traveled to Europe. Teacher plays the curious friend, asking natural follow-up questions ('Wait — what happened next?', 'How did you feel?'). Roberto must sustain the narrative for at least 3 minutes. Final product: a 3-minute spoken story recorded on the teacher's phone for Roberto's progress portfolio. Phase 5 — Debrief (10 min): Teacher replays 30 seconds of the recording. Roberto identifies: one thing he did well, one verb form to fix. Teacher highlights the most fluent moment as positive reinforcement.
curriculo.homework: Task 1 (20 min): Write your European diary — a 10-sentence paragraph about one real or imagined moment from the trip (airport, restaurant, museum, hotel). Use: past simple, past continuous, and at least 4 connectives (first, then, after that, suddenly, in the end). Handwrite it in your notebook. Task 2 (10 min): Record a 2-minute audio of the same story — speak naturally, as if telling a friend. Do NOT read your written version. Send the audio to the teacher. Task 3 (5 min): Look up 2 photos of the place you wrote about and bring them to Aula 42 to use as a speaking prompt.

curriculo.tema: Roberto Talks Business — Networking in English at European Conferences and Airport Lounges
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion consolidation): present simple for professional identity ('I run a company in São Paulo.', 'I work in the business sector.', 'I travel to Europe for…') + going to + will for professional plans ('I'm going to meet a client in London next year.', 'I'll send you my contact.'); NEW vocabulary (professional travel context, 40% foco): lounge, networking, contact, deal, schedule, partner, headquarters, market, opportunity, conference; NEW expressions: 'Nice to meet you — what brings you here?', 'Here's my card.', 'We should stay in touch.'; Idiomatic: 'It's a small world.' (transparent A2); Chunk: 'I'm in the business of…'; Target: Roberto introduces himself professionally in English, maintains a 2-minute small-talk exchange, and closes with a natural farewell — all without Portuguese.
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — Warm-up (10 min): Teacher sets the scene: 'Imagine you're in the business lounge at Heathrow, waiting for your flight back to São Paulo. A British businessman sits next to you.' Teacher plays the businessman and opens with: 'Long day?' Roberto must respond naturally. No preparation — this is a diagnostic for business register. Phase 2 — Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents a 'Business Small Talk Map' on a printed card — 4 conversation moves: (1) Opening (comment on situation), (2) Self-introduction (name + company + city), (3) Common ground (travel, destination, industry), (4) Closing (exchange contacts, farewell). Each move has 2 model sentences. Roberto reads and repeats with natural intonation. Phase 3 — Vocabulary Drilling (10 min): Flashcard game — teacher holds up business vocabulary flashcards (lounge, schedule, headquarters, partner, opportunity). Roberto says the word, uses it in a sentence, and rates his confidence (1–3). Cards rated 1 go back in the pile for re-drilling. Phase 4 — Guided Role-Play (20 min): Roberto and teacher do a structured airport lounge role-play. Teacher is an American tech entrepreneur; Roberto is himself. Script skeleton provided: Roberto must hit all 4 conversation moves. Teacher interrupts with realistic follow-up questions ('Oh, São Paulo — do you travel to the US often?'). Two rounds: first with skeleton card, second without. Phase 5 — Production (15 min): Roberto does a final 'cold' role-play — teacher is now a French hotel manager met at a Paris networking dinner. Roberto must adapt the same 4 moves to a new context. Final product: Roberto completes the exchange and writes the 'businessman's' email address and name as a closing action (physical notecard). Phase 6 — Debrief (10 min): Teacher highlights the most natural moment. Roberto writes 3 sentences he wants to remember in his notebook.
curriculo.homework: Task 1 (15 min): Write a 6-sentence LinkedIn-style introduction as if Roberto Pires is presenting himself to an international audience. Use: present simple, going to, and at least 2 new vocabulary words (lounge, market, opportunity, partner, etc.). Task 2 (10 min): Record a 90-second audio — 'You are at an airport lounge in Rome. Introduce yourself to the person next to you.' Speak naturally. No script. Send to teacher. Task 3 (5 min): Find and screenshot one real LinkedIn post or professional introduction in English and bring it to Aula 43.

curriculo.tema: Smooth Operator — Handling Every Unexpected Problem in Europe Without Freezing
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion — consolidation): modal verbs full set in problem-solving context — should (advice), could (option), might (uncertainty), must (necessity): 'You should call the airline.', 'We could take a different route.', 'The train might be delayed.', 'I must find my passport.'; past simple for problem narration: 'I lost my bag at the Gare du Nord.', 'The hotel gave me the wrong room.'; NEW vocabulary (problem + solution, 40% foco): delay, cancel, replace, complaint, refund, insurance, emergency, receipt, report, alternative; Expressions (review + production): 'I need help, please.', 'Can you explain that again?', 'What are my options?'; NEW expression: 'Is there anything you can do?'; Idiomatic: 'stay calm' used as a fixed chunk; Target: Roberto narrates a problem, uses modal verbs to explore solutions, and closes with a resolution — all in connected English under simulated pressure.
