curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — Warm-up: 'Problem Roulette' (10 min): Teacher has a deck of 8 'problem cards' (flight cancelled, hotel overbooked, wallet stolen, lost metro card, wrong food order, sick on travel day, language misunderstanding, overcharged at restaurant). Roberto picks a card and has 30 seconds to say what he would do — in English only. No judgment — teacher tracks which problems trigger code-switching. Phase 2 — Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents a 'Problem → Option → Solution' model on a printed card using one scenario (lost baggage at CDG airport). Three sentences per stage, each using a modal verb highlighted in bold. Roberto reads, identifies the modal, and explains why it was used (advice / option / uncertainty). Phase 3 — Controlled Practice (15 min): Gap-fill dialogue — teacher gives Roberto a printed conversation between a traveler and an airline desk agent. 8 blanks where modal verbs are missing. Roberto fills in alone, then reads the full dialogue aloud. Teacher corrects only modal verb errors. Phase 4 — Guided Role-Play (20 min): Two scenarios back to back. Scenario A: Roberto's hotel in Amsterdam gave away his room — he must negotiate with the receptionist (teacher) using modal verbs and problem vocabulary. Scenario B: Roberto was overcharged at a London restaurant — he must address the situation politely with the waiter (teacher). Roberto uses 'Is there anything you can do?' in each scenario. Phase 5 — Free Production (10 min): Roberto narrates — without prompts — the 'worst travel problem he can imagine' as a hypothetical past story. Teacher listens and counts: (1) modal verbs used, (2) past simple verbs used, (3) times he switched to Portuguese. Target: zero code-switches. Phase 6 — Debrief (10 min): Teacher gives Roberto a 'performance card' with 3 green (strength) and 1 red (focus) item. Roberto writes the red item in his notebook as his personal target for Aula 44.
curriculo.homework: Task 1 (15 min): Write a problem story — 8 sentences minimum. Choose any travel problem from the lesson. Use: past simple to describe what happened + modal verbs to describe what you could/should/might do. Task 2 (10 min): Record a 90-second audio — 'You are at a hotel reception in Paris. Your room is not ready and it is 11 PM. Explain the problem and ask for options.' Speak without notes. Send to teacher. Task 3 (5 min): Write 3 sentences using 'might' + 'should' + 'could' about something that could go wrong on your August trip — and what you would do.

curriculo.tema: Roberto's Language Toolkit — Fluency Strategies When You Don't Know the Word
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Expansion — consolidation across all structures): NO new grammar introduced — this is a fluency and communication strategies lesson; Focus: communication strategies for A2 speakers — paraphrasing ('It's a kind of…', 'It's like a…', 'The thing you use to…'), asking for clarification ('Sorry, could you say that again?', 'What do you mean by…?', 'How do you spell that?'), buying time ('Let me think…', 'How do I say this…', 'Give me a second.'), recovery from a freeze ('Actually, what I mean is…', 'Sorry — let me start again.'); NEW vocabulary (meta-communication, 60% geral): paraphrase, clarify, repeat, explain, describe, mean, understand, similar, kind of, basically; Expressions (NEW): 'Bear with me.', 'Does that make sense?'; Idiomatic (review): 'It depends on…' used in strategy selection; Target: Roberto completes a 5-minute conversation without freezing — using at least 3 communication strategies when blocked, instead of switching to Portuguese.
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — Warm-up: 'The Forbidden Word' (10 min): Teacher says a word in English (e.g., 'passport'). Roberto must explain it WITHOUT using the word, in English. 5 rounds. Vocabulary: items from the full program (metro, receipt, lounge, reservation, customs). This activates paraphrasing instinctively before it's taught formally. Phase 2 — Presentation (15 min): Teacher presents the 'Fluency Toolkit' — a laminated A5 card with 4 strategy categories: (1) Paraphrase, (2) Ask for clarification, (3) Buy time, (4) Recover from a freeze. Each has 2 model phrases. Roberto reads each phrase aloud with natural rhythm. Teacher models exaggerated intonation for 'Bear with me' and 'Let me think' — showing these are NORMAL, not embarrassing. Phase 3 — Drilling (10 min): Teacher deliberately says something too fast or complex. Roberto must use a clarification strategy — no guessing, no nodding and pretending. 6 rounds with different speeds and accents (teacher simulates mild British, American, non-native English). Phase 4 — Paraphrase Challenge (15 min): Teacher shows Roberto 8 flashcards with words he does NOT know (deliberate advanced vocabulary). Roberto must describe each one in simple English using 'It's a kind of…' or 'It's like a… but…'. Teacher counts successful paraphrases. Target: 6/8. Phase 5 — Free Conversation with Strategy Tracking (20 min): Teacher and Roberto have a free 5-minute conversation about Roberto's August trip plans — teacher deliberately uses unfamiliar words and speaks at near-natural pace. Roberto uses the Toolkit card. After the conversation, teacher replays the moments where Roberto froze and they identify together: did he use a strategy? Which one would have worked? Phase 6 — Debrief (10 min): Roberto writes in his notebook: 'My 3 favorite strategies from today.' He also selects his 'emergency phrase' — the one sentence he will use first if he freezes in Europe.
curriculo.homework: Task 1 (15 min): Watch a 3-minute English video (YouTube — travel vlog recommended: Mark Wiens or Lost LeBlancs). When you don't understand a word, pause and write your best GUESS of what it means based on context. List 5 words + your guesses. Task 2 (10 min): Record a 2-minute audio — describe your apartment or office in São Paulo WITHOUT using any Portuguese words. If you don't know a word, use a strategy from the Toolkit. Send to teacher. Task 3 (5 min): Practice saying these 3 phrases aloud 5 times each until they feel automatic: 'Bear with me.', 'What do you mean by that?', 'It's kind of like a…'

curriculo.tema: Grand Finale Preparation Part 1 — Roberto Practices Every City, Every Situation
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Full integration — all A2 structures): past simple + past continuous (narrative), present continuous (live description), going to + will (plans + instant decisions), modal verbs (should/could/might), comparatives + superlatives, quantifiers, verb + -ing vs verb + to — ALL in natural connected speech across travel scenarios; Vocabulary review (active retrieval — NOT new): airport (check-in, customs, gate, baggage claim), hotel (check-in, upgrade, complaint), restaurant (order, recommend, bill, tip), transport (metro, taxi, directions), shopping (price, size, refund), emergencies (delay, cancel, lost, insurance); Expressions (review + production): 'I'm looking forward to…', 'I would rather…', 'I used to…', 'In my opinion…', 'It depends on…'; Target: Roberto moves fluidly between 5 city contexts in a 20-minute simulation, using appropriate vocabulary and grammar for each WITHOUT teacher prompting grammar structures.
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — Warm-up: 'City Roulette' (10 min): 5 city name cards face down on the table. Roberto picks one, teacher says a situation (e.g., 'You're ordering dinner.') and Roberto has 30 seconds to produce 3 sentences. 5 rounds — one per city. This is diagnostic AND motivational — Roberto sees how much he remembers. Phase 2 — The 5-City Carousel (40 min): Five structured role-play stations, 7 minutes each. Station 1 — Barcelona: Roberto orders tapas and asks for the bill in a busy restaurant. Station 2 — Paris: Roberto asks for directions to the Louvre from a hotel concierge and describes what he sees when he arrives. Station 3 — Amsterdam: Roberto checks in to a canal hotel and requests a room change because of noise. Station 4 — London: Roberto is in a pub, makes small talk with a British local, and navigates the menu. Station 5 — Rome: Roberto's tour of the Colosseum was cancelled — he negotiates a refund and books an alternative. Teacher plays all characters. After each station, 30-second micro-debrief: one strength, one fix. Phase 3 — Cross-City Narrative (20 min): Roberto tells the complete story of all 5 cities in sequence — as if recounting his trip to a Brazilian business partner who asked 'How was Europe?' Teacher does NOT interrupt — only listens and notes. Target: 5 minutes of connected speech. Phase 4 — Debrief + Preview (10 min): Teacher gives Roberto a 'Readiness Map' — a visual showing all 5 cities with a confidence score (1–3) for each, based on today's performance. Roberto identifies his weakest city and weakest situation for targeted practice in Aula 46.
curriculo.homework: Task 1 (20 min): Record a 4-minute audio — 'Tell the story of your European trip from Barcelona to Rome.' Use past simple, past continuous, and connectives. Speak without notes. This will be your BEFORE recording for the final celebration in Aula 48. Send to teacher. Task 2 (10 min): Review your notebook from Aulas 41–44. Write the 10 words and 3 expressions you feel LEAST confident about. Bring this list to Aula 46. Task 3 (5 min): Say your 'emergency phrase' from Aula 44 out loud before sleeping tonight and before Aula 46.

curriculo.tema: Grand Finale Preparation Part 2 — Closing the Gaps and Building Roberto's Confidence Peak
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Targeted review — student-driven): gaps identified in Aula 45 Readiness Map + Roberto's self-identified list from homework; likely focus areas: past continuous precision ('I was waiting' vs. 'I waited'), modal verb selection under pressure, comparatives in spontaneous speech; Vocabulary (targeted retrieval — student's weak list from homework): personalized per Roberto's self-assessment; Expressions (consolidation): all 8 A2 expressions activated in natural speech — 'It's a piece of cake.', 'Once in a while.', 'I'm looking forward to…', 'I would rather…', 'I used to…', 'It depends on…', 'In my opinion…', 'I agree / I disagree'; NEW idiomatic (A2 transparent): 'The more, the merrier.' — introduced as a bonus; Target: Roberto uses ALL 8 A2 expressions naturally in a 15-minute conversation without being cued — and demonstrates measurable improvement in his 2 weakest areas from Aula 45.
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — Personal Gap Clinic (20 min): Teacher reviews Roberto's homework recording from Aula 45 and his self-identified weak list. Together they identify the 3 most important language items to consolidate before Aula 47. Teacher designs a 10-minute micro-drill specifically for those items — e.g., if past continuous is weak, 5 rapid-fire sentence prompts; if modal verbs are shaky, a quick 'advice game' where Roberto advises a fictional traveler. This session is 100% responsive to Roberto's actual performance data. Phase 2 — Expression Marathon (20 min): Teacher places 8 expression cards face down. Roberto picks one, and teacher immediately creates a travel scenario where that expression fits naturally. Roberto must use the expression in context within 10 seconds. E.g., card: 'I would rather…' — teacher: 'The waiter offers you the window table or the bar seat. Which do you prefer?' Roberto: 'I would rather sit by the window, please.' All 8 expressions covered. Roberto rates each: green (automatic), yellow (needs practice), red (still insecure). Phase 3 — Pressure Simulation (20 min): Teacher runs two 10-minute unscripted role-plays in Roberto's identified weak cities/situations from Aula 45. Teacher plays difficult characters: one who speaks fast, one who is impatient. Roberto uses communication strategies from Aula 44 when blocked. After each, Roberto self-assesses: 'Did I freeze? Did I use a strategy? Did I switch to Portuguese?' Phase 4 — Confidence Builder (15 min): Teacher replays a 60-second clip of Roberto's most fluent moment from today's session. Roberto listens and identifies: vocabulary used, grammar structure, fluency level. Teacher says: 'This is what you sound like when you're in flow. Remember this feeling for August.' Roberto writes one sentence in his notebook: 'I am ready for Europe because…' Phase 5 — Preview of Aula 47–48 (5 min): Teacher explains the format of the final two sessions — full unscripted simulation (Aula 47) and celebration + portfolio review (Aula 48). Roberto knows what to expect.
curriculo.homework: Task 1 (15 min): Practice all 8 A2 expressions — write one original sentence for each using your real August travel plans as context. Example: 'I'm looking forward to eating paella in Barcelona.' Handwrite in notebook. Task 2 (10 min): Record a 90-second audio using your 3 personally weakest language items from today's gap clinic — deliberately put them in context. Send to teacher. Task 3 (10 min): Read your Aula 45 homework recording transcript (if teacher sends it) OR listen again and count: How many times did you use a connective? How many modal verbs? Write the numbers in your notebook. Target for Aula 47: beat those numbers.

curriculo.tema: The Full European Simulation — Roberto Lives His August Trip in 90 Minutes
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (ALL A2 structures — unscripted integration): past simple + past continuous, present continuous, going to + will, modals (should/could/might), comparatives + superlatives, quantifiers, verb + -ing vs verb + to — all deployed naturally in unpredictable, sequential real-world scenarios without grammar prompting; Vocabulary (full program — ALL active): airport, hotel, restaurant, transport, shopping, emergencies, business networking, city sightseeing — Roberto retrieves from memory, no word banks provided; Expressions (all 8 A2 + communication strategies from Aula 44): fully autonomous deployment; Target: Roberto completes a 60-minute uninterrupted simulated travel day — check-in to Rome dinner — in English only, with zero teacher grammar prompting, using at least 3 communication strategies when blocked, and producing at least 10 different grammar structures across the simulation.
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — Briefing (5 min): Teacher sets the scene on a printed 'Mission Card': 'Today, you are Roberto Pires. It is August 15th. You are flying from São Paulo to Barcelona. From there, you will travel to Paris, London, and Rome. I will play everyone you meet. You must handle everything in English. There is no Portuguese today.' Roberto receives NO vocabulary cards, NO grammar cards — only his Aula 44 Fluency Toolkit card as emergency resource. Phase 2 — The Full Simulation (60 min): 8 sequential scenarios, approximately 7 minutes each. Scenario 1 — GRU Airport: Check-in desk. Bag is 3kg overweight. Roberto negotiates with agent. Scenario 2 — Barcelona Hotel: Check-in. Room not ready. Roberto requests an upgrade and explains he has a dinner reservation in 2 hours. Scenario 3 — Barcelona Restaurant: Orders tapas for himself and a business partner. One dish is wrong — he addresses it politely. Scenario 4 — Paris: Roberto asks a local (teacher, non-native English accent) for directions to the Eiffel Tower. Gets confused by the answer. Uses clarification strategy. Scenario 5 — Paris Fine Dining: Formal dinner. Waiter recommends dishes. Roberto asks about ingredients, makes a choice, and handles the bill with a discrepancy. Scenario 6 — London Pub: Roberto meets a British entrepreneur. Small talk, professional introduction, exchange contacts. Scenario 7 — London Emergency: Roberto's phone is stolen on the Tube. He goes to the police station and files a report. Scenario 8 — Rome Airport: Flight home is delayed 4 hours. Roberto speaks with the airline desk, finds out options, decides what to do, and calls his assistant (teacher) to update plans. Phase 3 — Debrief and Performance Review (20 min): Teacher gives Roberto a printed 'Performance Summary Card' with 5 categories: Fluency, Grammar Range, Vocabulary, Communication Strategies, Confidence. Each rated 1–5. Teacher reviews: top 3 most impressive moments (with exact quotes), 2 areas for final polish. Roberto writes in his notebook: 'I am ready for Europe. Here is the proof:' followed by 3 specific things he did well today. Phase 4 — Emotional Anchoring (5 min): Teacher says: 'Roberto, on August 15th, this is what you will do. You've already done it today.' Roberto identifies his single strongest moment from the simulation.
curriculo.homework: Task 1 (15 min): Write a full 'August Travel Diary' — 10 sentences minimum telling the story of your simulated travel day from today's class. Use ALL grammar structures you can. This will be read aloud at Aula 48. Task 2 (10 min): Record your FINAL audio — 4 minutes, telling your European story from start to finish. This is your AFTER recording, to compare with your Aula 45 homework. Send to teacher before Aula 48. Task 3 (Reflection — 5 min): Answer in writing: 'What was the moment in today's class when I felt most confident? Why?' Bring this to Aula 48.

curriculo.tema: Roberto's Grand Finale — 48 Aulas, 5 Cities, and a Man Ready for Europe: Celebration, Portfolio Review, and Farewell
curriculo.focoLinguistico: Grammar (Full A2 inventory — celebratory review): ALL structures present as living proof of progress — Roberto uses them in authentic reflection and narrative, not as exercises; Vocabulary (complete program portfolio): active retrieval of the most meaningful words from the full 48-aula journey — no new content; Expressions (all 8 A2 — farewell production): 'I'm looking forward to…' (August trip), 'I used to…' (before the program), 'In my opinion…' (reflection on the experience), 'It was a piece of cake… actually, no, but now it is.'; NEW final expression (gift): 'I've come a long way.' — presented as a meaningful farewell chunk; Idiomatic farewell: 'Go for it, Roberto.' — teacher's closing gift phrase; Target: Roberto speaks for 5 uninterrupted minutes about his learning journey, uses the expression 'I've come a long way,' and leaves Aula 48 with a documented language portfolio and a clear confidence statement for August.
curriculo.atividadeEmAula: Phase 1 — The Before and After (15 min): Teacher plays Roberto's very first Aula 1 diagnostic recording (baseline) and then his Aula 47 homework recording (after). Both play out loud. Roberto listens to BOTH without interruption. Then teacher asks: 'What do you notice?' Roberto describes the difference in his own words — in English. This is the emotional anchor of the entire session — Roberto hears his own transformation. Phase 2 — The Language Portfolio Walk (20 min): Teacher presents Roberto's printed 'Language Portfolio' — a 2-page A4 document summarizing: all grammar structures mastered (with example sentences from his own recordings), vocabulary count across the program (approximate), expressions learned and used, communication strategies acquired, top 5 breakthrough moments identified by teacher from the full program. Roberto reads each section aloud. Teacher adds personal commentary for each. This is Roberto's trophy. Phase 3 — The Final Story (15 min): Roberto reads his 'August Travel Diary' written for homework — aloud, with expression, as if telling his family after returning from Europe. Teacher listens without correcting. At the end, teacher asks 3 authentic questions: 'Which city are you most excited about?', 'What will you do if something goes wrong?', 'What's one phrase you will definitely use in Barcelona?' Roberto answers naturally. Phase 4 — The Confidence Statement (10 min): Roberto writes — by hand — in the front page of his notebook: 'My name is Roberto Pires. I speak English. I am ready for Europe.' Then he reads it aloud to the teacher. Teacher witnesses it. They shake hands. Phase 5 — Celebration and Practical Farewell (15 min): Teacher gives Roberto a 'Europe Ready Kit' — a printed pocket card with: 5 emergency expressions (laminated), 8 A2 expressions, 3 communication strategies, and Roberto's personal 'emergency phrase' from Aula 44. Teacher reviews: 'Keep this in your bag. Pull it out if you freeze. But you won't need it.' Final 5 minutes: open conversation — Roberto talks about August, his family, the trip, whatever he wants — in English, freely, joyfully. Teacher's final words: 'Go for it, Roberto. You've come a long way.'
curriculo.homework: No formal homework — Roberto has graduated. Optional personal challenge: Before your August trip, record one final 2-minute audio: 'My name is Roberto Pires. I'm going to Europe. Here's what I'm going to do.' Keep it. Listen to it on the flight. And when you land in Barcelona — say 'Excuse me, can you help me?' to the first person you need. You already know how.

perfil.temasAulas:
Aula 1: Diagnostic + First Words: Who Is Roberto Pires? — Self-Introduction and Travel Goals — Diagnostic baseline: present simple (I am, I work, I live, I have); basic personal information chunks; travel vocabulary activation check (airport, hotel, restaurant, trip); expressions: 'My name is…', 'I'm from…', 'I work in…', 'I want to…', 'This August, I'm going to…'; vocabulary circulating: name, city, work, trip, travel, hotel, restaurant, airport, Spain, France (10 words — all diagnostic, not new content)
Aula 2: Survival Kit Part 1 — The Magic Phrases That Open Every Door in Europe — Core travel chunks (Presentation focus): 'Excuse me…', 'Can you help me?', 'Where is…?', 'How much is this?', 'I don't understand.', 'Can you repeat that, please?', 'Do you speak English?'; grammar: yes/no questions with 'Can' and 'Do' (A2 Foundation); vocabulary NEW (6): excuse, repeat, slowly, understand, help, speak; vocabulary REVIEW (4): airport, hotel, restaurant, trip; expression: 'I'm sorry, I don't understand' as a complete survival phrase; idiomatic: 'Bear with me' (transparent for A2)
Aula 3: Survival Kit Part 2 — Numbers, Prices, and Saying No Politely at European Markets — Grammar (Foundation): present simple for transactions ('It costs…', 'I want…', 'I need…', 'I have…'); numbers 1–1,000 in context (prices in euros); polite refusals: 'No, thank you.', 'That's too expensive.', 'I'll think about it.'; vocabulary NEW (7): price, euro, receipt, cash, card, expensive, cheap; vocabulary REVIEW (5): help, understand, excuse, hotel, restaurant; expression new: 'I'll take it!' / 'Just looking, thanks.'; pronunciation focus: -teen vs. -ty (thirteen vs. thirty — common error for Brazilians)
Aula 4: At the Airport — Check-In, Security, and Finding Your Gate Without Panic — Grammar (Foundation): past simple — regular verbs (checked, asked, waited, showed, walked); time expressions: yesterday, last time, before (for telling stories at airport); airport-specific vocabulary NEW (8): boarding pass, gate, check-in, security, departure, arrival, carry-on, passport; vocabulary REVIEW (4): help, repeat, excuse, price; expression: 'I used to fly a lot…' (A2 expression list) for warm-up context; chunks: 'Window or aisle?', 'Place your bags on the belt.', 'Do you have anything to declare?', 'My flight is at…'

form.localEvento: Viagem turística para Barcelona e Paris
form.transcricao:
OBJETIVO GERAL
Desenvolver fluidez na comunicação em inglês, reduzindo a dependência do português e aprimorando a construção de frases com maior precisão gramatical, com aplicação direta em Travel English (Barcelona e Paris).
Documento confidencial —uso pedagógico interno
Diagnóstico Pedagógico
Roberto Pires —A2
PERFIL GERAL
Aluno em estágio A2. Apresenta vocabulário funcional básico e pronúncia confortável, mas demonstra dificuldade recorrente na construção de frases completas durante a fala espontânea —a compreensão evolui mais rápido do que a produção livre. Como há viagem prevista para agosto, o trabalho tem objetivo aplicado e prazo definido, o que aumenta a relevância pedagógica de cada aula e justifica uma trilha 100% direcionada a Travel English.
PONTOS DE FORÇA
PONTOS DE ATENÇÃO
Vocabulário funcional básico
Boa retenção de palavras isoladas, ainda com pouca variedade de chunks (frases prontas) e collocations —base lexical para apoiar a expansão da construção de frases.
Pronúncia clara e inteligível
Boa percepção sonora e ausência de bloqueios articulatórios.
Construção de frases (sintaxe)
Principal lacuna. Frases incompletas e estruturas truncadas —fragilidade em estruturas-base (SVO completo, do/does, marcação de tempo, conectivos básicos).
Recurso ao português (code-switching)
Frequente, como compensação para lacunas estruturais ou lexicais. Indica carga cognitiva elevada e ansiedade comunicativa moderada em contextos de pressão real.
IMPLICAÇÃO PEDAGÓGICA
Recomenda-se combinar duas frentes: corrigir e construir frases (form-focused instruction) com prática em situações reais de viagem (task-based learning). A ideia é expor o aluno a muitas frases prontas (chunks) para reduzir a tradução mental e gerar fala fluente nas situações previsíveis. Considerando a viagem em agosto (Barcelona e Paris), faz sentido priorizar ganho rápido emcenários previsíveis —aeroporto, hotel, restaurante, transporte —antes de expandir para conversação livre, garantindo retorno tangível em curto prazo.
FORMATO DE ESTUDO
PRE-CLASS
Estudo guiado com material direcionado pelo professor: introdução ao tema da aula e ativação prévia (priming) do vocabulário específico, garantindo repertório mínimo para participação ativa.
IN-CLASS
Aula majoritariamente comunicativa com conversação guiada em situações reais. Interrupções estratégicas para correção, reformulação e drilling. Alternância entre fluência (fala livre) e precisão (correção e construção).
POST-CLASS
Feedback estruturado pelo professor: pontos gramaticais observados, correções recorrentes, sugestão de reforço com micro-prática direcionada e consolidação dos erros mais frequentes.
